1. Choose suitable logs: Save the largest, straightest, and best logs for sills or foundations.
2. Protect from dampness: If building on the ground (mudsill), protect sill logs...
Materials needed: Straightest and largest logs.

Steps:
1. Choose suitable logs: Save the largest, straightest, and best logs for sills or foundations.
2. Protect from dampness: If building on the ground (mudsill), protect sill logs from moisture to prevent rotting.
3. Apply wood preservative: Use a wood preservative such as creosote or oil to extend the life of the logs.
sill logs, wood preservation, log cabin foundation, moisture protection
Piling Logs on a Skidway for Easier Handling
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To make logs easier to handle during construction:

Create a Skidway:
• Rest the top ends of several poles on a large log or elevated surface.
• Ensure the lower ends of the poles are on the ground.

Using the Skidway:
• Logs can be rolled...
To make logs easier to handle during construction:

Create a Skidway:
• Rest the top ends of several poles on a large log or elevated surface.
• Ensure the lower ends of the poles are on the ground.

Using the Skidway:
• Logs can be rolled off the skidway as they are needed for construction.
• This reduces effort and makes handling logs more efficient.
skidway, log handling, log cabin construction, pole setup
Preparing a Mudsill Floor
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Preparing a Mudsill Floor:

1. Wet the floor until it becomes spongy.
2. Use the butt end of a log to ram the dirt down hard, creating an even, hard surface.
3. This method was used by some of the greatest men in history...
Procedure for Preparing a Mudsill Floor:

1. Wet the floor until it becomes spongy.
2. Use the butt end of a log to ram the dirt down hard, creating an even, hard surface.
3. This method was used by some of the greatest men in history when they were babies.
mudsill, floor preparation, dirt, log cabin, construction

Emergency Shelter Construction (27) -

Construct a Snow Hole for Immediate Shelter
High Priority Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Materials Needed: Shovel, ponchos, ice or snowblocks, skis, ski poles, snowshoes, sled (optional), boughs (if available).

Steps to Construct a Snow Hole:
1. Choose a Location: Select an area with a thick snowdrift or dig a trench in the...
Materials Needed: Shovel, ponchos, ice or snowblocks, skis, ski poles, snowshoes, sled (optional), boughs (if available).

Steps to Construct a Snow Hole:
1. Choose a Location: Select an area with a thick snowdrift or dig a trench in the snow where you can create a shelter.
2. Dig the Hole: Use a shovel to burrow into the snowdrift or dig a trench deep enough to provide insulation from the cold.
3. Construct the Roof: Use ponchos, ice blocks, or snowblocks supported by skis, ski poles, or snowshoes to create a roof over the hole.
4. Add Insulation: Place a sled inside the shelter for additional insulation if available.
5. Cover and Bedding: If boughs are available, use them to cover the roof and as bedding material inside the shelter.

Notes: This method provides quick shelter in snowy environments and is ideal when immediate protection from wind and cold is necessary.
snow hole, emergency shelter, cold weather survival, snow digging, insulation techniques
Construct a Canoe Tent with Fly (Fig. 9)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, additional canvas for the fly, rope.

1. Combine No. 8 and No. 1 to create a shelter with an awning or fly.
2. Use a rope to suspend both the tent and the fly, allowing them to be adjusted...
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, additional canvas for the fly, rope.

1. Combine No. 8 and No. 1 to create a shelter with an awning or fly.
2. Use a rope to suspend both the tent and the fly, allowing them to be adjusted as needed.
3. The awning can be swung around to any angle, providing flexibility in use.
4. This design offers additional protection from rain and sun while maintaining access to the fire or camp area.

*The canoe tent with a fly is ideal for extended stays or when extra protection is required.*
canoe tent, fly, awning, rope
Construct a Conical Tent or Wigwam (Fig. 7)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas.

1. Turn two corners under to form the base of the tent.
2. Construct an entirely enclosed conical structure, with a door in front for access.
3. Ensure that the shape is stable and...
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas.

1. Turn two corners under to form the base of the tent.
2. Construct an entirely enclosed conical structure, with a door in front for access.
3. Ensure that the shape is stable and provides adequate headroom inside.
4. This design resembles a wigwam, offering protection from wind and rain.

*The conical tent or wigwam is ideal for use in open areas where full coverage is needed.*
conical tent, wigwam, canvas
Construct a Double Lean-To Shelter
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Steps to Construct a Double Lean-To Shelter:

1. Select a location: Choose an area with two sturdy trees or poles and flat ground.
2. Drive support posts: Insert two long, straight branches or poles into the ground at an angle (about 45...
Steps to Construct a Double Lean-To Shelter:

1. Select a location: Choose an area with two sturdy trees or poles and flat ground.
2. Drive support posts: Insert two long, straight branches or poles into the ground at an angle (about 45 degrees) near each tree.
3. Secure the support posts: Tie the top end of each support post to its respective tree trunk using paracord or other available material.
4. Lay the cover: Place a tarp, poncho, or large piece of fabric over both structures, securing one edge to the ground and the opposite edges to the two support posts.
5. Add additional supports: Use smaller branches or poles as crossbars between the support posts and tree trunks for added stability.
6. Cover with materials: Add layers of leaves, grass, or other natural materials on top of the cover for insulation and protection from rain.
7. Create a floor: If possible, place a tarp or other material under the shelter to create a dry sleeping area.
8. Insulate if needed: Use natural materials such as leaves, grass, or pine needles to insulate the ground and add comfort.

Notes:
• Ensure that both support posts are strong enough to hold the weight of the cover and any additional materials.
• If possible, use multiple layers for better insulation and protection from rain.
• Avoid using sharp objects to secure the structure to prevent tearing or damage.
double lean-to shelter, emergency shelter, survival shelter, paracord, crossbars, insulation
Construct a Fallen Tree Bivouac
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Steps to Construct a Fallen Tree Bivouac:

1. Select a location: Choose an area with a fallen tree that is long enough and flat enough to serve as the base of your shelter.
2. Clear the ground: Remove any debris or sharp objects from the...
Steps to Construct a Fallen Tree Bivouac:

1. Select a location: Choose an area with a fallen tree that is long enough and flat enough to serve as the base of your shelter.
2. Clear the ground: Remove any debris or sharp objects from the area where you will be sleeping to prevent injury.
3. Use the fallen tree as a base: Lay the fallen tree on the ground, ensuring it is stable and level.
4. Add insulation: Use leaves, grass, or other natural materials to create a layer of insulation between your body and the ground.
5. Cover with materials: If possible, use tarps, branches, or any available materials to cover the area above you for protection from rain and wind.
6. Secure the cover: Tie the edges of the cover to nearby trees or stakes if necessary to ensure stability.
7. Create a sleeping area: Ensure that the insulation layer is thick enough to provide comfort and protection from cold ground.

Notes:
• Avoid using sharp objects to secure the cover to prevent tearing or damage.
• If possible, use multiple layers for better insulation and protection from rain.
• Ensure that the fallen tree is not rotting or unstable before using it as a base.
fallen tree bivouac, emergency shelter, survival shelter, insulation, tarps, paracord
Construct a Lean-To Shelter Using a Rectangle of Canvas
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, rope or horizontal pole, cords.

1. Fold the rectangle to create a lean-to shelter structure.
2. Suspend the roof front from a rope or horizontal pole using cords.
3. **Fold the two unused...
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, rope or horizontal pole, cords.

1. Fold the rectangle to create a lean-to shelter structure.
2. Suspend the roof front from a rope or horizontal pole using cords.
3. Fold the two unused corners under to form a partial ground cloth.
4. Ensure that the square open front is directed toward the camp fire for warmth and visibility.

*This shelter provides protection from rain and wind while allowing for easy access to the fire.*
lean-to, shelter, canvas, rope, camp fire
Construct a Sapling Shelter
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Steps to Construct a Sapling Shelter:

1. Select a location: Choose an area with sturdy saplings (at least 2 inches in diameter) and flat ground.
2. Dig holes: Dig shallow pits at each end of the intended shelter length, about 6-8 inches...
Steps to Construct a Sapling Shelter:

1. Select a location: Choose an area with sturdy saplings (at least 2 inches in diameter) and flat ground.
2. Dig holes: Dig shallow pits at each end of the intended shelter length, about 6-8 inches deep.
3. Insert saplings: Place two saplings into each hole at a slight angle, leaning inward toward the center of the shelter.
4. Secure saplings: Tie the saplings together at the top with paracord or other available material to form an A-frame structure.
5. Add crossbars: Use additional saplings or branches as crossbars between the two main saplings for added stability and support.
6. Cover with materials: Use tarps, branches, leaves, or any available materials to cover the shelter from above and sides, ensuring it is waterproof and insulated.
7. Create a floor: If possible, place a tarp or other material under the shelter to create a dry sleeping area.
8. Insulate if needed: Use natural materials such as leaves, grass, or pine needles to insulate the ground and add comfort.

Notes:
• Ensure that the saplings are strong enough to support the weight of the covering materials.
• If possible, use multiple layers for better insulation and protection from rain.
• Avoid using sharp objects to secure the structure to prevent tearing or damage.
sapling shelter, emergency shelter, survival shelter, paracord, crossbars, insulation
Construct a Snow Wall for Wind Protection
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Materials Needed: Blocks of compact snow or ice.

Steps to Construct a Snow Wall:
1. Select Location: Choose an area with sufficient snow and ice coverage in open terrain.
2. Form the Structure: Use blocks of compact snow or ice to...
Materials Needed: Blocks of compact snow or ice.

Steps to Construct a Snow Wall:
1. Select Location: Choose an area with sufficient snow and ice coverage in open terrain.
2. Form the Structure: Use blocks of compact snow or ice to build a wall that acts as a windbreak.
3. Ensure Stability: Stack the blocks securely to form a continuous barrier that can effectively reduce wind exposure.

Notes: This method is particularly useful in snowy environments where wind protection is critical for survival.
snow wall, windbreak construction, cold weather shelter, snow protection, ice blocks
Construct a Toque Tent Shelter (Fig. 5)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, two or more guy lines.

1. Use all the cloth to form a triangular ground plan.
2. Create a square front opening for access and ventilation.
3. Ensure there is **plenty of head room at the...
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, two or more guy lines.

1. Use all the cloth to form a triangular ground plan.
2. Create a square front opening for access and ventilation.
3. Ensure there is plenty of head room at the back for comfort.
4. Secure the tent using two or more guy lines for stability.
5. This shelter resembles a toque, providing good coverage and protection from the elements.

*The toque tent is ideal for use in open areas where additional support is needed.*
toque tent, guy lines, canvas
Construct a Triangular Front Shelter (Fig. 4)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, one point of suspension.

1. Use the same plan as No.3, but modify it to have a triangular front.
2. Suspend the shelter from a single point using cords or rope.
3. Ensure that the...
Materials needed: A rectangular piece of canvas, one point of suspension.

1. Use the same plan as No.3, but modify it to have a triangular front.
2. Suspend the shelter from a single point using cords or rope.
3. Ensure that the structure is stable and provides adequate coverage.
4. The triangular front design allows for better wind resistance in certain conditions.

*This variation of the lean-to is useful when only one point of suspension is available.*
triangular shelter, single suspension, canvas
Constructing a Northland Tilt Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Logs or small poles
• Forked sticks
• Sod or browse
• Grass, fine brush, or browse
• Moss and dry grass (for calking)
• Sod or mud
• Fireplace materials (sod or stone)
• Log chimney components

**Steps to Build a Northland...
Materials Needed:
• Logs or small poles
• Forked sticks
• Sod or browse
• Grass, fine brush, or browse
• Moss and dry grass (for calking)
• Sod or mud
• Fireplace materials (sod or stone)
• Log chimney components

Steps to Build a Northland Tilt Shelter:
1. Set Up the Ridge-Pole: Place a ridge-pole on two forked sticks, ensuring it is stable and level.
2. Construct the Frame: Lay logs slanted up against each other and resting upon the ridge-pole. This forms the basic structure of the shelter.
3. Add Support Poles: Use smaller poles laid up against the frame on both the front and rear to reinforce the structure.
4. Cover with Insulation: Cover the frame with sod or browse to create a warm, insulated winter house.
5. Build the Fireplace and Chimney:
- Construct a fireplace using sod or stone as shown in Figs. 269 and 270.
- Build a chimney on top of the fireplace, following the same method used for constructing a log house.
6. Finish the Structure: For the front of the shelter, face it with small logs set on end (as seen in Fig. 189). This provides additional insulation and structural integrity.
7. Seal Cracks:
- If logs are uneven or have gaps, cover them first with grass, fine brush, or browse.
- Apply a layer of sod or mud over the cracks to ensure the shelter is weatherproof and warm.

Tips: This structure can be made more comfortable by adding a log chimney connected to a generous fireplace. The result will be a warm and substantial winter camp suitable for long-term use.
northland tilt, indian log tent, shelter construction, winter house, emergency shelter, insulation, fireplace, chimney
Constructing a Stefansson Sod Shack
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Sod (grass with soil)
• Smaller trees or browse for shingles
• Cane, bamboo, or palmetto leaves (for southern regions)
• Cottonwood and adobe/mud (for southwestern regions)
• Tools for cutting and shaping materials

**Steps...
Materials Required:
• Sod (grass with soil)
• Smaller trees or browse for shingles
• Cane, bamboo, or palmetto leaves (for southern regions)
• Cottonwood and adobe/mud (for southwestern regions)
• Tools for cutting and shaping materials

Steps to Construct the Stefansson Sod Shack:
1. Select a Suitable Location:
- Choose an area with access to sod or alternative roofing materials.
- Ensure the site is elevated to avoid flooding during heavy rains.
2. **Prepare the Foundation:
- Clear the ground of debris and level it as much as possible.
3. **Build the Frame:
- Construct a rectangular frame using available timber, such as smaller trees or poles.
- Ensure the walls are sturdy enough to support the roof structure.
4. **Construct the Roof with Sod:
- Cut sod into manageable pieces and lay them over the frame in overlapping layers.
- The walls should project slightly above the rafters to allow for proper insulation and protection from the elements.
5. **Create an Opening for the Fireplace:
- Leave a gap in the roof directly above the fireplace area to serve as a chimney.
6. **Construct the Doorway:
- Create an opening similar to that of a tent for the doorway.
- In colder months, build a hallway outside the entrance (similar to Navajo earth lodges or Pawnee hogans) to provide additional protection from the cold.
7. **Insulate and Protect the Structure:
- In milder weather, cover the doorway with a skin or other insulating material.
8. **Secure the Roofing Material:
- For southern regions, use cane, bamboo, or palmetto leaves as shingles.
- In the Southwest, cover the roof with adobe or mud for added insulation and durability.

Notes:
• The Stefansson sod shack is particularly effective in cold climates when the sod is frozen and covered with snow.
• In summer, the roof may leak, so it's advisable to use a tent during warmer months.
• This design was adapted from traditional northern shelters and is suitable for outdoor survival scenarios.
Stefansson sod shack, emergency shelter construction, outdoor shelter, survival shelter, cold weather shelter
Constructing the Red Jacket Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Overview: The Red Jacket is a more complex camp structure with straight walls and is considered a white man's camp in form. It evolved from simpler forms such as the New Brunswick and Christopher Gist shelters.

**Steps to Build the Red...
Overview: The Red Jacket is a more complex camp structure with straight walls and is considered a white man's camp in form. It evolved from simpler forms such as the New Brunswick and Christopher Gist shelters.

Steps to Build the Red Jacket:
1. Start with Simpler Structures: Understand how to build the New Brunswick shelter first, then move on to constructing the Christopher Gist shelter.
2. Build the New Brunswick Shelter:
- Use basic materials such as logs and poles to create a simple, sturdy structure.
3. Construct the Christopher Gist Shelter:
- This is an intermediate step between the New Brunswick and Red Jacket shelters. It involves more refined construction techniques but still retains simplicity.
4. Evolve into the Red Jacket: Once the simpler forms are mastered, build upon them to create the Red Jacket shelter with straight walls and a more defined structure.

Notes: The Red Jacket is considered a good, comfortable, rough camp that can be adapted for various environments. It is shown in Figs. 190 and 191 as an evolved form of earlier shelters.
red jacket shelter, new brunswick, christopher gist, emergency shelter construction, straight wall shelters
Constructing the Scout-Master Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Three forked sticks (A, B, and C)
• Branches or poles for framework
• Browse or boughs for thatching
• Optional: Elm, spruce, or birch bark; shingles

Steps to Build the Shelter:
1. Create the Framework: Take three...
Materials Needed:
• Three forked sticks (A, B, and C)
• Branches or poles for framework
• Browse or boughs for thatching
• Optional: Elm, spruce, or birch bark; shingles

Steps to Build the Shelter:
1. Create the Framework: Take three forked sticks (A, B, and C) and interlock their forked ends so they stand as shown in Fig. 12.
2. Rest Branches on the Framework: Place branches with their butt ends up over this framework, as shown in the right-hand shelter of Fig. 13.
3. Alternative Framework Option: Alternatively, lay a number of poles as shown in the left-hand figure (Fig. 12) and thatch them with browse as illustrated by the left-hand shelter in Fig. 13.
4. Thatching Options: Use elm, spruce, or birch bark and shingle as shown in Fig. 14 for additional protection. Alternatively, use balsam, spruce, pine, or hemlock boughs; cat-tails; rushes (see Figs. 66 and 69); or any kind of long-stemmed weeds or palmetto leaves.
5. Adjust Size: These shelters may be built for one person or made large enough to accommodate several men.

Notes:
• This shelter is versatile and can be adapted based on available materials.
• The framework should be sturdy enough to support the weight of thatching materials.
scout-master shelter, emergency shelter, framework construction, thatching materials
Proper Thatching of Walls and Roofs with Boughs
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Green boughs with leaves
• Common sense and understanding of natural materials

Steps to Thatch Properly:
1. Select the Boughs: Use green boughs with leaves adhering, which can be rested against the cliffs or other...
Materials Needed:
• Green boughs with leaves
• Common sense and understanding of natural materials

Steps to Thatch Properly:
1. Select the Boughs: Use green boughs with leaves adhering, which can be rested against the cliffs or other structures.
2. Position the Boughs Correctly: Set the boughs upside down so that they will shed rain and not hold it, preventing dripping into your shelter.
3. Ensure Proper Orientation: Make sure all the boughs point downward and not upward as most naturally grow. This is crucial for proper water drainage.
4. Avoid Common Mistakes: Do not place the boughs with their branches pointing upward, as this can lead to water retention and dripping into your shelter. This mistake was observed in some Boy Scout instructors who failed to consider natural rain shedding properties.

Notes:
• The wilderness itself provides privacy, so there is no need for excessive thatching for concealment.
• Proper orientation of boughs ensures effective protection from the elements.
thatching walls, proper bough placement, rain shedding, shelter construction
Building a Chimehuevis Shelter in the Southwest
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Upright poles
• Additional poles for roof support
• Logs or poles for roofing
• Sod or dirt for covering

Steps:
1. Set up upright poles to form the walls of the shelter (as shown in Fig. 141).
2. Surround the structure...
Materials Required:
• Upright poles
• Additional poles for roof support
• Logs or poles for roofing
• Sod or dirt for covering

Steps:
1. Set up upright poles to form the walls of the shelter (as shown in Fig. 141).
2. Surround the structure with a circle of additional poles, forming a frame for the roof.
3. Place logs or poles across the top of the frame to create a sloped roof.
4. Cover the roof with sod or dirt to provide insulation and protection from the sun and rain.
5. Ensure that all parts of the structure are securely fastened together.

Notes:
• This shelter is ideal for hot weather due to its natural insulation properties.
• Can be constructed quickly using locally available materials.
Chimehuevis shelter, Southwest construction, sod roof, upright poles
Building a Solid-Wall Railroad-Tie Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Railroad ties
• Stakes or posts for support

Steps:
1. Lay railroad ties flat on the ground, stacking them to form solid walls (as shown in Fig. 137).
2. Secure each tie with stakes driven into the ground on both sides...
Materials Required:
• Railroad ties
• Stakes or posts for support

Steps:
1. Lay railroad ties flat on the ground, stacking them to form solid walls (as shown in Fig. 137).
2. Secure each tie with stakes driven into the ground on both sides of the ties.
3. Continue building up the structure until desired height is achieved.
4. Ensure that all ties are tightly packed and secured for stability.

Notes:
• This method provides a more stable and durable shelter than the tent-style.
• Ideal for use in areas where railroad ties are readily available.
railroad tie shelter, solid wall construction, stake support
Construct a Bog Ken Shelter on Stilts
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Stilts, platform framework, arches (similar to Iroquois design), horizontal poles, lashing material (e.g., rope or twine), thatching materials (bark, rushes, hay), browse for floor covering.

Steps:
1. **Build the Platform...
Materials Needed: Stilts, platform framework, arches (similar to Iroquois design), horizontal poles, lashing material (e.g., rope or twine), thatching materials (bark, rushes, hay), browse for floor covering.

Steps:
1. Build the Platform Framework: Construct a raised platform on stilts where the ground is marshy and unfit to sleep upon. Ensure the platform is stable and elevated above the damp ground.
2. Create Arched Structure: Use uprights on both sides of the platform, bending their ends over and lashing them together to form arches for the roof. This structure resembles the Iroquois lodge design.
3. Add Horizontal Poles: Over the arches, lash horizontal poles in the same manner as described for the construction of an Iroquois lodge. These will support the thatching or roofing material.
4. Thatch the Roof: Secure thatching materials (such as bark or rushes) to the horizontal poles using lashing techniques shown in Fig. 66. Ensure the roof is tightly covered and waterproof.
5. Floor Construction: The floor of the bog ken should be more elaborate than other shelters. Halve the poles before laying them for the floor. Cover these with browse, hay, or rushes to create a dry and insulated sleeping surface.

Additional Features:
• To prevent varmints from climbing the supporting poles, slide a tin pan with a hole in the bottom over the poles (as shown in Fig. 67).
• For added protection around the front porch or balcony, spring sticks into place to form a railing (as illustrated in Fig. 68).
bog ken, shelter on stilts, marshy ground shelter, arch structure, thatching roof, varmint prevention
Construct a Fagot Shack Using Small Twigs and Underbrush
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Dwarf willows
• Bamboo cane
• Alders or other small underbrush
• Improvised twine
• Stakes
• Browse (small twigs with leaves)

Procedure Steps:
1. Gather Materials: Collect a plentiful supply of twigs and small...
Materials Needed:
• Dwarf willows
• Bamboo cane
• Alders or other small underbrush
• Improvised twine
• Stakes
• Browse (small twigs with leaves)

Procedure Steps:
1. Gather Materials: Collect a plentiful supply of twigs and small branches from dwarf willows, bamboo cane, alders, or similar underbrush.
2. Bind Twigs into Bundles: Use improvised twine to bind the twigs into bundles of equal size. These will serve as building blocks for the structure.
3. Lay the Wall Structure: Arrange the bundles in a wall-like formation, ensuring that joints between bundles are staggered so they do not form continuous lines. This helps increase stability and strength.
4. Secure with Stakes: Hold the wall in place by driving stakes into the ground at appropriate intervals (as shown in Fig. 26).
5. Level Bundles Using Browse: Use small twigs with leaves attached (browse) to level the bundles and prevent them from rocking on uneven surfaces.
6. Create Doorways and Windows: Construct doorways and window openings using standard techniques. For windows, use rods laid over the top of fagots to create a firm window-sill (as shown in Fig. 27). Similarly, place rods across the top of doorways for stability.
7. Roof Construction: After erecting the walls, construct a roof either using a beaver-mat or by creating a frame and thatching it with small sticks, straw, hay, rushes, or browse (as shown in Figs. 66, 69, and 28). This will help shed rain effectively.

Additional Notes:
• The fagot shack is ideal for areas where large timber is not available but smaller underbrush is plentiful.
• This method avoids the need to cut down large or valuable trees, as it uses lower branches of existing trees (cut close to the trunk, as shown in Fig. 121).
• For a more permanent structure, plaster the outside and inside walls with soft mud or clay to transform it into an adobe shack.
fagot shack, small twigs, underbrush, emergency shelter, no tree cutting
Construct a Lean-To Shelter
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Materials Needed: Two trees or sturdy poles (4–12” in diameter), a pole for the roof support (at least 3–4” in diameter), and several long poles for stringers.

Steps to Construct:
1. Select a site with two trees spaced far enough apart...
Materials Needed: Two trees or sturdy poles (4–12” in diameter), a pole for the roof support (at least 3–4” in diameter), and several long poles for stringers.

Steps to Construct:
1. Select a site with two trees spaced far enough apart that a man can lie down between them. If no trees are available, insert two sturdy poles into the ground at the proper distance apart.
2. Cut a pole to support the roof. It should be long enough to extend 4–6” past both trees and tied horizontally between the two trees approximately 1 meter off the deck.
3. Cut several long poles to be used as stringers. Place them along the horizontal support bar approximately every 1½’ and lay on the ground. All stringers may be tied to or laid on the horizontal support bar.
4. Construct a short wall or use rocks/logs on the ground to lift the stringers off the ground, creating additional height and living room dimensions.
5. Cut several saplings and weave them horizontally between the stringers. Cover the roof with water-repellent and insulating material.

Considerations:
• This is an effective shelter in heavily forested areas but does not offer a great degree of protection from the elements.
• Ensure that the shelter provides adequate protection from the elements (sun, wind, rain, snow).
• Plan for worst-case scenarios.
lean-to shelter, emergency shelter, forested area construction, stringer framework
Construct a Snow Trench Shelter
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Materials Required: Hard pack snow or ice, knife or sharp object for cutting snow blocks.

Steps to Construct a Snow Trench Shelter:
1. Select the Location: Choose an area with hard pack snow (such as alpine or glacier environments)...
Materials Required: Hard pack snow or ice, knife or sharp object for cutting snow blocks.

Steps to Construct a Snow Trench Shelter:
1. Select the Location: Choose an area with hard pack snow (such as alpine or glacier environments) where trees or building materials are not available.
2. Dig the Trench: Use your hands, a knife, or any sharp object to dig a shallow trench in the snow. The trench should be wide enough to accommodate one person lying down and deep enough to provide insulation from the cold air above.
3. Cut Snow Blocks: Cut blocks of snow or ice using a knife or other sharp tool. These blocks will be used as walls or additional insulation within the trench.
4. Build Walls (if needed): Place the cut snow blocks along the sides and ends of the trench to create walls that help retain body heat inside the shelter.
5. Pack Snow for Insulation: Add more loose snow on top of the trench and around the structure to provide additional insulation from the cold air above.

Important Notes:
• This is a short-term shelter suitable for extreme cold environments where traditional materials are not available.
• Ensure that the trench is deep enough to protect against wind and cold air.
• Use caution when cutting snow blocks with sharp tools to avoid injury.
snow trench shelter, hard pack snow, emergency shelter construction
Construct a Wigwam Shelter
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Materials Required:
• Three main poles (approx. 10 feet long, 3 inches thick)
• Several side poles (2 inches thick or less, same length as main poles)
• Short poles for support
• Ponchos, canvas, or other suitable material for covering
• Boughs...
Materials Required:
• Three main poles (approx. 10 feet long, 3 inches thick)
• Several side poles (2 inches thick or less, same length as main poles)
• Short poles for support
• Ponchos, canvas, or other suitable material for covering
• Boughs or branches for bedding and insulation
• Snow for sealing the shelter

Steps to Build a Wigwam Shelter:
1. In lightly wooded areas, select three main poles about 10 feet long and 3 inches thick.
2. Tie them together approximately one foot from the small end.
3. Place the tied ends uppermost and position the poles upright in the ground.
4. Spread the poles so that the distance from the ground to the point where they are secured is about eight feet.
5. Select several side poles (about 2 inches thick or less) of similar length to the main poles.
6. Lean these side poles against the tie position of the main poles for additional support.
7. Place short poles flat on the ground between the ground ends of the side and main poles to hold them in place.
8. Cover the frame with ponchos, canvas, or other suitable material, tying separate pieces together.
9. Leave an opening for an entrance.
10. If boughs or branches are the only available material, use more side poles to create a closer frame on which to weave.
11. Bank the lower edge of the completed wigwam with snow to prevent drafts.
12. Build a bough bed in the same manner as for a lean-to.
13. Hang an improvised fire box (made from a large tin can) from the top of the lean-to by a wire.
14. Create a draft channel under the wall and include a smoke hole at the apex for ventilation.
15. Alternatively, heat the wigwam by building a fire outside the door with a reflector wall.
wigwam shelter, cold weather survival, emergency shelter construction, military shelter, insulation techniques
Construct an A-Frame Shelter
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Materials Required: Pine boughs or tentage, sturdy branches or poles for framework.

Steps to Construct an A-Frame Shelter:
1. Select and Prepare Framework: Choose two long, straight poles or branches that are approximately 6–8 feet in...
Materials Required: Pine boughs or tentage, sturdy branches or poles for framework.

Steps to Construct an A-Frame Shelter:
1. Select and Prepare Framework: Choose two long, straight poles or branches that are approximately 6–8 feet in length. These will form the A-frame structure.
2. Assemble the Frame: Place the two poles together at one end to create a triangle shape (A-frame). Secure them together using natural materials such as vines, rope, or by interweaving the ends if possible.
3. Add Support Posts: If needed for stability, add additional shorter posts between the two main poles to reinforce the structure and provide more space inside the shelter.
4. Cover with Insulation Material: Interweave pine boughs or use tentage over the framework to create a roof and walls. Ensure that the material is tightly packed to prevent gaps through which cold air can enter.
5. Insulate with Snow: Pack snow onto the outside of the shelter structure for insulation. The snow will act as an insulating layer, helping to retain body heat inside the shelter.

Important Notes:
• This shelter is suitable for 1–3 individuals.
• Ensure that the A-frame is stable and not leaning to one side before adding insulation materials.
• Avoid using wet or decaying pine boughs as they may not provide adequate insulation.
A-frame shelter, pine boughs, snow insulation, emergency shelter construction
Constructing a Barrel Dugout Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Barrels
• Sand, gravel, or dirt for filling
• Logs or poles for roofing
• Sod or dirt for covering
• Stakes or posts for support

Steps:
1. Dig a hole in the ground to serve as the foundation of the shelter (as shown in...
Materials Required:
• Barrels
• Sand, gravel, or dirt for filling
• Logs or poles for roofing
• Sod or dirt for covering
• Stakes or posts for support

Steps:
1. Dig a hole in the ground to serve as the foundation of the shelter (as shown in Fig. 142).
2. Level the floor inside the dugout.
3. Place rows of barrels around the foundation, filling them with sand, gravel, or dirt for stability.
4. Stack another row of barrels on top of the first, leaving space for a window and door opening.
5. Cover the structure with logs or poles to form a roof, then cover it with sod or dirt for insulation.
6. Fill in the sides of the dugout with dirt, leaving the window and door openings exposed.
7. Use one barrel as a chimney by cutting an opening in its top and placing it near the roof.

Notes:
• This shelter is ideal for areas where timber is scarce but barrels are available (e.g., mining or homesteading camps).
• Provides good insulation and protection from harsh weather conditions.
barrel dugout shelter, barrel construction, sod roof, chimney barrel
Constructing a Tent-Style Railroad-Tie Shack
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Railroad ties
• Ridge-pole (for safety)

Steps:
1. Lay railroad ties flat on the ground, resting their ends together in a tent-like shape.
2. Ensure that the structure is stable by using the weight of the ties to hold...
Materials Required:
• Railroad ties
• Ridge-pole (for safety)

Steps:
1. Lay railroad ties flat on the ground, resting their ends together in a tent-like shape.
2. Ensure that the structure is stable by using the weight of the ties to hold it up.
3. Warning: This style may be dangerous due to the risk of falling into the structure. Avoid building this for children or inexperienced builders.
4. For added safety, install a ridge-pole (as shown in Fig. 139) and rest the ties against it.
5. Secure the ridge-pole firmly to prevent collapse or injury.

Notes:
• This structure is not recommended for long-term use due to its instability.
• Use only if temporary shelter is needed, and always ensure safety precautions are followed.
railroad tie shack, tent-style shelter, ridge pole, temporary shelter
Constructing an Adirondack-Style Railroad-Tie Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Railroad ties
• Ridge-pole
• Shears (two sticks bound together)

Steps:
1. Set up two shears at each end of the structure by binding two sticks together near the top and spreading them apart to form a V-shape.
2. Place...
Materials Required:
• Railroad ties
• Ridge-pole
• Shears (two sticks bound together)

Steps:
1. Set up two shears at each end of the structure by binding two sticks together near the top and spreading them apart to form a V-shape.
2. Place the ridge-pole across the shears, securing it in place.
3. Lay railroad ties flat on the ground and rest them against the ridge-pole (as shown in Figs. 139 and 140).
4. Ensure that all ties are securely placed and supported by the ridge-pole.
5. Cover the structure with dirt or sod for insulation and weather protection.

Notes:
• This style is more stable than the tent-style and provides better protection from the elements.
• Ideal for use in areas where timber is scarce but railroad ties are available.
Adirondack-style shelter, railroad tie construction, ridge pole, shears
Construct a Simple Lean-To Thatched Shelter
Reference Source: the ten bushcraft books
Title: Construct a Simple Lean-To Thatched Shelter

Overview: A simple lean-to thatched shelter is ideal for short-term use in temperate climates and can be built quickly with local materials.

Materials Required:
• Round poles (for the...
Title: Construct a Simple Lean-To Thatched Shelter

Overview: A simple lean-to thatched shelter is ideal for short-term use in temperate climates and can be built quickly with local materials.

Materials Required:
• Round poles (for the framework)
• Long grass, ferns, reeds, or other fibrous material (for thatching)
• Vines, bark strips, or other natural fibrous material (for lashings)
• A good knife, hatchet, or axe

Steps:
1. Gather Materials: Collect sufficient poles for the framework and thatching materials from your immediate surroundings.
2. Prepare Site: Choose a flat, dry area for construction. Clear any debris or obstacles.
3. Build Framework: Use round poles to create a simple A-frame structure leaning against a tree or sturdy object (e.g., a fallen log). Secure the base of the framework with lashings made from vines or bark strips.
4. Add Thatching: Layer long grass, reeds, or other fibrous material over the framework in overlapping rows to ensure weatherproofing. Press down firmly to secure each layer.
5. Secure Lashings: Use additional lashings to tie down the thatching and reinforce the structure where necessary.
6. Complete Shelter: Ensure the shelter is stable and provides adequate protection from rain and wind.

Notes: This shelter can be constructed in one to three hours by a single person or small group.
lean-to, thatched shelter, temporary shelter, bushcraft, survival shelter

Roof Construction (19) -

Constructing a Clay Roof for Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Browse
• Hay
• Straw
• Dry leaves or dry grass
• Moist blue clay, yellow clay, hard-pan, or simple mud

Steps to Construct the Roof:
1. Lay Base Layer: Cover the roof with a thick layer of browse, hay, straw, dry...
Materials Needed:
• Browse
• Hay
• Straw
• Dry leaves or dry grass
• Moist blue clay, yellow clay, hard-pan, or simple mud

Steps to Construct the Roof:
1. Lay Base Layer: Cover the roof with a thick layer of browse, hay, straw, dry leaves, or dry grass.
2. Apply Clay Mixture: Spread moist blue clay, yellow clay, hard-pan, or simple mud over the base layer.
3. Compact the Clay: Trample down the clay mixture firmly to ensure it fills all cracks and crannies in the thatch underneath.
4. Form a Firm Covering: Continue trampling until you have formed a firm covering of clay several inches thick.

Notes:
• This method creates a durable, waterproof roof suitable for long-term shelter.
• Ensure the clay is moist enough to bind the materials together effectively.
clay roof, roofing material, shelter construction, waterproofing
Calculate Roof Slope Using Standard Units
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Calculate Roof Slope:

1. Understand the slope terminology: The slope of a roof is expressed as a ratio of rise (vertical distance) to run (horizontal distance), typically in inches per foot.
2. Determine the rise and run:...
Steps to Calculate Roof Slope:

1. Understand the slope terminology: The slope of a roof is expressed as a ratio of rise (vertical distance) to run (horizontal distance), typically in inches per foot.
2. Determine the rise and run: Measure the vertical height (rise) and horizontal length (run) of the roof section you are calculating.
3. Use standard units: Express the slope using the standard unit of 12 inches for the run. For example, a slope of 8/12 means that the roof rises 8 inches for every 12 inches of horizontal distance.
4. Convert non-standard measurements: If the plan provides rise and run in different units (e.g., 2 inches per 5 inches), use proportion to convert it to standard units:
- Example: 2/5 = x/12 → 5x = 24 → x = 4.8 (or 4 4/5)
5. Apply the slope ratio: Use this calculated slope ratio for framing and material selection.

Notes:
• This method is commonly used in roof construction to ensure proper drainage and structural integrity.
• Accurate calculation of slope ensures that materials are properly aligned and fastened.
roof slope, rise run, standard units, proportion calculation, framing
Calculate Total Rise for Roof Framing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Calculate Total Rise:

1. Understand the term: The total rise is the vertical distance from the intersection of the upper edge of the rafter with the building limit line to the top of the ridge.
2. Measure the rise: Use a...
Steps to Calculate Total Rise:

1. Understand the term: The total rise is the vertical distance from the intersection of the upper edge of the rafter with the building limit line to the top of the ridge.
2. Measure the rise: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine the vertical height from the base of the rafter to the ridge.
3. Calculate using formula: Total rise = (rise in inches) × (total run in feet)
4. Record the total rise: This value is essential for determining roof slope and framing requirements.

Notes:
• The total rise helps determine how steep or flat a roof should be based on structural needs and environmental conditions.
• It is used in conjunction with span to calculate rafter length and other components.
total rise, vertical distance, ridge measurement, framing calculation
Construct a Gable Roof for Army Use
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Construct a Gable Roof:

1. Understand the design: A gable roof has two sloped sides that meet at a central ridge, forming a triangular shape (gable) at the top.
2. Use appropriate materials: Ensure the roof is constructed with...
Steps to Construct a Gable Roof:

1. Understand the design: A gable roof has two sloped sides that meet at a central ridge, forming a triangular shape (gable) at the top.
2. Use appropriate materials: Ensure the roof is constructed with materials suitable for the climate and load-bearing requirements.
3. Ensure proper slope: The roof must be sloped to allow water runoff. In areas with heavy snowfall or high winds, construct it more rigidly and/or increase the pitch of the roof.
4. Install ridge beam: Place a central ridge beam at the top of the structure where the two slopes meet.
5. Frame rafters: Install rafters on both sides of the ridge, sloping downward to meet the wall plates.
6. Secure with proper fasteners: Use nails or other appropriate fasteners to secure all components together for stability and durability.
7. Add roofing material: Cover the roof with waterproof materials such as shingles, tar paper, or metal sheets to protect against rain and snow.
8. Check for structural integrity: Ensure that the roof is strong enough to withstand environmental conditions like wind and heavy snow loads.

Notes:
• The gable roof is simple in design and economical to construct, making it ideal for military use.
• This type of roof can be used on any type of structure.
gable roof, roof construction, army shelter, waterproofing, slope, ridge beam
Construct a Gable and Valley Roof for Complex Structures
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Construct a Gable and Valley Roof:

1. Understand the design: This roof is formed by two gable roofs intersecting each other, with a valley where they meet.
2. Frame both gable sections: Begin by constructing two separate gable...
Steps to Construct a Gable and Valley Roof:

1. Understand the design: This roof is formed by two gable roofs intersecting each other, with a valley where they meet.
2. Frame both gable sections: Begin by constructing two separate gable roofs on either side of the building.
3. Create the valley: Where the two gable roofs intersect, form a valley by ensuring that the slopes are angled in different directions.
4. Install ridge beams and rafters: Place ridge beams at the top of each section and install rafters to support both sides of the roof.
5. Secure all components: Use fasteners such as nails or screws to ensure that all parts are securely attached.
6. Cover with roofing material: Install waterproof materials like shingles, metal sheets, or tar paper over the sloped surfaces.
7. Check for structural integrity: Ensure the roof is strong enough to withstand wind and snow loads.

Notes:
• This type of roof is more complex and requires careful planning and construction.
• It is not commonly used in standard military construction due to its complexity and labor requirements.
gable valley roof, complex structure, roof construction, valley intersection, waterproofing
Construct a Hip Roof for Structural Stability
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Construct a Hip Roof:

1. Understand the design: A hip roof has four sloped sides that meet at the center of the building, with rafters extending diagonally from the corners.
2. Frame the structure: Begin by constructing the...
Steps to Construct a Hip Roof:

1. Understand the design: A hip roof has four sloped sides that meet at the center of the building, with rafters extending diagonally from the corners.
2. Frame the structure: Begin by constructing the main walls and ensuring they are level and plumb.
3. Install ridge beam: Place a central ridge beam where all four slopes will converge.
4. Add diagonal rafters: Install rafters at each corner of the building, extending diagonally toward the center or end of the ridge.
5. Frame additional rafters: Add more rafters between the diagonal ones to provide support and stability.
6. Secure all components: Use fasteners such as nails or screws to ensure that all parts are securely attached.
7. Cover with roofing material: Install waterproof materials like shingles, metal sheets, or tar paper over the sloped surface.
8. Check for structural integrity: Ensure the roof is strong enough to withstand wind and snow loads.

Notes:
• This type of roof provides greater stability and resistance to wind compared to other types.
• It requires more time and labor to construct than simpler roof designs.
hip roof, structural stability, roof construction, diagonal rafters, waterproofing
Construct a Lean-To or Shed Roof for Temporary Use
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Construct a Lean-To or Shed Roof:

1. Understand the design: A lean-to roof has a single slope, with one side of the structure higher than the other.
2. Use temporary materials: Since this is typically used for hasty or...
Steps to Construct a Lean-To or Shed Roof:

1. Understand the design: A lean-to roof has a single slope, with one side of the structure higher than the other.
2. Use temporary materials: Since this is typically used for hasty or temporary construction, use lightweight and easily available materials.
3. Frame walls: Construct two parallel walls, with one wall at a higher elevation than the other.
4. Install support posts: Place vertical posts along the lower wall to provide additional support if needed.
5. Add roof slope: Install rafters or beams that slope from the higher wall down to the lower wall.
6. Secure roofing material: Cover the sloped surface with waterproof materials such as tar paper, tarpaulin, or shingles.
7. Ensure stability: Use fasteners and bracing to ensure the roof is stable and can withstand wind and weather conditions.

Notes:
• This type of roof is suitable for sheds, additions to buildings, or temporary structures.
• It is ideal when quick construction is needed.
lean-to roof, shed roof, temporary shelter, waterproofing, slope
Constructing a Roof Using Various Materials
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials: Lumber, poles, planks, tar paper, barrel staves, old tin roofing, sod.

Steps:
1. Use lumber for the roof as shown in Fig. 160.
2. Use poles like those on the Wyoming Olebo (Fig. 236) for a different style of roof.
3. Construct a...
Materials: Lumber, poles, planks, tar paper, barrel staves, old tin roofing, sod.

Steps:
1. Use lumber for the roof as shown in Fig. 160.
2. Use poles like those on the Wyoming Olebo (Fig. 236) for a different style of roof.
3. Construct a roof using planks and cover with tar paper, as illustrated in Figs. 296–299.
4. Create a shingled roof by using barrel staves as shingles.
5. Cover the planking with bits of old tin roofing tacked over it.
6. Regardless of material used, ensure that the roof is waterproof and will keep out water.
7. After construction, cover the roof with sod to improve concealment and insulation.
roof construction, waterproofing, sod roof, tar paper, barrel staves, tin roofing
Constructing a Thatched Roof
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials needed: Rye or wheat straw, hay, bulrushes, wooden needle (1 foot long and pointed at both ends), thatching twine, wire netting.

Steps:
1. Soak the thatch in water and straighten any bent straws.
2. Build the roof steeply,...
Materials needed: Rye or wheat straw, hay, bulrushes, wooden needle (1 foot long and pointed at both ends), thatching twine, wire netting.

Steps:
1. Soak the thatch in water and straighten any bent straws.
2. Build the roof steeply, as shown in Fig. 57.
3. Create a wooden needle with a foot length and pointed at both ends, as illustrated in Fig. 59.
4. Tie thatching twine to the middle of the needle.
5. Bundle the thatch into groups four inches thick and one foot wide, like those shown in Fig. 60.
6. Lay these bundles along next to the eaves, as depicted in Fig. 58.
7. Sew the bundles in place by running the needle up through the wire netting to the man on the outside who pushes it back to the man on the inside.
8. Make a knot at each wisp of thatch until one layer is finished.
9. Allow the lower ends to overhang the eaves.
10. Proceed as illustrated by Fig. 66 and described under the heading of the bog ken.
thatch, roof construction, straw, wire netting, wooden needle, thatching twine
Define and Calculate Line Length for Roof Framing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Define and Calculate Line Length:

1. Understand the term: The line length is the hypotenuse of a triangle formed by the total run (base) and total rise (altitude).
2. Measure the run and rise: Use a measuring tape or ruler to...
Steps to Define and Calculate Line Length:

1. Understand the term: The line length is the hypotenuse of a triangle formed by the total run (base) and total rise (altitude).
2. Measure the run and rise: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine both values.
3. Calculate using Pythagorean theorem: Line length = √(total run² + total rise²)
4. Record the line length: This value is essential for determining rafter length and other components.

Notes:
• The line length helps in accurately calculating the length of rafters needed for construction.
• It ensures that all components are properly aligned and spaced.
line length, hypotenuse calculation, rafter measurement, framing square
Define and Calculate Pitch for Roof Construction
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Define and Calculate Pitch:

1. Understand the term: Pitch is the ratio of rise to roof span, typically expressed as a fraction (e.g., 8/12).
2. Measure the rise and span: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine both...
Steps to Define and Calculate Pitch:

1. Understand the term: Pitch is the ratio of rise to roof span, typically expressed as a fraction (e.g., 8/12).
2. Measure the rise and span: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine both values.
3. Calculate pitch: Divide the total rise by the total span to get the pitch ratio.
4. Record the pitch: This value is essential for determining roof slope and framing requirements.

Notes:
• Pitch helps in selecting appropriate materials and ensuring proper drainage.
• It is used in conjunction with other measurements to calculate rafter length and other components.
roof pitch, rise span ratio, framing calculation, slope determination
Define and Calculate Rise in Inches for Roof Slope
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Define and Calculate Rise in Inches:

1. Understand the term: The rise in inches is the number of inches that a roof rises for every foot of run.
2. Measure the rise: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine how much the roof...
Steps to Define and Calculate Rise in Inches:

1. Understand the term: The rise in inches is the number of inches that a roof rises for every foot of run.
2. Measure the rise: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine how much the roof rises over a 1-foot horizontal distance.
3. Record the measurement: This value is essential for calculating slope and determining rafter length.

Notes:
• The rise in inches helps ensure that the roof has the correct pitch for proper drainage and structural integrity.
• It is used in conjunction with run to calculate total rise and other components.
rise inches, horizontal distance, slope calculation, framing measurement
Define and Measure Roof Span for Proper Framing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Define and Measure Roof Span:

1. Understand the term: The span of a roof is the shortest distance between two opposite rafter seats, measured at right angles to the ridge.
2. Measure the span: Use a measuring tape or ruler to...
Steps to Define and Measure Roof Span:

1. Understand the term: The span of a roof is the shortest distance between two opposite rafter seats, measured at right angles to the ridge.
2. Measure the span: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine the distance from one outside wall plate to the other, perpendicular to the direction of the ridge.
3. Record the measurement: Ensure that the span is accurately recorded for use in calculating rafters and other roof components.

Notes:
• The span is essential for determining the size and number of rafters needed.
• It helps ensure proper structural balance and load distribution across the roof.
roof span, rafter seats, ridge measurement, framing calculation
Define and Measure Total Run for Roof Framing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Define and Measure Total Run:

1. Understand the term: The total run is the level distance over which a rafter passes, typically one-half of the span in an ordinary gable roof.
2. Measure the run: Use a measuring tape or ruler...
Steps to Define and Measure Total Run:

1. Understand the term: The total run is the level distance over which a rafter passes, typically one-half of the span in an ordinary gable roof.
2. Measure the run: Use a measuring tape or ruler to determine the horizontal distance from one end of the rafter to the other.
3. Record the measurement: This value is essential for calculating slope and determining rafter length.

Notes:
• The total run helps in laying out rafters using a framing square.
• It ensures that all components are properly aligned and spaced.
total run, horizontal distance, rafter measurement, framing calculation
Understand Plumb and Level Lines in Roof Framing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Understand and Use Plumb and Level Lines:

1. Define plumb line: A plumb line is a vertical line on a rafter when it is in its proper position.
2. Define level line: A level line is a horizontal line on a rafter when it is in...
Steps to Understand and Use Plumb and Level Lines:

1. Define plumb line: A plumb line is a vertical line on a rafter when it is in its proper position.
2. Define level line: A level line is a horizontal line on a rafter when it is in its proper position.
3. Use for layout and alignment: These lines are used to ensure that rafters are properly aligned and positioned during construction.
4. Check with tools: Use a plumb bob or level to verify the accuracy of these lines.

Notes:
• Plumb and level lines help in ensuring structural integrity and proper alignment of roof components.
• They are essential for accurate framing and construction.
plumb line, level line, rafter alignment, framing tools
Understand the Unit of Run in Roof Framing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Understand and Use the Unit of Run:

1. Define the term: The unit of run is a standard measurement used for roof framing, typically 12 inches (1 foot).
2. Use the unit of run: This standard unit allows for consistent...
Steps to Understand and Use the Unit of Run:

1. Define the term: The unit of run is a standard measurement used for roof framing, typically 12 inches (1 foot).
2. Use the unit of run: This standard unit allows for consistent calculations when using a framing square or other tools.
3. Apply to slope calculation: For example, if a plan provides a rise of 8 inches per 5 inches of run, convert it to the standard unit by proportion:
- Example: 8/5 = x/12 → 5x = 96 → x = 19.2 (or 19 1/5)
4. Record and use: This converted measurement is used for accurate framing and material selection.

Notes:
• The unit of run ensures consistency in roof construction across different projects.
• It simplifies the process of calculating slope and other measurements.
unit run, standard measurement, framing square, slope calculation
Construct Truss Type Rafters for TO Type Building
Reference Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. General Assembly:
- Rafters are assembled into trusses, not used singly.
- Two rafters are connected at the top by a rafter or collar tie well nailed into both rafters.
- Before any ties or chords are nailed, check...
Procedure:

1. General Assembly:
- Rafters are assembled into trusses, not used singly.
- Two rafters are connected at the top by a rafter or collar tie well nailed into both rafters.
- Before any ties or chords are nailed, check accuracy of the rafter cuts by spreading the lower ends to match the building width. This can be done using a template or measuring with a tape between seat cuts.

2. Truss Members:
- Rafters (Upper Chords): Same as rafters in end panel; carry roof load to studs.
- Lower Chord (Ceiling Joist): A 1 x 6 member spanning from the lower ends of two rafters. Increased to 2 x 6 if a ceiling is used and attached to them.
- Hanger: A 1 x 6 vertical member in the center of the truss connecting the peak of the rafters to the center of the lower chord.
- Web Members: Diagonal braces (cut from 2 x 4's) that tie the center of the lower chord with the centers of the rafters.
- Rafter Tie: A piece of 1 x 8 connecting two rafters at the peak, similar to end panel configuration.
- Knee Braces: Members tying the roof system to the wall system. Attached to rafter, lower chord, and stud in the sidewall panel.

3. Advantages of Truss System:
- Smaller size lumber can be used compared to plain rafter systems.
- Trusses can be fabricated as a unit for later erection, saving time and labor on most projects.

4. Templates or Jigs:
- Rafter framing without ridgeboards can be completed rapidly using truss assembly jigs or templates.
- Template Layout Steps:
1. Measure and mark a straight line on a selected surface, the exact length of the joists forming the chord of the truss (base line A).
2. From the center of base line A, lay out a line at right angles to it, forming a leg of a right triangle with half the length of base line A as its base and the rafter length as its hypotenuse (centerline C).
3. Nail 2 x 4 by 8-inch blocks flush with ends of base line A and centerline C.
4. Set a rafter in the jig with plate cut fitted over the jig block at one end of base line A. Peak should be flush with the centerline on the peak jig block. Use a holding block outside the rafter at point D. Repeat for other rafter at E.

5. Truss Assembly Steps:
- Lay one 2 x 4 joist or chord in place across base blocks.
- Place two 2 x 4 rafters over the joist.
- Center one end of a 1 x 6 hanger under rafter peak and center rafters against peak block.
- Nail through rafters into hanger with six 8-penny nails.
- Line up one end of chord, nail through rafter with 16-penny spikes. Repeat for other end of chord.
- Center bottom of hanger on top of chord and nail with 8-penny nails.

6. Truss Placement:
- After assembly, place trusses on the building.
- First set may be assembled in the end section or at the center as indicated in figure 2-21.
- Raise trusses by hand into position and nail to plate with 16- or 20-penny nails.
- Temporarily brace trusses to the end section of the building until sheathing is applied.

7. Installation Procedures:
- Mark proper positions of all truss assemblies on top plate, showing exact position of a given face of all rafters (south or north, etc.).
- Rest one end of a truss assembly, peak down, on an appropriate mark on the top plate on one side of the structure.
- Rest other end of truss on opposing mark on top plate on the other side of the structure.
- Rotate assembly into position using a pole or rope.
- Line up rafter faces flush against marks and secure.
- Raise, align, and nail several truss assemblies into position. Use temporary 1 x 6 braces across these assemblies as they are brought into position.
- Check rafter spacing at peaks as braces are nailed on.

8. Knee Braces:
- May be applied before or after sheathing is applied.
- Not used on every truss unless severe wind conditions are anticipated.
- Spaced as widely as 16 feet OC, as shown in figure 2-19.

9. Temporary Platforms:
- Building temporary platforms or work-benches for workers to stand on while erecting trusses will save time.
truss type rafters, rafter assembly, truss construction, roof framing, shelter building, US Army frame structures
Construct a Roof for Fagot Shacks Using Beaver-Mat or Thatch
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Beaver-mat (if available)
• Small sticks
• Straw, hay, rushes, or browse

Procedure Steps:
1. Prepare the Frame: After erecting the walls of the fagot shack, construct a frame for the roof if using thatch (as shown in...
Materials Needed:
• Beaver-mat (if available)
• Small sticks
• Straw, hay, rushes, or browse

Procedure Steps:
1. Prepare the Frame: After erecting the walls of the fagot shack, construct a frame for the roof if using thatch (as shown in Fig. 28).
2. Lay the Roof Material: Place small sticks over the frame to create a base layer.
3. Add Thatch Layer: Cover the small sticks with straw, hay, rushes, or browse to form a thick, water-shedding thatch layer (as shown in Figs. 66 and 69).
4. Secure the Roof: Ensure the roof is tightly packed and secured so it can effectively shed rain.

Additional Notes:
• A beaver-mat may be used directly over the walls for a simpler roof construction.
• This method of roofing ensures that the structure remains dry in rainy conditions.
beaver-mat, thatch roof, fagot shack roof, emergency shelter roofing
Identify and Understand Rafter Types in Roof Framing
Reference Source: US Army Frame Structures
Overview: Rafters are inclined members that form the main body of a roof framework. They function similarly to joists on floors and studs on walls.

Key Points:
• Common rafters extend from plate to ridge-board at right angles (fig....
Overview: Rafters are inclined members that form the main body of a roof framework. They function similarly to joists on floors and studs on walls.

Key Points:
• Common rafters extend from plate to ridge-board at right angles (fig. 2-12).
• Hip rafters extend diagonally from corners formed by intersection plates to the ridgeboard (fig. 2-12).
• Valley rafters extend from plates to ridgeboards along lines where roof slopes intersect (fig. 2-12).
• Jack rafters are shorter than common rafters and rest on the plate with their upper ends against hip rafters (fig. 2-12).
• Cripple jacks are nailed between hip and valley rafters (fig. 2-12).

Rafter Cuts:
• Top of plumb cut is made at the end of the rafter to be placed against the ridgeboard or opposite rafters (fig. 2-12).
• Seat, bottom, or heel cut allows the rafter to rest on the plate (fig. 2-12).
• Side or cheek cut is a bevel cut on the side of a rafter to fit it against another frame member (fig. 2-12).
• Eave or tail refers to the overhang of the rafter extending beyond the outer edge of the plate (fig. 2-12).

Rafter Line Length: Measured along the upper edge of the rafter, it is the distance from the ridge line to a point marking the intersection of the upper edge of the rafter with the building limit (fig. 2-13).
rafters, roof framing, common rafters, hip rafters, valley rafters, jack rafters, cripple jacks, rafter cuts, plumb cut, heel cut, cheek cut, eave tail, rafter line length

Primitive Shelters (10) -

Constructing a Pawnee Hogan Framework
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Short forked sticks
• Longer forked sticks
• Horizontal side-plates or purlins
• Sticks for doorway and hallway

Steps to Construct the Framework:
1. Erect the Circle of Uprights:
- Use short forked sticks to...
Materials Needed:
• Short forked sticks
• Longer forked sticks
• Horizontal side-plates or purlins
• Sticks for doorway and hallway

Steps to Construct the Framework:
1. Erect the Circle of Uprights:
- Use short forked sticks to create a circular structure.
- Set their ends firmly in the ground to ensure stability.
2. Add Vertical Supports:
- Inside this circle, erect four longer forked sticks.
3. Install Horizontal Side-Plates or Purlins:
- Place horizontal side-plates across the four vertical supports.
- These may be referred to as 'purlins' and should align with the structure of a conventional dwelling.
4. Create the Doorway and Hallway:
- Use two short-forked sticks to form the doorway.
- Connect these to the side-plates using additional sticks to create a hallway from the door to the side walls.

Additional Notes:
• The rafter poles should be placed closely together in the finished structure, as should the short sticks forming the side walls and hallway.
Pawnee Hogan, framework construction, shelter building, primitive shelters
Thatching or Covering the Roof of a Pawnee Hogan
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Browse, hay, straw, or rushes
• Sod or adobe (if applicable)
• Thatching material (e.g., bark, canvas, thatch)

Steps to Thatch or Cover the Roof:
1. Prepare the Base Layer:
- Lay browse, hay, straw, or rushes over...
Materials Needed:
• Browse, hay, straw, or rushes
• Sod or adobe (if applicable)
• Thatching material (e.g., bark, canvas, thatch)

Steps to Thatch or Cover the Roof:
1. Prepare the Base Layer:
- Lay browse, hay, straw, or rushes over the entire structure to create a thick mattress.
2. Cover with Sod or Adobe (if applicable):
- Place sod or adobe on top of the base layer.
3. Create an Opening for Ventilation and Smoke:
- Leave an opening at the top of the roof to act as a chimney for your central camp-fire (Fig. 43).

Additional Notes:
• If using bark, canvas, or thatch instead of sod or adobe, ensure it is securely fastened.
• The opening should be positioned in such a way that smoke can escape efficiently.
roof covering, Pawnee Hogan, thatching, shelter construction
Adding Windows and Improving Ventilation in a Permanent Pawnee Hogan
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Hollow log or barrel (for chimney)
• Additional sticks or materials for window frames

Steps to Add Windows and Improve Ventilation:
1. Install Windows:
- If the hogan is intended for more permanent use, add...
Materials Needed:
• Hollow log or barrel (for chimney)
• Additional sticks or materials for window frames

Steps to Add Windows and Improve Ventilation:
1. Install Windows:
- If the hogan is intended for more permanent use, add windows in the sides of the structure.
- Ensure that the windows allow sufficient light and air circulation.
2. Improve Ventilation with a Chimney:
- Use a hollow log or barrel as a chimney to improve ventilation and smoke removal.
- Position the chimney appropriately within the structure (as shown in Fig. 44).

Additional Notes:
• Windows should be constructed securely to prevent drafts or rain ingress.
• The chimney should be placed in such a way that it aligns with the central camp-fire for efficient smoke removal.
windows, ventilation, permanent shelter, Pawnee Hogan
Build a Log Shack with Bark Roofing and Insulation
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Logs for the main structure
• Bark (preferably from birch or similar trees)
• Shingles or splits for roof covering

Procedure Steps:
1. **Construct the Frame:
- Use logs as the primary structural elements, stacking...
Materials Required:
• Logs for the main structure
• Bark (preferably from birch or similar trees)
• Shingles or splits for roof covering

Procedure Steps:
1. **Construct the Frame:
- Use logs as the primary structural elements, stacking them horizontally to form walls and a base.
2. **Insulate the Sides:
- Cover the sides of the log structure with bark shingles or splits, ensuring that only the ends of the logs are visible. This provides insulation and protection from the elements.
3. **Build the Roof:
- Use additional logs or poles to create a sloped roof frame.
- Cover the roof with bark shingles or splits for waterproofing and insulation.
4. Add Details:
- Ensure that the structure is sturdy, with all joints and seams properly sealed using natural materials like tar or resin if available.

Notes:
• This method was used by fishermen and early settlers in remote areas to create warm, insulated shelters.
• The use of bark provides both insulation and a natural aesthetic.
log shack, bark roofing, primitive shelter, fisherman's hut
Clearing the Ground for Your Shelter
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 2: Clear the Ground

• Rake back forest debris to clear a spot for your shelter.
• This step helps:
- Vacate insects and other critters from the area.
- Begin drying out the dirt of your shelter floor.

This preparation is essential...
Step 2: Clear the Ground

• Rake back forest debris to clear a spot for your shelter.
• This step helps:
- Vacate insects and other critters from the area.
- Begin drying out the dirt of your shelter floor.

This preparation is essential before laying the foundation of your debris hut.
clear ground, debris hut construction, shelter preparation
Construct a Kolshian Shelter Using Tomahawk Techniques
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Timber (sawn or milled lumber)
• Splits or shakes for roofing
• Poles for securing roof
• Upright planks for walls
• Heavy planks for eaves and sill

Procedure Steps:
1. Choose the Location: Select a flat, stable...
Materials Required:
• Timber (sawn or milled lumber)
• Splits or shakes for roofing
• Poles for securing roof
• Upright planks for walls
• Heavy planks for eaves and sill

Procedure Steps:
1. Choose the Location: Select a flat, stable area for construction. Ensure it is sheltered from strong winds if possible.
2. Prepare the Foundation: Dig a shallow trench or use natural ground level to create an underground floor (as in traditional kolshian designs). This helps with insulation and stability.
3. **Construct the Frame:
- Use upright planks for the walls, set them vertically into the ground or secured with stakes.
- Place heavy planks at the eaves, running down through holes in two upright corner planks (as shown in Fig. 45).
- Secure these planks to the sill plank, which binds the upright wall planks in place.
4. **Build the Roof:
- Lay poles across the top of the structure for support.
- Cover the roof with splits or shakes, securing them in place using the poles as a base.
5. Create the Entrance: Construct a large totem-pole at the front of the building and cut a doorway into it for entry.
6. Finish the Structure: Ensure all sides are covered with hewn planks set upright. Leave only the ends of logs protruding if using bark or similar materials for insulation.

Notes:
• This design is inspired by traditional Alaskan kolshian shelters and can be built without a lumberman's axe (hence, 'tomahawk camp').
• The underground floor suggests a connection to ancient European cave dwellings.
kolshian, tomahawk camp, shelter construction, primitive building, Alaska shelters
Covering the Log Stack with Dirt
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 4: Cover with Dirt

• Cover the mound of sticks with dirt.
• This step helps:
- Keep the sticks held together firmly.
- Create a more weather-tight seal as the shelter begins to take shape.

This is an important step in stabilizing and...
Step 4: Cover with Dirt

• Cover the mound of sticks with dirt.
• This step helps:
- Keep the sticks held together firmly.
- Create a more weather-tight seal as the shelter begins to take shape.

This is an important step in stabilizing and securing your debris hut.
dirt covering, log stabilization, debris hut construction
Elevating the Ridge Pole with Logs
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 3: Elevate the Ridge Pole

• Create a small stack of logs, building them on top of each other like a pyramid.
• The ridge pole will rest on top of this stack.
• This creates room for your feet inside the shelter.

This step ensures comfort...
Step 3: Elevate the Ridge Pole

• Create a small stack of logs, building them on top of each other like a pyramid.
• The ridge pole will rest on top of this stack.
• This creates room for your feet inside the shelter.

This step ensures comfort and proper structural support for the debris hut.
ridge pole elevation, log stacking, debris hut structure
Lashing the A-Frame for Structural Support
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 5: Lashing the A-Frame

• Lash two poles together to form an A-Frame that will support the ridge pole of your debris hut.
• Options for lashing include:
- Using simple jute twine (cordage is NOT necessary).
- Propping a pole against a...
Step 5: Lashing the A-Frame

• Lash two poles together to form an A-Frame that will support the ridge pole of your debris hut.
• Options for lashing include:
- Using simple jute twine (cordage is NOT necessary).
- Propping a pole against a tree.
- Using a Y-shaped branch as an alternative.

This step provides essential structural support to your debris hut.
A-Frame lashing, debris hut structure, primitive shelter construction

Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 1: Shelter Location

• Find an area with plenty of leaves and sticks.
• Ensure the location is free from natural hazards such as:
- Flooding
- "Widow maker" limbs or trees that could fall in a storm.

This step ensures your shelter is...
Step 1: Shelter Location

• Find an area with plenty of leaves and sticks.
• Ensure the location is free from natural hazards such as:
- Flooding
- "Widow maker" limbs or trees that could fall in a storm.

This step ensures your shelter is both functional and safe.

Log House Construction (10) -

Building an Adirondack Open Log Camp with Puncheon Floor and Shingle Roof
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To build an Adirondack open log camp:

1. Use logs notched and placed like those of a log house (Figs. 162, 163, 164, 166).
2. Secure the front ends of the side logs by toe-nailing them to two upright supports (Fig. 173).
3. Flatten the inside...
To build an Adirondack open log camp:

1. Use logs notched and placed like those of a log house (Figs. 162, 163, 164, 166).
2. Secure the front ends of the side logs by toe-nailing them to two upright supports (Fig. 173).
3. Flatten the inside of the logs for a smoother finish, as was common in old Virginia and Kentucky log houses.

This method provides a durable and traditional structure suitable for outdoor living.
Adirondack log camp, puncheon floor, shingle roof, log house construction
Construct an American Totem Log House
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required: Logs, carving tools, posts, and decorative elements.

Steps to Build the Structure:
1. Design the Layout: Use Figs. 251 and 252 as a general plan for arranging logs and constructing the house.
2. Carve Totems:...
Materials Required: Logs, carving tools, posts, and decorative elements.

Steps to Build the Structure:
1. Design the Layout: Use Figs. 251 and 252 as a general plan for arranging logs and constructing the house.
2. Carve Totems: Carve the ends of all extending logs to represent the heads of reptiles, beasts, or birds.
