3. Carve Posts: Carve the posts that support the end logs on the front gallery, porch, or veranda in the form of totem-poles.
4. Add Totem-Posts: Place small totem-posts where your steps begin on the walk (Fig. 253).
5. Install a Family Totem-Pole: Add a tall totem-pole (Fig. 255) for your family or clan's totem.
6. Interior Layout: The dining-room is behind the half partition next to the kitchen; the other half of this room is open, forming a large living-room.
7. Staircase and Sleeping Areas: Construct stairs leading up to sleeping-rooms overhead. Divide the space with partitions as needed for convenience.

Additional Notes: The structure can be adjusted in size (e.g., 10 by 12 or 20 by 30 feet) depending on whether it is a camp or dwelling.
log house, totem pole, shelter construction, carving, log house design
Constructing Bunks in a Log Cabin
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To construct bunks within a log cabin:

1. Set the ends of two poles into holes bored in the logs for that purpose (Fig. 185).
2. Nail slats across the poles to form the framework of the bunk.
3. Lay a bed of browse over the slats.
4. Spread...
To construct bunks within a log cabin:

1. Set the ends of two poles into holes bored in the logs for that purpose (Fig. 185).
2. Nail slats across the poles to form the framework of the bunk.
3. Lay a bed of browse over the slats.
4. Spread blankets on top of the browse for sleeping.

This method provides a simple and effective way to create sleeping accommodations within a log cabin.
log cabin bunks, pole framework, browse bed, log house construction
Constructing a Permanent Log House Using Peeled Logs
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Constructing a Permanent Log House with Peeled Logs

1. Use peeled logs only: For any structure intended to be permanent, never use logs with bark on them. Use _peeled_ logs instead.
2. Prepare the foundation: Lay up the...
Procedure for Constructing a Permanent Log House with Peeled Logs

1. Use peeled logs only: For any structure intended to be permanent, never use logs with bark on them. Use _peeled_ logs instead.
2. Prepare the foundation: Lay up the pen of this house as already described and illustrated by Figs. 229, 233, etc.
3. Build walls: Construct the sides and front walls until they reach the desired height.
4. Frame the roof: Frame your roof after the manner shown in Fig. 49 or any other method that suits your preference or convenience. In this case, use the Susitna form for the end plates.
5. Create end plates: Make the end plates by first severing the root of a tree and leaving an elbow or bend at the end of the trunk (Fig. 264). Flatten the elbow by scoring and hewing as described under the heading of the Susitna house.
6. Carve grotesque heads: Carve the elbows at the terminals of the end plate to represent grotesque heads (Fig. 253).
7. Ensure quality construction: The house should be built very carefully, even though it is made of rude material. Handle the materials in a neat and workmanlike manner.
8. Use straight logs for walls: Only use the straightest logs available for the walls of the house.
9. Supports for piazza: If there is a wide front to the house, additional supports may be needed for the piazza. With a narrow front, log puncheons will be sufficiently stiff to support themselves.
10. Compare with Wyoming olebo: The house should resemble the Wyoming olebo (Fig. 236) but differ in that it is a two-story house built on the American log-cabin plan, with logs continued up to the top of the gable as seen in the Olympic (Fig. 240).
peeled logs, log house construction, shelter building, permanent structure, roof framing, end plates, Susitna form, Wyoming olebo
Create a Miniature Model for Planning a Log House
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required: Jack-knife, small sticks (to represent logs), glue.

Steps to Build the Model:
1. Scale Down Measurements: Use a scale where an inch or half-inch represents a foot. Measure all sticks accordingly.
2. **Build a Toy...
Materials Required: Jack-knife, small sticks (to represent logs), glue.

Steps to Build the Model:
1. Scale Down Measurements: Use a scale where an inch or half-inch represents a foot. Measure all sticks accordingly.
2. Build a Toy House: Sit on the ground or floor and experiment with building a toy house or miniature model until you are satisfied with the design.
3. Assemble the Model: Glue the little logs of the pen together, ensuring that the roof can be removed and reattached like the lid of a box.
4. Use as Reference: Keep your model to use in place of an architect's drawing; it will help backwoods workmen understand the design better than traditional plans or sections on paper.

Additional Notes: The model allows for flexibility in adjusting the layout, such as placing the kitchen at the back of the house instead of on one side.
model building, log house planning, miniature model, shelter design
Log-Rolling Using Hand Power
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To roll logs using hand power:

1. Securely fasten the ends of two ropes inside the cabin.
2. Pass the free ends of the ropes around the log, first under it and then over the top of it.
3. Bring the free ends of the ropes up to a group of...
To roll logs using hand power:

1. Securely fasten the ends of two ropes inside the cabin.
2. Pass the free ends of the ropes around the log, first under it and then over the top of it.
3. Bring the free ends of the ropes up to a group of men.
4. By pulling on the free ends, the men roll the log up to the top of the cabin.

This method was commonly used by early pioneers without the use of tools or animals.
log-rolling, hand power, log house construction, rope technique
Log-Rolling Using Oxen and Pulley System
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To roll logs using oxen and a pulley system:

1. Fasten a chain to each end of the log.
2. Attach a pulley-block to the side of the cabin opposite the skids.
3. Run the rope through the pulley-block to the oxen, similar to how it is run to men...
To roll logs using oxen and a pulley system:

1. Fasten a chain to each end of the log.
2. Attach a pulley-block to the side of the cabin opposite the skids.
3. Run the rope through the pulley-block to the oxen, similar to how it is run to men in Fig. 182.
4. When the oxen are started, the log slides up the skids to the loose rafters _N_, _O_, _P_.
5. Once the log reaches the rafters, it can be easily shoved and fitted into place.

This method was used when oxen were available for assistance in moving heavy logs.
oxen, pulley system, log rolling, log house construction
Pit-Sawing Technique for Log Work
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Logs, saws (two-handed), planks, temporary supports.

Steps to Pit-Saw a Log:
1. Position the Log: Place the log on a stable surface or in a pit if available, ensuring it is secure and level.
2. Use Two-Handed Saw:...
Materials Needed: Logs, saws (two-handed), planks, temporary supports.

Steps to Pit-Saw a Log:
1. Position the Log: Place the log on a stable surface or in a pit if available, ensuring it is secure and level.
2. Use Two-Handed Saw: Use a two-handed saw (as shown in Fig. 175) for cutting through logs.
3. Work with Assistant: Have another person assist by holding the other end of the saw to ensure even pressure and control during the cutting process.
4. Cut Along Desired Line: Work the saw back and forth along the desired line, maintaining a steady rhythm and applying consistent force.
5. Secure Log if Necessary: If the log is particularly large or difficult to cut, use temporary supports or braces to stabilize it during the process.

Notes: Pit-sawing is a traditional method used for cutting logs into planks or other shapes. It can be physically demanding but is an effective technique when done properly.
pit-sawing, log work, two-handed saw, cutting logs, assistant required
Tree Felling with Notching Technique
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Tree to be felled, axe, saw (two-handed), temporary supports.

Steps to Fell a Tree Safely:
1. Notch the Tree with an Axe: Begin by notching the tree with an axe at the desired point where you want it to fall. This...
Materials Needed: Tree to be felled, axe, saw (two-handed), temporary supports.

Steps to Fell a Tree Safely:
1. Notch the Tree with an Axe: Begin by notching the tree with an axe at the desired point where you want it to fall. This notch should be on the side opposite to the direction in which you want the tree to fall.
2. Use Two-Handed Saw for Final Cut: After notching, use a two-handed saw (as shown in Fig. 176) to make the final cut through the tree trunk.
3. Ensure Proper Direction of Fall: Make sure that the notch and subsequent cuts are made so that the tree will fall in the intended direction, avoiding obstacles or structures.
4. Use Temporary Supports if Needed: If the tree is large or unstable, use temporary supports to help guide its fall and prevent it from toppling unpredictably.

Notes: This method was commonly used in early logging practices and is still applicable today for controlled felling of trees.
tree felling, notching technique, axe use, two-handed saw, temporary supports
Constructing Log Steps for a Log House
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To construct front steps for a log house:

• Use flattened logs or puncheons, as shown in Fig. 183.
• Ensure the steps are securely attached to the structure of the house.
• The design should allow for safe and stable access to the entrance...
To construct front steps for a log house:

• Use flattened logs or puncheons, as shown in Fig. 183.
• Ensure the steps are securely attached to the structure of the house.
• The design should allow for safe and stable access to the entrance of the log house.

This method provides a functional and traditional way to add front steps to a log house.
log steps, puncheons, log house construction

Construction Methods (9) -

Constructing a Sandbag Revetment Wall
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Steps to construct a sandbag revetment wall:

1. Fill the bags: Fill each bag about three-fourths full with earth or a dry soil-cement mixture and tie the choke cords.
2. Tuck in corners: After filling, tuck in the bottom corners of the...
Steps to construct a sandbag revetment wall:

1. Fill the bags: Fill each bag about three-fourths full with earth or a dry soil-cement mixture and tie the choke cords.
2. Tuck in corners: After filling, tuck in the bottom corners of the bags.
3. Bottom row construction: Place all bags as headers for the bottom row of the revetment wall.
4. Alternate rows: Build using alternate rows of stretchers and headers with joints broken between courses. The top row consists of headers.
5. Positioning layers: Position the sandbags so that the planes between the layers have the same pitch as the base (at right angles to the slope of the revetment).
6. Orientation: Place all bags so that side seams on stretchers and choked ends on headers are turned toward the revetted face.
7. Backfilling: As the revetment is built, backfill it to shape the revetted face to a slope of 1:4 (the face must lean against the earth it is to hold in place). The base for the revetment must be on firm ground and dug at a slope of 4:1.

Note: A sandbag revetment will not stand with a vertical face. It requires a sloped face.
sandbag revetment,headers,stretchers,joints,breaking courses,backfilling,slope requirements
Constructing Protective Shelters and Fighting Bunkers
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Protective shelters are typically constructed aboveground using cavity wall revetments and earth-covered roof structures, or below ground using airtransportable sections. They serve as command posts, observation posts, medical aid stations,...
Protective shelters are typically constructed aboveground using cavity wall revetments and earth-covered roof structures, or below ground using airtransportable sections. They serve as command posts, observation posts, medical aid stations, supply and ammunition shelters, and sleeping or resting shelters.

Fighting bunkers are enlarged fighting positions designed for squad-size units or larger. They can be built aboveground or below ground and are usually made of concrete. Some are prefabricated and transported forward to the battle area by trucks or air.

If constructed properly with appropriate collective protection equipment, shelters and bunkers can provide protection against chemical and biological agents.
protective shelters,fighting bunkers,construction methods,command posts,observation posts,medical aid stations,supply shelters,sleeping shelters
Sandbag Construction Methods for Fighting and Protective Positions
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Sandbags are used to build walls of fighting and protective positions in a manner similar to bricks. They are also useful for retaining wall revetments.

• Material: Sandbags are made of acrylic fabric, which is rot and weather resistant....
Sandbags are used to build walls of fighting and protective positions in a manner similar to bricks. They are also useful for retaining wall revetments.

• Material: Sandbags are made of acrylic fabric, which is rot and weather resistant. They have a life of at least 2 years with no visible deterioration under all climatic conditions. Older-style cotton bags deteriorate much sooner.

• Filling: To prolong the useful life of sandbags, fill them with a mixture of dry earth and portland cement in the ratio of 1 part cement to 10 parts dry earth. Alternatively, use a 1:6 ratio for sand-gravel mixtures. Filled bags can also be dipped in a cement-water slurry.

• Stabilization: After filling, each sandbag is pounded with a flat object (e.g., a 2 by 4) to make the retaining wall more stable.
sandbag construction,acrylic fabric,cement-earth mixture,sand-gravel mix,stabilization,pounding sandbags
Using Funnel Systems for Filling Sandbags
Standard Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
When the requirement for filled sandbags exceeds the capabilities of soldiers using only shovels, it is more efficient to fill bags from a stockpile using a lumber or steel funnel as shown in illustrations. This method allows for faster and...
When the requirement for filled sandbags exceeds the capabilities of soldiers using only shovels, it is more efficient to fill bags from a stockpile using a lumber or steel funnel as shown in illustrations. This method allows for faster and easier filling of large quantities of sandbags.
funnel system,sandbag filling,stockpile,lumber funnel,steel funnel,equipment efficiency
Using Hand Tools for Position Construction
Standard Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Individual soldiers carry entrenching tools and have access to picks, shovels, machetes, and hand carpentry tools for excavation and vertical construction work.

Earthmoving equipment, such as backhoes, bulldozers, and bucket loaders, is used...
Individual soldiers carry entrenching tools and have access to picks, shovels, machetes, and hand carpentry tools for excavation and vertical construction work.

Earthmoving equipment, such as backhoes, bulldozers, and bucket loaders, is used for excavating protective positions for vehicles and supplies. These machines are typically used for larger or more rapid excavation when the situation permits. However, they cannot dig out the exact shape desired or remove the amount of earth necessary, so manual completion of the excavation is usually required.
hand tools,entrenching tool,picks,shovels,machetes,carpentry tools,earthmoving equipment,bulldozers,backhoes
Built-in-Place Erection Procedures
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Overview: When using the built-in-place method, the officer in charge of construction divides the men into working parties with specific duties:

1. Laying out the foundation
2. Grading and excavating
3. **Laying out and cutting various...
Overview: When using the built-in-place method, the officer in charge of construction divides the men into working parties with specific duties:

1. Laying out the foundation
2. Grading and excavating
3. Laying out and cutting various sizes of material

If a party completes its task before the building is completed, it is assigned a new task. For example, if the party laying out the foundation completes its work before erection of the building is begun, it may be assigned a new duty such as cutting rafters.

Order of Construction: Parts are built in the following order:
• Footings
• Piers
• Sills
• Joists
• Floor
• Sole
• Studs
• Plates
• Girts
• Rafters
• Bracing
• Siding
• Sheathing
• Roofing
• Doors
• Windows
• Steps
• Inside finish (if used)
built in place, shelter construction, foundation laying, order of construction
Cutting and Assembling Panels
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Overview: The number and size of each piece in a section is given to the man in charge of the cutting party.

Steps for Cutting Timber:
1. Cut the timber to the correct length with a handsaw or power saw.
2. Measure the length using a square...
Overview: The number and size of each piece in a section is given to the man in charge of the cutting party.

Steps for Cutting Timber:
1. Cut the timber to the correct length with a handsaw or power saw.
2. Measure the length using a square and tape.
3. After one piece has been cut, it may be used as a pattern for marking the remaining pieces.
4. Set up the pattern by nailing two blocks to the piece of correct size, one near each end, as shown in figure 5-2. These blocks act as stops to hold the pattern in place on the timber to be marked.

Multiple Crews: Several cutting and assembling parties may be used at one time on different types of sections.
cutting timber, panel assembly, construction techniques, pattern marking
Panel Method Erection Procedures
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Overview: The panel method (preassembly method) requires careful planning before the actual construction. Most Army buildings are now built by this method as follows:

1. Determine Section Sizes and Numbers: Before measuring and cutting...
Overview: The panel method (preassembly method) requires careful planning before the actual construction. Most Army buildings are now built by this method as follows:

1. Determine Section Sizes and Numbers: Before measuring and cutting lumber, the number and size of sections that are alike should be determined from the blueprint. This ensures the correct numbers of each piece.
2. Assign Crews for Cutting and Assembling: The carpenter assigns a crew to cut and assemble one section. In most cases, a template is built as a guide for assembling the section. It should be built square, correct in size, and level.
3. Use Standard Panels: Most TO construction now built by the panel method uses the basic 8 foot by 10 foot panel (fig. 51) with special door panels, window panels, and modified panels. Modified panels are smaller by the actual width of a 2 x 4 and are used as end panels.

Additional Details: Detailed drawings of this type of panel construction including roof and gable panel are included in TM 5-302.
panel method, preassembly method, shelter construction, standard panels, blueprint planning
Selecting a Building Erection Method
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Overview: The method of erecting buildings directly affects the time, labor, and material needed. There are two main types of erection methods:

• Built in place: Each piece is separately erected in its proper place.
• Panel method: A...
Overview: The method of erecting buildings directly affects the time, labor, and material needed. There are two main types of erection methods:

• Built in place: Each piece is separately erected in its proper place.
• Panel method: A complete section is built as a unit and then set in the building in the proper place. This method is used extensively because it makes for greater speed, better control over working parties, and better use of manpower. It also allows the use of a standard list of sizes for each similar section; standard plans shown in TM 5-302 further simplify construction.

Considerations: When selecting an erection method, factors such as construction plant layout, distribution of material, number of skilled and semiskilled men available, and number and type of units to be constructed must be considered. Planning permits an orderly series of operations and prevents duplication of effort and waste of material.
building erection, construction methods, panel method, built in place, shelter construction

Tent Construction (9) -

Constructing a Combination Tent Using Triangular Shape (Fig. 10)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• A single piece of canvas
• Coarse seam or tapes for fastening

Procedure:
1. Cut the canvas into an equal-sided triangle, with one side designated as the door opening.
2. Ensure there is no waste cloth by...
Materials Required:
• A single piece of canvas
• Coarse seam or tapes for fastening

Procedure:
1. Cut the canvas into an equal-sided triangle, with one side designated as the door opening.
2. Ensure there is no waste cloth by carefully measuring and cutting the fabric to fit the triangular shape.
3. Fasten the two sides (Nos. 1 and 2) together using a coarse seam or tapes for secure attachment.
4. The resulting structure will form a simple, efficient tent with one side open as the entrance.

Notes:
• This design is ideal for lightweight camping due to its minimal material usage.
• Ensure that the seams are strong enough to withstand wind and weather.
tent construction, triangular tent, canvas sewing, scout shelter
Creating a Conical Canopy Tent (Fig. 11)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• Canvas
• Small-size gas pipe or limber poles
• Guy lines (optional)

Procedure:
1. Take the triangular tent design from Fig. 10 and modify it into a conical shape, forming a canopy.
2. Use **small-size gas pipe or...
Materials Required:
• Canvas
• Small-size gas pipe or limber poles
• Guy lines (optional)

Procedure:
1. Take the triangular tent design from Fig. 10 and modify it into a conical shape, forming a canopy.
2. Use small-size gas pipe or limber poles to create a large hoop, which will be used to secure the circular shape of the canopy.
3. Secure the hoop by attaching it to the edges of the canvas.
4. If using guy lines, ensure they are attached at strategic points for stability; however, note that guy lines may interfere with the structure and should be used cautiously.
5. A small addition of material can transform this canopy into a Sibley tent by adding walls to enclose the space.

Notes:
• This design is suitable for use as an awning or temporary shelter in open areas.
• The hoop provides structural support and helps maintain the shape of the canopy.
conical tent, canvas canopy, scout shelter design
Laying Out Rings for Tent Construction (Fig. 12)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• Canvas
• Marking tools (e.g., chalk or pins)

Procedure:
1. Lay out the canvas on a flat surface.
2. Use radial lines to divide a semi-circle into eight equal angles of 22-1/2 degrees each, creating reference...
Materials Required:
• Canvas
• Marking tools (e.g., chalk or pins)

Procedure:
1. Lay out the canvas on a flat surface.
2. Use radial lines to divide a semi-circle into eight equal angles of 22-1/2 degrees each, creating reference points for ring placement.
3. Mark these radial lines and use them as guides to locate all necessary loops and rings based on the diagram in Fig. 12.
4. Ensure that the layout is consistent with the dimensions provided in the accompanying table for different tent designs (Fig. 1-11).
5. Each ring should be labeled according to its corresponding figure number for clarity during construction.

Notes:
• This method ensures uniformity and accuracy when constructing multiple types of tents from a single piece of canvas.
• The layout is scalable, meaning it can be applied to any size of material.
tent ring layout, scout canvas marking, shelter construction planning
Preparing Canvas for Tent Construction (Fig. 12)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• Canvas
• Safety pins or temporary fasteners
• Guy lines with hooks and slides
• Tape or double hemming tools

Procedure:
1. If unsure about the placement of rings on your canvas, suspend the tent by its center ring...
Materials Required:
• Canvas
• Safety pins or temporary fasteners
• Guy lines with hooks and slides
• Tape or double hemming tools

Procedure:
1. If unsure about the placement of rings on your canvas, suspend the tent by its center ring to test the shape.
2. Temporarily secure the loops using safety pins to allow for adjustments before finalizing the structure.
3. Ensure that all guy lines have hooks or snaps at one end and slides at the other end for easy attachment and adjustment.
4. The edge of the canvas where the large ring is fastened should be bound with tape or hemmed twice to reinforce it, especially since this area forms the door in most tents.

Notes:
• This preparation step ensures that the tent is both functional and durable.
• Reinforcing the edges helps prevent tearing and extends the life of the canvas.
canvas preparation, scout tent durability, temporary fastening methods
Tent Dimensions for 90-Inch Wide Canvas (Table of Dimensions)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• A single piece of canvas measuring 90 inches wide by 15 feet long

Procedure:
Use the following table to determine dimensions for various tent shapes:

|Size|Dimensions|Area, Sq. Ft.|Height,...
Materials Required:
• A single piece of canvas measuring 90 inches wide by 15 feet long

Procedure:
Use the following table to determine dimensions for various tent shapes:

|Size|Dimensions|Area, Sq. Ft.|Height, Ft.|Remarks|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|1|7-1/2 ft. triangle|25|6-1/4|One side open|
|2|6-1/2 X 15 ft.|65|6-1/4|One side open|
|3|6 x 7-1/2 ft.|45|4-1/2|One side open|
|4|7-1/2 x 8 ft.|60|5-1/2|One side open|
|5|7-1/2 ft. triangle|25|7-1/2|One side open|
|6|6-1/4 x 6-1/4 ft.|39|7|Enclosed|
|7|7-1/2 ft. diam.|44|6-1/2|Enclosed|
|8|5 x 7-1/2 ft.|37-1/2|6-1/2|2-1/2 ft. wall|
|9|7-1/2 x 8 ft.|60|6-1/2|No.8, with fly|
|10|15 ft. triangle|97|6-1/4|Enclosed|
|11|11-1/4 ft. circle|108|5|Canopy, no sides|

Notes:
• These dimensions are scalable and can be adjusted based on the size of your canvas.
• The table provides a variety of options for different camping needs, including enclosed tents, open-sided shelters, and canopies.
tent dimensions, scout canvas measurements, shelter design table
Using a Single Piece of Canvas to Make Ten Different Tents (Fig. 12)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• A single piece of canvas measuring 90 inches wide by 15 feet long

Procedure:
1. Use the layout diagram in Fig. 12 as a guide for marking and cutting the canvas.
2. The dimensions provided in the table can be used...
Materials Required:
• A single piece of canvas measuring 90 inches wide by 15 feet long

Procedure:
1. Use the layout diagram in Fig. 12 as a guide for marking and cutting the canvas.
2. The dimensions provided in the table can be used to create ten different tent shapes, including triangles, rectangles, and circular designs.
3. Ensure that each design is cut according to its specified area, height, and remarks (e.g., enclosed or open sides).
4. Use the marked rings and loops as reference points for assembling each type of tent.
5. Adjust fastenings as needed by temporarily securing with safety pins before finalizing the structure.

Notes:
• This method is highly efficient, allowing for maximum use of a single piece of canvas.
• The versatility of this approach makes it ideal for scouts preparing for various camping scenarios.
single canvas tent making, scout shelter versatility, multiple tent designs
Construct an Open Outing Tent from Canvas
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• 13 yards of 8 oz. duck canvas
• Scissors or cutting tool
• Ruler or measuring tape
• Pins
• Sewing needle and thread
• Hemming tools

Steps to Construct the Tent:
1. Purchase Canvas: Obtain 13 yards of 8 oz. duck...
Materials Required:
• 13 yards of 8 oz. duck canvas
• Scissors or cutting tool
• Ruler or measuring tape
• Pins
• Sewing needle and thread
• Hemming tools

Steps to Construct the Tent:
1. Purchase Canvas: Obtain 13 yards of 8 oz. duck canvas from a department store or dry goods store at approximately seventeen or eighteen cents per yard, totaling $2.21.
2. Lay Out Canvas: Place the strip of canvas on a flat surface and cut one end square.
3. Mark Measurements for First Gore:
- Measure up 8 inches along one edge from the cut corner and draw a line to the opposite corner.
- From this corner, measure out 7 ft. 8 in. along the edge on one side and 5 ft. 9 in. on the other side from the end of your 8-inch measurement.
4. Cut First Gore: Use a ruler to draw another diagonal across the canvas at the ends of these measurements. Cut this section out; this is the first gore of your tent.
5. Create Corresponding Gore for Other Side:
- Turn the gore over and lay it down on the strip of canvas so as to measure off another one exactly like it. This will be the corresponding gore for the other side of the tent.
6. Make Second Pair of Gores:
- On the remaining strip of canvas, measure 5 ft. 9 in. along one edge from the pointed end and 3 ft. 10 in. on the opposite edge.
- Join these points with a diagonal to create the second gore. Use this as a pattern to cut out another duplicate by reversing it and laying it down on the strip of canvas.
7. Make Third Gore:
- On one edge of your remaining strip, measure 3 ft. 10 in. from the point, and on the other side, measure 1 ft. 11 in.
- Draw a diagonal across these measurements to create the third gore.
8. Use Leftover Canvas for Back Triangle:
- From the leftover piece of canvas (approximately two feet long), cut out a triangle with sides measuring 1 ft. 11 in.
9. Assemble Tent Pieces:
- Pin your three gores together to form one side of the tent, as shown in the illustrations.
- Pin the other two sides together along the ridge pole area.
10. Sew and Hem Edges:
- Sew all the pieces together securely.
- Sew in the back triangle and hem all around the edges.
11. Create Ridge Pole Opening:
- Leave a small hole at the peak of the little triangle through which the ridge pole must pass.

Notes:
• Ensure that the canvas is cut precisely to match the measurements provided.
• Use strong thread and secure stitching to ensure durability in outdoor conditions.
canvas tent, open outing tent, gore cutting, shelter construction, Boy Scouts, camping
Constructing a Rectangle Tent for Additional Space and Comfort
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• A rectangular piece of canvas or cloth equal to two squares (e.g., 7x14 or 9x18 sheet)

Steps to Construct the Tent:
1. Select the Material: Choose a rectangular piece of canvas or cloth that is twice the size of a...
Materials Required:
• A rectangular piece of canvas or cloth equal to two squares (e.g., 7x14 or 9x18 sheet)

Steps to Construct the Tent:
1. Select the Material: Choose a rectangular piece of canvas or cloth that is twice the size of a square (e.g., 7x14 or 9x18). This will provide more space and comfort.
2. Prepare the Canvas: Ensure the material is clean and free from any damage. No cutting is required; simply use the canvas as-is.
3. Attach Suspension Ring (Optional): If desired, fasten a suspension ring at the center of one side for hanging the tent from a support such as a tree or pole. The ring should be 1-1/2 inches or 2 inches in diameter and well-reinforced to prevent tearing out.
4. Hang the Tent: Hang the rectangle by one corner from any convenient support (e.g., a tree, pole, or rope). This will create a roomy and warm shelter with part of one side open toward the fire.
5. Secure the Tent: If needed, use pegs and guy lines to secure the tent to the ground for stability.

Additional Notes:
• Reinforce the canvas along the lines of strain from peg to pole if needed.
• If your material is large, additional pegs and guy lines may be required for stability.
rectangle,tent,construction,space,comfort,boy scout handbook
Constructing a Simple Tent from a Square of Canvas
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Required:
• A square piece of canvas or cloth (7x7 sheet for one person, 9x9 for two)

Steps to Construct the Tent:
1. Select the Material: Choose a square of canvas or cloth that is large enough to accommodate your needs. A...
Materials Required:
• A square piece of canvas or cloth (7x7 sheet for one person, 9x9 for two)

Steps to Construct the Tent:
1. Select the Material: Choose a square of canvas or cloth that is large enough to accommodate your needs. A 7x7 sheet is suitable for a single person, while a 9x9 sheet can comfortably house two people.
2. Prepare the Canvas: Ensure the material is clean and free from any damage. No cutting is required; simply use the canvas as-is.
3. Attach Suspension Ring (Optional): If desired, fasten a suspension ring at the center of one side for hanging the tent from a support such as a tree or pole. The ring should be 1-1/2 inches or 2 inches in diameter and well-reinforced to prevent tearing out.
4. Hang the Tent: Hang the square by one corner from any convenient support (e.g., a tree, pole, or rope). This will create a comfortable shelter that sheds rain and reflects heat.
5. Use as a Fly or Ground Cloth: The square can also be used as a tent fly or ground cloth for other tents.

Additional Notes:
• Reinforce the canvas along the lines of strain from peg to pole if needed.
• If your material is large, additional pegs and guy lines may be required for stability.
canvas,tent,construction,square,shelter,boy scout handbook

Cold Weather Sheltering (8) -

Constructing Shelters in Wooded Areas - Specific Techniques
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with sufficient timber and natural materials for construction.
2. Select the appropriate shelter type based on available resources and environmental conditions (e.g., single lean-to, double lean-to, wigwam,...
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with sufficient timber and natural materials for construction.
2. Select the appropriate shelter type based on available resources and environmental conditions (e.g., single lean-to, double lean-to, wigwam, tree-pit shelter, or chopped/fallen tree).
3. Gather materials such as branches, leaves, bark, and other natural resources to construct the framework and insulation of the shelter.
4. Construct the shelter using the following steps:
- Single lean-to: Use a single pole or branch for support, then cover it with branches, leaves, or fabric for insulation.
- Double lean-to: Build two leaning structures that meet at the top to form a more stable and insulated shelter.
- Wigwam: Create a dome-like structure using multiple poles tied together at the top, then cover with natural materials such as leaves, bark, or snow for insulation.
- Tree-pit shelter: Dig around the base of a tree and use it to support the structure. Cover the pit with branches, leaves, or fabric for insulation.
- Chopped or fallen tree: Use an existing fallen tree or cut down a tree to create a natural framework that can be covered with materials for insulation.
5. Ensure the shelter provides adequate protection from wind, cold, and precipitation.
6. Check the stability of the structure regularly and make adjustments as needed.
wooded area shelter construction, lean-to building, wigwam technique, tree-pit shelter, chopped tree shelter
Types of Shelters in Wooded and Open Areas
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Identify the appropriate shelter type based on the environment:
- In wooded areas, consider constructing one of the following:
- Single lean-to: A simple structure using a single pole or branch for support.
-...
Procedure:

1. Identify the appropriate shelter type based on the environment:
- In wooded areas, consider constructing one of the following:
- Single lean-to: A simple structure using a single pole or branch for support.
- Double lean-to: Two leaning structures joined together to form a more stable and insulated shelter.
- Wigwam: A dome-like structure made from branches and covered with natural materials such as leaves, bark, or snow.
- Tree-pit shelter: A shelter built around the base of a tree, using the trunk for support and insulation.
- Chopped or fallen tree: Use a fallen tree or cut a tree to create a natural structure that can be covered with materials for insulation.
- In open country or barrens, consider constructing one of the following:
- Tent roof shelter: A simple frame covered with canvas, tarps, or other available material to mimic a tent.
- Snow trench: Dig a shallow trench in snow and cover it with branches or fabric for insulation and protection from wind.
- Snow house: Construct a dome-shaped structure using compacted snow as the primary building material.
- Snow wall: Build a barrier of snow to block wind and provide some level of insulation.
- Snow cave: Dig into a snowbank or drift to create an insulated shelter that can protect against extreme cold.
wooded area shelters, open country shelters, barrens shelters, lean-to, wigwam, snow house, snow trench, shelter types
Building Shelters in Cold Weather - General Guidelines
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand that shelter construction is not based on strict rules but should be adapted to the environment and available materials.
2. Use only tools and equipment typically issued to a tent group for building shelters.
3....
Procedure:

1. Understand that shelter construction is not based on strict rules but should be adapted to the environment and available materials.
2. Use only tools and equipment typically issued to a tent group for building shelters.
3. Consider the following factors when constructing a shelter:
- Type and condition of terrain: Choose flat or stable ground where possible.
- Availability of natural materials: Use timber, branches, leaves, snow, or other available resources.
- Individual imagination and initiative: Be creative in adapting construction methods to the situation.
4. Follow general measurements as a guide but adjust based on environmental conditions and material availability.
5. Ensure that the shelter provides adequate protection from wind, cold, and precipitation.
shelter construction, cold weather building, improvised shelter, terrain conditions, material availability
Optimal Tent Orientation and Vestibule Use in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Install the vestibule only at one end of the tent, facing the prevailing wind, to reduce heat loss when the door is frequently used.
2. Avoid using a vestibule on the more leeward end where drift will accumulate.
3. Ensure...
Procedure:

1. Install the vestibule only at one end of the tent, facing the prevailing wind, to reduce heat loss when the door is frequently used.
2. Avoid using a vestibule on the more leeward end where drift will accumulate.
3. Ensure that rapid exit in case of fire or other emergency is assured by proper vestibule placement.
4. In areas with severe winds, site the tent crosswise to the anticipated wind direction since the curved roof tolerates wind load better than flat ends, reducing buffeting.
5. Do not use a vestibule on a tent intended for aid station use because standard litters cannot negotiate the right angle turn required in the short vestibule.
tent orientation, wind protection, vestibule placement, aid station tents
Pitching Cold Weather Tents in Snow or Frozen Ground
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Clear snow from the ground surface to obtain a lower silhouette and gain advantage of warmer ground temperatures.
2. Place coniferous boughs or similar material on the ground for insulation and comfort.
3. If clearing snow...
Procedure:

1. Clear snow from the ground surface to obtain a lower silhouette and gain advantage of warmer ground temperatures.
2. Place coniferous boughs or similar material on the ground for insulation and comfort.
3. If clearing snow is impractical, pack the snow with skis or snowshoes until a firm base is provided for pitching the tent.
4. Place the tent pole on a log or other suitable support to keep it from sinking into the snow.
5. Provide support for the stove under similar conditions.
6. In open terrain with strong wind, build a snow wall on the windward side of the tent to protect it from wind and make heating easier.
7. Pitch the tent with the entrance 45° downwind (see figure 3-5). Variable winds may require construction of a windbreak at the entrance.
8. In high winds, anchor the tent securely using natural anchors or by digging suitable holes in the snow for deadmen.
9. Place snow cloth flat on the ground outside the tent, and use stones, logs, or other heavy objects on top of the snow cloth to assist in anchoring the tent.
10. If natural anchors are not available, dig a hole into the snow large enough to insert a pole or log approximately one meter (3') long with the tent line attached. Fill the hole with snow and pack it well. In time, the packed snow will freeze and securely anchor the tent.
11. Avoid driving metal pins into frozen or rocky ground when excessive force is required.
12. On rocky ground, tie tent lines around heavy rocks and weight them down with other stones.
13. When pitching on ice:
- If the ice thickness is not excessive, chop a small hole through the ice.
- Use a short stick or pole with a piece of rope or wire tied in the middle to push through the hole and turn it across underneath the ice (see figure 3-6c).
- For very thick ice, cut a hole 30 to 60 cm (1' to 2') deep, insert a deadman, and fill the hole with slush or water. When frozen, this provides an excellent anchor point.
- Fasten a piece of rope or wire to the tent line after securing the deadman to prevent accidental damage when removing it from the ice.
pitching tents, cold weather sheltering, snow camping, tent anchoring, frozen ground, wind protection, deadman anchor
Striking Cold Weather Tents in Winter Conditions
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. When striking the tent in winter, remove snow and ice that has accumulated on the tent to prevent it from doubling in weight.
2. Remove snow and ice by shaking the tent or beating it with a mitten or stick.
3. If the snow...
Procedure:

1. When striking the tent in winter, remove snow and ice that has accumulated on the tent to prevent it from doubling in weight.
2. Remove snow and ice by shaking the tent or beating it with a mitten or stick.
3. If the snow cloth is frozen to the ground:
- Carefully remove the snow and ice around it using chopping or shoveling to avoid damaging the material.
- Use a shovel to ease between the cloth and the ground, gently prying the cloth away from the ice.
4. Ensure that all materials are removed carefully to prevent damage to the tent fabric.
striking tents, snow removal, ice removal, winter shelter maintenance
Construct a Two-Man Poncho Shelter
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Materials Required:
• 2 ponchos
• Stakes or short pieces of rope
• Forked sticks (optional)
• A third poncho (optional, for ground cloth)

Steps to Construct the Shelter:
1. Lay Ponchos Flat: Spread two ponchos on the ground with their...
Materials Required:
• 2 ponchos
• Stakes or short pieces of rope
• Forked sticks (optional)
• A third poncho (optional, for ground cloth)

Steps to Construct the Shelter:
1. Lay Ponchos Flat: Spread two ponchos on the ground with their hood sides up and long sides together.
2. Secure Hood Openings: Adjust and tie the hood drawstrings tightly on both ponchos to close the hood openings completely.
3. Join the Ponchos: Fasten the snap fastener studs of one poncho to the snap fastener sockets of the other poncho at the point where they are joined.
4. Form a Ridge: Raise the joined portion of the ponchos to create a ridge.
5. Secure with Ropes and Forked Sticks: Attach ropes to grommets at the ends of the ridge, run them over forked sticks, and secure them on the other side.
6. Stake Out the Shelter: Stake out the corners and sides of the combined ponchos as described in the one-man shelter procedure.
7. Add a Ground Cloth (Optional): A third poncho may be snapped into the other ponchos to form an additional ground cloth for added protection.
two-man shelter, poncho shelter, cold weather shelter, military shelter, emergency shelter
Selecting Appropriate Shelter Locations in Cold Weather
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Identify suitable areas for building shelters based on environmental conditions:
- Wooded areas: Preferred location due to availability of timber, wood for fires and heat, cover from air and ground observation, and natural...
Procedure:

1. Identify suitable areas for building shelters based on environmental conditions:
- Wooded areas: Preferred location due to availability of timber, wood for fires and heat, cover from air and ground observation, and natural windbreak.
- Open country: Can be used but may require more effort to construct shelter and provide protection from the elements.
- Barren areas: Limited materials available; only snow can be used for construction. This is less ideal due to lack of resources for building and insulation.

2. Consider factors such as terrain, availability of natural materials, and wind direction when selecting a location.
3. Avoid exposed or elevated positions that increase exposure to cold winds and reduce protection from the elements.
wooded areas, open country, barren areas, shelter location, cold weather survival, windbreak, terrain selection

Debris Hut Construction (8) -

Avoiding the Need to Exit a Debris Hut During Cold Weather
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Objective: Minimize the need to exit the debris hut during cold nights to prevent heat loss.

Procedure:
1. Plan ahead and use the bathroom before entering the debris hut for the night.
2. If it is necessary to leave the shelter, do so only...
Objective: Minimize the need to exit the debris hut during cold nights to prevent heat loss.

Procedure:
1. Plan ahead and use the bathroom before entering the debris hut for the night.
2. If it is necessary to leave the shelter, do so only in an emergency or when absolutely required.
3. When exiting the shelter, cover yourself with additional layers of clothing or blankets to retain body heat.
4. Move quickly and minimize the time spent outside the shelter to reduce exposure to cold air.
5. Upon returning to the shelter, immediately reseal all openings and reinforce the insulation around the entrance.

Notes:
• Exiting a debris hut during cold weather can cause significant heat loss that may be difficult to recover from.
• In extreme survival situations, it is best to avoid leaving the shelter unless absolutely necessary.
exit debris hut, heat loss, cold weather, insulation, survival
Add Debris to the Shelter
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 1: Collect debris and add it around the shelter.

Step 2: Aim for a thickness of about two feet on every side of the shelter.

Step 3: Keep collecting debris until you think you have enough, as this is only halfway done. Many people...
Step 1: Collect debris and add it around the shelter.

Step 2: Aim for a thickness of about two feet on every side of the shelter.

Step 3: Keep collecting debris until you think you have enough, as this is only halfway done. Many people underdo this step, leading to discomfort or cold during the night.
add debris, two feet thick, shelter insulation, debris hut construction
Build a Tunnel Entrance for the Shelter
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 1: As the walls of the shelter grow, begin constructing the entrance tunnel.

Step 2: The tunnel should be at least three feet long to provide enough space to pack leaves as you bed in for the night.
tunnel entrance, debris hut construction, tunnel length, shelter insulation
Continue Adding Debris for Insulation
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 1: Keep collecting leaves and adding them to the shelter.

Step 2: Continue until it becomes painful to bend over, as this indicates sufficient insulation has been achieved.
continue adding debris, insulation, leaf collection, shelter construction
Correcting Free Space in a Debris Hut
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Objective: Reduce free space inside the debris hut to improve heat retention.

Procedure:
1. After constructing the basic framework of the debris hut, assess the interior for any open or unused spaces.
2. Add more debris (such as leaves,...
Objective: Reduce free space inside the debris hut to improve heat retention.

Procedure:
1. After constructing the basic framework of the debris hut, assess the interior for any open or unused spaces.
2. Add more debris (such as leaves, branches, and other natural materials) to fill in all gaps on the floor and around the sleeping area.
3. Ensure that the debris is packed tightly to minimize air movement inside the shelter.
4. Use additional layers of insulation if available, such as dry grass or animal fur, to further reduce heat loss.

Notes:
• The more free space there is in a shelter, the harder it is for body heat to maintain a comfortable temperature.
• Packing the interior with debris improves thermal efficiency and helps retain warmth during cold nights.
free space, insulation, debris hut, heat retention, thermal efficiency
Finalize the Shelter Construction
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 1: Continue adding leaves for several hours after initial construction.

Step 2: The shelter should be worked on until it is "good enough" for use, even if additional work may still be needed.
finalize construction, debris hut completion, insulation, shelter building
Seal the Shelter with Dirt
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Step 1: Crawl into the shelter.

Step 2: Pack dirt in around the vertical crosspieces to block wind from creeping underneath your shelter.
seal, dirt, vertical crosspieces, wind blocking, debris hut construction
Sealing the Tunnel Entrance of a Debris Hut
Standard Source: How to Build a Debris Hut
Objective: Properly seal the tunnel entrance to prevent cold drafts and heat loss.

Procedure:
1. After digging the tunnel entrance for the debris hut, ensure that it is positioned away from direct wind exposure if possible.
2. Use natural...
Objective: Properly seal the tunnel entrance to prevent cold drafts and heat loss.

Procedure:
1. After digging the tunnel entrance for the debris hut, ensure that it is positioned away from direct wind exposure if possible.
2. Use natural materials such as leaves, branches, and dirt to create a tight seal at the entrance.
3. Layer additional debris over the entrance to block any gaps or openings that could allow cold air to enter.
4. Check the entrance periodically during the night for signs of drafts or movement of debris.
5. If a draft is detected, immediately take steps to reinforce the seal using available materials.

Notes:
• A poorly sealed tunnel entrance can significantly reduce the effectiveness of the shelter in cold weather.
• It is important to be thorough when sealing the entrance to ensure maximum insulation and comfort.
tunnel entrance, cold draft, debris hut, sealing, insulation

Roofing (8) -

Apply Tar or Pitch Cement for Secure Joints
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Applying Tar or Pitch Cement

• Before fastening laps, paint a two-inch strip with the tar or pitch cement that comes with all patent roofing.
• This helps the roofing stick to the lower strip and ensures a tight joint when put in place.
-...
Applying Tar or Pitch Cement

• Before fastening laps, paint a two-inch strip with the tar or pitch cement that comes with all patent roofing.
• This helps the roofing stick to the lower strip and ensures a tight joint when put in place.
• Do not drive nails carelessly or with too much force; ensure the cap fits snugly against the roofing.
• If nails go into holes or open cracks, do not remove them, but thoroughly cement around them.
cement application, nail placement, tight joints
Ensure Proper Overlaps and Avoid Leaks
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Proper Overlap and Leak Prevention

• Allow six inches for overlaps at joints where one strip joins another.
• Ensure that two strips of roofing never meet at the ridge, leaving a joint to invite a leak over the ridge-pole.
• Examine...
Proper Overlap and Leak Prevention

• Allow six inches for overlaps at joints where one strip joins another.
• Ensure that two strips of roofing never meet at the ridge, leaving a joint to invite a leak over the ridge-pole.
• Examine diagrams if you fail to understand the description.
overlap, leak prevention, ridge joints
Establish a Proper Roofing Foundation
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Roofing Foundation

• The roofing foundation is crucial for the quality of the roof.
• Ensure the sheathing boards are:
- Matched and of uniform thickness if possible.
- Laid close together with no gaps.
- Free from nails, protruding...
Roofing Foundation

• The roofing foundation is crucial for the quality of the roof.
• Ensure the sheathing boards are:
- Matched and of uniform thickness if possible.
- Laid close together with no gaps.
- Free from nails, protruding knots, and sharp edges.
• Avoid using green lumber, as it may shrink, warp, or even break the roofing material when exposed to sunlight.
• For very particular work where rafters are spaced wide apart, lay a course of boards over the planking at right angles to it.
foundation, sheathing boards, green lumber, roofing quality
Lay Roofing Over Gutters and Edges
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Gutters

• To finish gutters, fasten and carefully cement with pitch or tar the edge of the strip about half-way to the gutter.
• Bring the other edge onto the roof, then lay the next strip over this strip so that it will overlap at...
Gutters

• To finish gutters, fasten and carefully cement with pitch or tar the edge of the strip about half-way to the gutter.
• Bring the other edge onto the roof, then lay the next strip over this strip so that it will overlap at least two inches.
• Proceed to lay the balance of the roofing in the same way.
• Never nail the middle of the strips; nail only along the edges.
• The end strips should always be lapped over the edges of the roof and fastened.
gutters, cementing, overlapping, nailing edges
Lay Roofing in Valleys
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Valleys

• If there are valleys in the roof, use a long strip of roofing and lay it up and down in the direction of the valley.
• Press the strip into the hollow so that it takes the shape of the valley itself.
• Allow the edges of the...
Valleys

• If there are valleys in the roof, use a long strip of roofing and lay it up and down in the direction of the valley.
• Press the strip into the hollow so that it takes the shape of the valley itself.
• Allow the edges of the roofing to overlap the strip in the valley an equal distance on both sides of the valley.
valleys, roofing strips, overlap, valley shape
Proper Technique for Laying Roofing Strips
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
How to Lay the Roofing

• Begin laying roofing at the eaves.
• Always lay the roll of patent roofing with the inside surface facing outward, and in the same direction that the boards run—not at right angles to them.
• Start nailing at...
How to Lay the Roofing

• Begin laying roofing at the eaves.
• Always lay the roll of patent roofing with the inside surface facing outward, and in the same direction that the boards run—not at right angles to them.
• Start nailing at the center of the edges of the strips, working both ways toward the ends—never the reverse, as this may cause the roofing to become wrinkled, twisted, or crooked.
• Set caps even with the edge of the laps, spacing them about two inches apart between their centers.
laying technique, eaves, nailing, caps, spacing
Prepare Roofing Material for Laying
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Preparing the Roofing for Laying

• Use tar paper, birch bark, or patent roofing which are more pliable than tin or shingles.
• In very cold weather, place patent roofing in a warm room for a few hours before using it to make it easier...
Preparing the Roofing for Laying

• Use tar paper, birch bark, or patent roofing which are more pliable than tin or shingles.
• In very cold weather, place patent roofing in a warm room for a few hours before using it to make it easier to work with.
• Never attempt to cut birch bark, tar paper, or patent roofing with a dull knife.
roofing, preparation, cutting, cold weather, tools
Shingling a Shelter with Bark
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Start at the bottom of the shelter and lay the first row of bark shingles for walls, ensuring edges overlap.
2. For the roof:
- Lay one row of shingles from the bottom up to the ridge pole.
- Rest a pole on top of this row...
Procedure:

1. Start at the bottom of the shelter and lay the first row of bark shingles for walls, ensuring edges overlap.
2. For the roof:
- Lay one row of shingles from the bottom up to the ridge pole.
- Rest a pole on top of this row to hold them in place.
3. Lay the next row of shingles alongside by slipping their edges under the first row.
4. Continue until both sides are fully covered.
5. Cover the ridge with additional bark as shown in Fig. 36 for a finished look.
6. This method will create a comfortable and aesthetically pleasing camp.
bark shingles, roofing with bark, shelter construction

Roofing Construction (8) -

Calculate Rafter Line Length Using Pythagorean Theorem
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
To calculate the rafter line length, use the following steps:

1. Measure or determine the total run (horizontal distance from the wall plate to the center of the ridge) and the total rise (vertical height from the wall plate to the...
To calculate the rafter line length, use the following steps:

1. Measure or determine the total run (horizontal distance from the wall plate to the center of the ridge) and the total rise (vertical height from the wall plate to the ridge).
2. Use the Pythagorean theorem: $\text{Rafter Line Length} = \sqrt{(\text{Total Run})^2 + (\text{Total Rise})^2}$.
3. Ensure that all measurements are taken accurately, as even small errors can lead to misfitting rafters.

Notes:
• This method assumes a common rafter, which is the most basic type of rafter used in gable roofs.
• The formula calculates the length of the rafter line (the sloped edge), not including any overhangs or other adjustments.
• If detailed building plans are available, use them to verify total run and rise measurements.
rafter calculation, pythagorean theorem, rafter line length, roof construction, common rafter
Constructing a Hood or Canopy for Tropical Climates
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Purpose: The hood or canopy is used in tropical climates to protect the screened opening at the ends of buildings.
2. Materials Required:
- 2 x 4 lumber for rafters and knee braces.
- 8- or 10-penny nails.
-...
Procedure:

1. Purpose: The hood or canopy is used in tropical climates to protect the screened opening at the ends of buildings.
2. Materials Required:
- 2 x 4 lumber for rafters and knee braces.
- 8- or 10-penny nails.
- Sheathing material (same as roof sheathing).
- Roll roofing.
3. Steps to Construct the Hood:
a. Frame the hood to the end walls using short rafters.
b. Secure the rafters to the building with knee braces, as shown in figure 3-4.
c. Nail the rafters to the wall so that the bottom edge is flush with the bottom of the end plate.
d. Use 2 x 4's for both the rafters and knee braces, fastened with 8- or 10-penny nails.
e. Apply sheathing using the same material as the roof sheathing.
f. Cover the sheathing with roll roofing to complete the hood.
g. Ensure that the hood extends approximately 2 to 3 feet from the building for adequate protection.
4. Notes: This structure is specifically designed for use in tropical climates and should be installed according to figure 3-4.
hood, canopy, tropical climate, shelter construction, roof framing
Determine Rafter Dimensions Using Steel Square
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
To determine the rafter dimensions using a steel square:

1. Measure or determine the unit run (horizontal distance per rafter spacing) and the unit rise (vertical height increase per rafter spacing).
2. Mark the unit run on one leg of...
To determine the rafter dimensions using a steel square:

1. Measure or determine the unit run (horizontal distance per rafter spacing) and the unit rise (vertical height increase per rafter spacing).
2. Mark the unit run on one leg of the steel square and the unit rise on the other leg.
3. Align the steel square with the edge of the rafter material and mark the intersection point, which represents the slope of the rafter.
4. Use this marked line as a guide to cut the rafters at the correct angle for installation.

Notes:
• This method is useful when detailed building plans are not available or verification is needed.
• The unit run and unit rise can be determined based on the type of roof covering, use of the building, or other controlling factors.
• Ensure that all measurements are consistent with the overall dimensions of the structure to avoid misalignment during installation.
steel square, rafter marking, roof construction, unit run, unit rise
Finishing the Eaves Without Overhang in TO Construction
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish eaves when rafters have no overhang.
2. Materials Required:
- Sheathing boards.
- Fascia board (minimum size: 1 x 4).
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Eaves Without Overhang:
a. Place the...
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish eaves when rafters have no overhang.
2. Materials Required:
- Sheathing boards.
- Fascia board (minimum size: 1 x 4).
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Eaves Without Overhang:
a. Place the first piece of sheathing on the rafters so that it hangs over the rafters by 3 to 4 inches.
b. Install a fascia board under the sheathing and nail it to the rafter ends to close the opening between the rafters.
c. Ensure that the fascia board is at least a 1 x 4 in size.
4. Notes: This method ensures proper closure of the eave area when no overhang is present.
eave finishing, fascia board, sheathing, TO construction
Finishing the Eaves of a Sloping Roof in TO Construction
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the eaves of a sloping roof in TO (Tentative Order) construction.
2. Materials Required:
- Boards for sheathing and fascia.
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Eaves:
a. The lines where the sloping...
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the eaves of a sloping roof in TO (Tentative Order) construction.
2. Materials Required:
- Boards for sheathing and fascia.
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Eaves:
a. The lines where the sloping roof meets the vertical wall are called eaves.
b. In TO construction, finish the eaves as shown in figures 1 and 2 of figure 3-5 (see text for reference).
c. Ensure that the roof has an overhang at the eaves. The length of this overhang depends on climatic conditions and the level of protection required.
d. Close the spaces between rafters on top of the plate with boards.
4. Notes: This method is the most efficient and should be used wherever possible.
eave finishing, roof construction, TO construction, overhang, shelter
Finishing the Ends of a Roof Sheathing Without Overhang on Gables
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the ends of a roof sheathing when there is no overhang on gables.
2. Materials Required:
- 1 x 4 boards (for securing roofing at the gable ends).
- Nails.
3. **Steps to Finish Ends Without Overhang...
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the ends of a roof sheathing when there is no overhang on gables.
2. Materials Required:
- 1 x 4 boards (for securing roofing at the gable ends).
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Ends Without Overhang on Gables:
a. If the sheathing does not overhang the gables, it should end at the rafters edge.
b. The wall sheathing should end at the top edge of the rafter.
c. Lap the roofing over the end of the rafters by a minimum of 3 inches and fasten it with a 1 x 4 board nailed over the roofing along the gable.
d. Ensure that the top edge of the board is even with the top of the rafter.
4. Notes: This method ensures proper finishing at the ends of the roof sheathing when no overhang is present.
gable finishing, roofing lapping, sheathing end, shelter construction
Finishing the Ridge of a Roof with Corrugated Sheet Metal
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the ridge of a roof using corrugated sheet metal.
2. Materials Required:
- Corrugated sheet metal.
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Ridge with Corrugated Sheet Metal:
a. When using corrugated...
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the ridge of a roof using corrugated sheet metal.
2. Materials Required:
- Corrugated sheet metal.
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Ridge with Corrugated Sheet Metal:
a. When using corrugated sheet metal, ensure that the metal overhangs the sheathing by 1 to 2 inches.
b. The sheathing may also overhang on the gable ends as it does on the eaves to provide added protection to the walls.
4. Notes: This method ensures proper coverage and protection at the ridge of the roof.
ridge finishing, corrugated metal, roof construction, shelter
Finishing the Ridge of a Roof with Roll Roofing
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the ridge of a roof using roll roofing.
2. Materials Required:
- Roll roofing.
- 1 x 2 strips (for securing roofing at the ends).
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Ridge with Roll Roofing:
a....
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To finish the ridge of a roof using roll roofing.
2. Materials Required:
- Roll roofing.
- 1 x 2 strips (for securing roofing at the ends).
- Nails.
3. Steps to Finish Ridge with Roll Roofing:
a. At the ridge of the roof, where two slopes meet, ensure that the covering is finished in such a way that it will not leak.
b. Use roll roofing and finish it as shown in figures 3 and 4 of figure 3-5 (see text for reference).
c. Roll the roofing around the edge of the sheathing and back on the under side far enough so that a 1 x 2 strip can be nailed underneath to hold the roofing.
d. Alternatively, cut the roll roofing flush with the end of the sheathing and nail a 1 x 2 strip flush with the edge to secure it.
4. Notes: This is the simplest method for finishing the ridge and promotes efficiency in construction.
ridge finishing, roll roofing, roof construction, shelter

Fireplace Construction (7) -

Dig and Prepare Chimney Foundation
High Priority Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Shovel, small cobblestones or broken bluestone.

1. Dig a foundation for your fireplace and chimney at least three feet deep.
2. Fill the hole with small cobblestones or broken bluestone until nearly reaching ground...
Materials Needed: Shovel, small cobblestones or broken bluestone.

1. Dig a foundation for your fireplace and chimney at least three feet deep.
2. Fill the hole with small cobblestones or broken bluestone until nearly reaching ground level.
3. Use this filled area as the base to lay your hearth and chimney foundation.
4. Failing to dig a proper foundation can lead to frost heaving, which may cause the chimney to tilt, crack, or even collapse over time.
5. A solid foundation is essential for both structural integrity and aesthetic appearance of the house.
chimney foundation, frost heaving, cobblestone base, structural integrity
Construct a Stone Chimney
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Stones, mortar or cement.

1. When laying stones for the chimney, rough and uneven exteriors are acceptable and may even enhance the visual appeal of the structure.
2. Ensure that the inside is as smooth and even as...
Materials Needed: Stones, mortar or cement.

1. When laying stones for the chimney, rough and uneven exteriors are acceptable and may even enhance the visual appeal of the structure.
2. Ensure that the inside is as smooth and even as possible to reduce soot buildup and minimize the risk of chimney fires.
3. Lay the stones in mortar or cement, ensuring each stone fits firmly in its bed without rocking.
4. Make sure each stone breaks joints with the one directly below it, meaning that the crack between two stones on an upper tier should align with the center of a stone on the lower tier.
5. This technique helps lock the stones together and prevents cracks from spreading further.
6. If joints are not broken properly, a crack may extend from the top to the bottom of the chimney, potentially causing it to collapse.
7. Above the fireplace, build four walls for the chimney (as done with the stick chimney), as shown in Figure 271.
8. Ensure that the top of the chimney extends at least three feet above the roof, which improves airflow and reduces fire risk.
stone chimney construction, joint breaking, mortar application, chimney height
Building a Fireplace with a Central Location and High Design
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Construct the fireplace in the middle of the room, if desired. This is not preferred by the author but may be chosen for novelty.
2. Use rough stones to build the fireplace, ensuring it is high enough so that one tending...
Procedure:

1. Construct the fireplace in the middle of the room, if desired. This is not preferred by the author but may be chosen for novelty.
2. Use rough stones to build the fireplace, ensuring it is high enough so that one tending the fire does not have to stoop and risk backache.
3. Build the foundation in the ground underneath the cabin, extending up through the floor.
4. Place a flat stone on top of the fireplace, similar to other designs shown in previous figures (Fig. 274, 275).

Details:
• The fireplace should be built with proper support and materials for long-term use.
• In Fig. 276, the fireplace is constructed using rough stones and designed to be high for comfort when tending the fire.
• A puncheon support can be used for a plank mantel (as shown in Fig. 277).
central fireplace, high design, rough stones, foundation construction, puncheon support
Building a Mud Hearth for a Fireplace
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Clay or mud
• Stones (pebbles to flagstones)
• Planks (for temporary walls, if needed)

Steps to Build the Mud Hearth:
1. Construct the fireplace walls using logs and sticks for the chimney.
2. Apply clay plaster...
Materials Needed:
• Clay or mud
• Stones (pebbles to flagstones)
• Planks (for temporary walls, if needed)

Steps to Build the Mud Hearth:
1. Construct the fireplace walls using logs and sticks for the chimney.
2. Apply clay plaster over the stones of the fireplace to protect them from fire damage.
3. Build a large opening in the wall of your house where you want the fireplace, ensuring it is properly framed with planks or logs.
4. Lay sill logs on the fireplace foundation, starting with two side logs and then adding a back log, notched to match the cabin walls.
5. Build the fireplace walls as shown in the diagram (Fig. 271).
6. Mix clay or mud and use it to create a firm, smooth hearth by hammering the clay down hard until it is level and compacted.
7. Place stones of varying sizes on top of the clay hearth, leveling them by sinking their undersides into the clay for uniformity.
8. Use moist clay as mortar when building the fireplace walls, ensuring they are at least one foot thick and tightly packed.
9. Optionally build a temporary plank wall inside the fireplace, fill the space between it and the logs with moist clay, and ram it down hard until reaching the top of the fireplace.
10. Remove the temporary planks, then smooth the interior of the fireplace using a wet cloth.

Notes:
• Clay is essential to prevent fire from igniting wooden structures.
• This method ensures durability and safety for long-term use.
mud hearth construction, clay fireplace, fire protection, log cabin hearth
Constructing a Fireplace with Stone Work and Iron Bars
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Erect the fireplace stone work so that it extends into the cabin itself, protecting the ends of the logs from the fire.
2. Place two flat iron bars beneath the stone over the top of the fireplace (labeled _A_ , _B_ in Fig....
Procedure:

1. Erect the fireplace stone work so that it extends into the cabin itself, protecting the ends of the logs from the fire.
2. Place two flat iron bars beneath the stone over the top of the fireplace (labeled _A_ , _B_ in Fig. 274). These iron bars are essential for safety and to prevent cracking or falling of the stone due to heat or settling of the chimney.
3. Ensure the stone (_A_, _B_) rests on the flat iron bars even if it has cracks, as this will prevent damage from the fire or structural settling.
4. For a more durable setup, use a single slab of bluestone (as in Fig. 275) and line the fireplace with fire-brick for added protection.

Details:
• The stone work should be built up inside the cabin to support a mantelpiece.
• A plank (_C_, _D_) is nailed under the mantelpiece against the log before the stone work is constructed, providing a finished look to the mantel.
• In Fig. 274, the hearth is made of flat stones laid in cement, while in Fig. 275, it is one large slab of bluestone from the quarry.
• The fireplace can be used for cooking purposes, with a crane hanging over the flames and additional items like a wrought-iron broiler, toaster, and brazier placed on or near the mantel.
fireplace construction, stone work, iron bars, firebrick, mantelpiece, hearth design
Constructing a Simple Fireplace and Chimney for a Log Cabin
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Stones or sod
• Stakes (forked or notched)
• Small logs and billets

Steps to Build the Fireplace and Chimney:
1. Drive stakes into the ground at the location where you want to build the fireplace.
2. **Lay three...
Materials Needed:
• Stones or sod
• Stakes (forked or notched)
• Small logs and billets

Steps to Build the Fireplace and Chimney:
1. Drive stakes into the ground at the location where you want to build the fireplace.
2. Lay three walls for windbreaks using stones or sod against these stakes.
3. Place small logs horizontally in the forks of the stakes.
4. Build a pyramidal log pen on top of these logs, which will serve as the chimney.
5. Use this design for camps, rudimentary shacks, and simple log cabins.

Notes:
• This is a basic version suitable for temporary or primitive shelters.
• For more elaborate structures, consider building a finished fireplace and chimney with proper materials.
fireplace construction, log cabin chimney, primitive shelter, fire protection
Ensure Chimney Durability and Longevity
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Stones, mortar or cement.

1. In regions like East Tennessee and Kentucky, stick chimneys have been observed to last for many years.
2. These fireplaces are often lined with stones, but in areas where stones were scarce...
Materials Needed: Stones, mortar or cement.

1. In regions like East Tennessee and Kentucky, stick chimneys have been observed to last for many years.
2. These fireplaces are often lined with stones, but in areas where stones were scarce (like Illinois), they were made using the stick chimney method described above.
3. A stone chimney is considered an improvement over a log chimney, though it may not require significantly more skill to build.
4. Ensure that the structure of the chimney is stable and well-constructed for long-term use.
chimney durability, stone chimney, log chimney, fireplace longevity

Roof Design (5) -

Calculate Load-Bearing Capacity of Earth-Covered Roof (Contact Burst Rounds)
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure:

1. Enter the unit weight of the soil (lb/cf) as determined on site.
2. Enter the proposed depth of soil cover (ft).
3. Enter the S value (in³):
- If using wood, use values from Table B-2.
- If using...
Procedure:

1. Enter the unit weight of the soil (lb/cf) as determined on site.
2. Enter the proposed depth of soil cover (ft).
3. Enter the S value (in³):
- If using wood, use values from Table B-2.
- If using steel, use values from Table B-4.
4. Enter the stringer spacing (in).
5. Enter the FS value (psi):
- If using wood, use values from Table B-3.
- If using steel, enter 50,000 psi.
6. Enter the stringer span length (ft).
7. Multiply line 1 by line 4, and enter the result.
8. Multiply line 7 by line 2, and enter the result.
9A. Multiply line 8 by line 6, and enter the result.
9B. Multiply line 9A by line 6, and enter the result.
9C. Divide line 9B by 8, and enter the result.
9D. Divide line 9C by line 3, and enter the result.
9E. Divide line 9D by line 5, and enter the result.
10. If the value from line 9E is:
- Greater than 0 but less than 1.0: Proceed to line 10.
- Greater than 1.0: The roof system is overloaded. Take one of the following actions and recompute from line 1:
a. Decrease stringer spacing.
b. Decrease span length.
c. Use a material with a higher "S" or "FS" value.
d. Decrease soil cover.

Notes:
• This procedure is used to determine if the roof can withstand the expected blast load from contact burst rounds.
• If the result exceeds 1.0, the design must be adjusted to ensure structural integrity.
earth-covered roof calculation, contact burst rounds, shelter construction
Determine Roof Load Capacity Using Figure B-2 (Contact Burst Rounds)
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure:

1. Use the result from line 9E as the input for Figure B-2.
2. Locate the value on side A of Figure B-2 that corresponds to the line 9E result.
3. Find the corresponding value on side B and enter it into the...
Procedure:

1. Use the result from line 9E as the input for Figure B-2.
2. Locate the value on side A of Figure B-2 that corresponds to the line 9E result.
3. Find the corresponding value on side B and enter it into the calculation.
4. Use the following curves based on material type:
- If using wood, use the µ = 1 curve.
- If using steel, use the µ = 10 curve.

Notes:
• This step is used to determine the final load capacity of the roof after adjusting for material properties and design parameters.
• Ensure that the figure or graph is accurate and corresponds to the correct version of the source text.
figure b-2, roof load capacity, contact burst rounds
Determine Stringer Characteristics for Earth-Covered Roof Design
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure:

1. For wood stringers, use the values from Table B-2 and Table B-3 (from the source text) to determine the moment of inertia (I), section modulus (S), modulus of elasticity (E), and **maximum dynamic flexural...
Procedure:
