1. For wood stringers, use the values from Table B-2 and Table B-3 (from the source text) to determine the moment of inertia (I), section modulus (S), modulus of elasticity (E), and maximum dynamic flexural stress (FS).
2. For steel stringers, use the values from Table B-4 for moment of inertia (I) and section modulus (S).
3. Use the following default values:
- Modulus of elasticity (E): 29
- Maximum dynamic flexural stress (FS): 50,000 psi

Notes:
• These parameters are essential for calculating the structural integrity and load-bearing capacity of the roof.
• If tables are unavailable, consult a structural engineer or use standard values based on material type.
stringer characteristics, earth-covered roof design, shelter construction
Determine Transmission Coefficient for Earth-Covered Roof Design
High Priority Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure:

1. Use Table B-1 (from the source text) to find the transmission coefficient based on the type of soil and its properties.
2. The transmission coefficient is used in conjunction with other parameters to calculate the roof's...
Procedure:

1. Use Table B-1 (from the source text) to find the transmission coefficient based on the type of soil and its properties.
2. The transmission coefficient is used in conjunction with other parameters to calculate the roof's ability to withstand blast loads.

Notes:
• This value is essential for determining how much of the blast load is transmitted through the earth cover to the structure below.
• If Table B-1 is unavailable, consult a structural engineer or use default values based on soil type.
transmission coefficient, earth-covered roof design, shelter construction
Designing a Flat or Pitched Roof for Different Materials
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Clay
• Shingles
• Thatch

Steps to Design the Roof Based on Material:
1. Clay Roof: Construct it as flat as possible with only enough pitch to shed water.
2. Shingle Roof: Ensure a rise of at least one foot high...
Materials Needed:
• Clay
• Shingles
• Thatch

Steps to Design the Roof Based on Material:
1. Clay Roof: Construct it as flat as possible with only enough pitch to shed water.
2. Shingle Roof: Ensure a rise of at least one foot high for every four feet wide.
3. Thatched Roof: Create a rise of 45°, which is the same as the rise of a line drawn from corner to corner of a square.

Notes:
• These specifications help ensure proper water runoff and structural integrity based on the roofing material used.
• Refer to figures in the original text for visual examples.
roof pitch, roof design, shelter construction

Construction Techniques (5) -

Discuss Thatching Job on Shelter in Survival Situations
High Priority Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:
1. Discuss the importance of a proper thatching job when building a shelter.
2. Highlight how effective thatching can protect against weather elements such as rain and wind.
3. Emphasize the need for durable materials and proper...
Procedure:
1. Discuss the importance of a proper thatching job when building a shelter.
2. Highlight how effective thatching can protect against weather elements such as rain and wind.
3. Emphasize the need for durable materials and proper construction techniques.
thatching,shelter building,survival shelter
Constructing Adirondack, Wickup, Bark Teepee, Pioneer, and Scout Shelters
High Priority Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Select the shelter type: Choose between an Adirondack, Wickup, Bark Teepee, Pioneer, or Scout shelter based on your needs and available materials.
2. Gather materials: Collect bark, poles, and other necessary supplies for...
Procedure:

1. Select the shelter type: Choose between an Adirondack, Wickup, Bark Teepee, Pioneer, or Scout shelter based on your needs and available materials.
2. Gather materials: Collect bark, poles, and other necessary supplies for construction.
3. Prepare the site: Clear a flat area of debris and ensure it is suitable for building.
4. Build the structure:
- For an Adirondack shelter: Use logs or poles to form a frame with a sloping roof.
- For a Wickup shelter: Construct a dome-like shape using bent poles covered with bark or other materials.
- For a Bark Teepee: Create a conical structure with a central pole and layers of bark around it, leaving the top open as a chimney.
- For a Pioneer shelter: Build a simple rectangular frame with a roof made from bark or branches.
- For a Scout shelter: Use lightweight materials to construct a quick, temporary shelter suitable for short stays.
5. Secure the structure: Ensure all components are firmly tied and anchored to withstand weather conditions.
6. Add finishing touches: Cover any exposed areas with additional layers of bark or other insulating materials for protection against rain and cold.

Notes:
• Each shelter type has specific construction techniques detailed in the original text (referenced as figures 11-14).
• Choose a shelter design that matches your environment, available resources, and intended use.
adirondack,wickup,bark teepee,pioneer,scout,shelter,construction,types
Using Bark for Shingling a Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Cut the bark: Divide the bark into convenient sections based on the size of your shelter.
2. Start at the bottom: Begin placing the first piece of bark flat against the wall of your shelter, with one edge set on edge...
Procedure:

1. Cut the bark: Divide the bark into convenient sections based on the size of your shelter.
2. Start at the bottom: Begin placing the first piece of bark flat against the wall of your shelter, with one edge set on edge (vertical).
3. Overlap pieces: Place each subsequent piece so that its edge overlaps the previous one by a few inches.
4. Continue around the structure: Repeat this process all the way around the shelter until you complete the first layer.
5. Add layers above: Stack another layer of bark directly above the first, ensuring both the top and bottom edges overlap by three to four inches or more.
6. Secure the bark: Use stakes driven into the ground or poles laid over the bark to hold it in place, depending on your shelter's shape.
7. Cover the roof: Continue layering bark up to the comb (peak) of the roof and over the area where the sides meet at the top to cover the ridge, crown, or apex for protection from rain.
8. Chimney consideration: In teepee-shaped shelters, leave the top open as a chimney for smoke to escape.

Notes:
• This method is suitable for various shelter types like wigwams and teepees.
• Ensure proper overlap between layers for durability and weather resistance.
bark,shingling,roofing,shelter,construction,overlap,securing
Building a Concrete House Using Thatch and Wire Netting
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials needed: Mud, cement or concrete, wire netting.

Steps:
1. Fill the space between walls with mud.
2. Plaster the outside with cement or concrete.
3. The wire netting will hold the plaster or concrete, allowing for a...
Materials needed: Mud, cement or concrete, wire netting.

Steps:
1. Fill the space between walls with mud.
2. Plaster the outside with cement or concrete.
3. The wire netting will hold the plaster or concrete, allowing for a thinner covering than in ordinary buildings.
4. After the mud dries on the inside, it will be practically as strong as a solid concrete wall when covered with a crust of cement or plaster.
concrete house, mud, wire netting, plaster, construction techniques
Construct Slanting Walls for Shacks in the Woods
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Understand the purpose of slanting walls: Slanting walls are easier to thatch and cover with turf, especially in northern regions where true sod is not available.
2. Imitate Native American techniques: Follow the example...
Procedure:

1. Understand the purpose of slanting walls: Slanting walls are easier to thatch and cover with turf, especially in northern regions where true sod is not available.
2. Imitate Native American techniques: Follow the example of Indian shelters by constructing slanting walls for your shacks, shanties, or shelters in the woods.
3. Use perpendicular walls if necessary: If you have materials such as boards, stone, brick, or logs, it is acceptable to use perpendicular walls.
4. Consider regional differences: In areas with thick grass and interlacing roots (middle country), slanting walls may be less critical than in the far North.

Notes:
• The Pima Indians thatch their houses with arrow brush and sometimes bank the sides of their shacks with dirt.
slanting walls, construction techniques, shelter building, thatching, woodlands

Revetments (4) -

Construct a Brushwood Hurdle Facing Revetment
Reference Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Materials Required:
• Brushwood about 1 inch in diameter
• Sharpened pickets
• Twine or wire for tying

Steps to Construct:
1. Prepare the Framework:
- Drive sharpened pickets into the ground at 20-inch intervals.
2. **Weave...
Materials Required:
• Brushwood about 1 inch in diameter
• Sharpened pickets
• Twine or wire for tying

Steps to Construct:
1. Prepare the Framework:
- Drive sharpened pickets into the ground at 20-inch intervals.
2. Weave Brushwood:
- Weave pieces of brushwood (about 1 inch in diameter) onto the framework.
3. Complete the Hurdle:
- Continue weaving until the hurdle is approximately 6½ feet long and as high as the revetted wall.
4. Transport and Install:
- Carry the completed hurdle to the position where it will be installed.
5. Secure the Hurdle:
- Tie the tops of the pickets back to stakes or holdfasts for stability.
- Wire the ends of the hurdles together to ensure they are firmly in place.
brushwood hurdle, revetment construction, shelter materials, military engineering
Construct a Corrugated Metal or Plywood Revetment
Reference Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Materials Required:
• Corrugated metal sheets
• Plywood
• Mud for camouflage and thermal radiation reduction

Steps to Construct:
1. Install Sheets/Planks:
- Use corrugated metal sheets or plywood to construct the revetment.
2. **Lap...
Materials Required:
• Corrugated metal sheets
• Plywood
• Mud for camouflage and thermal radiation reduction

Steps to Construct:
1. Install Sheets/Planks:
- Use corrugated metal sheets or plywood to construct the revetment.
2. Lap Edges as Needed:
- Lap edges and ends of sheets or planks to achieve the desired height and length.
3. Apply Mud for Camouflage:
- Smear all exposed metal surfaces with mud to reduce reflection of thermal radiation and improve camouflage.
corrugated metal revetment, plywood revetment, military engineering
Construct a Pole Revetment
Reference Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Materials Required:
• Small horizontal round poles
• Pickets (3 inches in diameter)
• Boards or planks (if available)
• Holdfasts or struts

Steps to Construct:
1. Install Pickets:
- Drive pickets into the ground at appropriate...
Materials Required:
• Small horizontal round poles
• Pickets (3 inches in diameter)
• Boards or planks (if available)
• Holdfasts or struts

Steps to Construct:
1. Install Pickets:
- Drive pickets into the ground at appropriate intervals, similar to the continuous brush revetment.
2. Use Poles Instead of Brushwood:
- Replace small horizontal brushwood with small round poles, cut to the length of the revetted wall.
3. Alternative Material:
- If available, use boards or planks for quicker installation instead of poles.
4. Secure Pickets:
- Hold pickets in place using holdfasts or struts.
pole revetment, shelter construction, military engineering
Support Facing Revetments with Timber Frames or Pickets
Reference Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Materials Required:
• Dimensioned timber for frames
• Pickets (3 inches in diameter)
• Stakes, holdfasts, and wire

Steps to Construct:
1. Construct Timber Frames:
- Build frames from dimensioned timber that fit the bottom and sides...
Materials Required:
• Dimensioned timber for frames
• Pickets (3 inches in diameter)
• Stakes, holdfasts, and wire

Steps to Construct:
1. Construct Timber Frames:
- Build frames from dimensioned timber that fit the bottom and sides of the position.
2. Install Pickets:
- Drive pickets into the ground on the position side of the facing material at appropriate intervals (determined by soil type and facing material).
3. Secure Pickets:
- Brace pickets apart across the width of the position to hold the facing material in place.
4. Anchor Pickets:
- Drive an anchor stake or holdfast into the top of the bank and tie it to the top of the picket.
5. Wire Fixing:
- Place several strands of wire straight and taut across the width of the position, passing them through a groove or channel cut in the parapet.
timber frame revetment support, picket installation, military engineering

Traditional Shelters (4) -

Construct a Chippewa Indian Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Small saplings or poles
• Birch bark (or alternative materials such as brush, hay, straw, or mud)
• Ropes made from natural fibers

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Create a dome-shaped structure...
Materials Required:
• Small saplings or poles
• Birch bark (or alternative materials such as brush, hay, straw, or mud)
• Ropes made from natural fibers

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Create a dome-shaped structure using small saplings bent into arches.
- Sink the ends of the poles into the ground in a circular pattern and bind them together at the top for stability.
2. Covering the Frame:
- Traditionally, cover the framework with large strips of birch bark secured by ropes stretched over it (Fig. 32).
- If birch bark is unavailable, use smaller pieces of bark or other local materials such as brush, hay, straw, palmetto leaves, palm leaves, rushes, or mud.
3. Add a Door:
- Install a door using a blanket portière for entry and privacy.

Notes:
• This design is highly adaptable to different environments and can be modified based on available resources.
• Using mud as a covering material transforms the structure into an adobe hut, which provides additional insulation.
Chippewa shelter, dome-shaped frame, birch bark covering
Construct a Pima Indian Flat-Roofed Lodge
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Upright posts
• Crosspieces
• Poles for the roof and sides
• Local materials (such as brush or thatch)

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Use four upright posts to support two crosspieces at the top.
...
Materials Required:
• Upright posts
• Crosspieces
• Poles for the roof and sides
• Local materials (such as brush or thatch)

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Use four upright posts to support two crosspieces at the top.
- Place poles across the crosspieces to form a flat roof, similar to wick-ups described in traditional manuals.
2. Construct the Sides:
- The sides are not upright but made by resting leaning poles against the eaves of the structure.
3. Adaptability for Climate:
- In colder regions, add insulation using local materials such as brush or thatch to make the lodge warm and tight.
- In arid regions, ensure proper ventilation by leaving gaps in the sides or using lighter materials.

Notes:
• This design is versatile and can be adapted for use in various climates across the country.
• The flat roof provides a stable surface that can be used for additional purposes such as storage.
Pima Indian lodge, flat-roofed shelter, climate adaptability
Construct a San Carlos Apache Dome-Shaped Hut
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Small saplings
• Bear-grass or other local thatching material
• Yucca leaves (for string)

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Use small saplings bent into arches, as shown in Fig. 30.
- Sink the...
Materials Required:
• Small saplings
• Bear-grass or other local thatching material
• Yucca leaves (for string)

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Use small saplings bent into arches, as shown in Fig. 30.
- Sink the ends of the poles into the ground in a circular pattern.
2. Secure the Structure:
- Bend the tips of the poles over and bind them together to form overlapping loops for stability.
3. Thatching the Roof:
- Cover the dome with bear-grass arranged in overlapping rows.
- Secure the thatch using strings made from yucca leaves (Fig. 31).

Notes:
• This design is adaptable to various environments and can be modified based on local materials.
• The use of bear-grass provides a durable, weather-resistant roof.
San Carlos Apache hut, dome-shaped shelter, thatching with bear-grass
Construct an Apache Hogan Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Small saplings or poles
• Rank grass (or corn-stalks and leaves)

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Use small saplings or poles to create a tent-shaped structure.
- The framework should be similar...
Materials Required:
• Small saplings or poles
• Rank grass (or corn-stalks and leaves)

Steps to Construct:
1. Build the Framework:
- Use small saplings or poles to create a tent-shaped structure.
- The framework should be similar to that described in Figs. 18 and 19, but without birch bark covering.
2. Thatching the Roof:
- Cover the long, sloping sides of the shelter with a thick layer of rank grass found locally.
- In modern times, corn-stalks and leaves are used for thatching due to their availability and effectiveness.
3. Stability Check:
- Ensure the structure is stable and secure before adding any additional materials or using it as a shelter.

Notes:
• The Apache Hogan is designed for practicality and adaptability to local conditions.
• Corn-stalks are recommended for their durability and availability in certain regions.
Apache Hogan, shelter construction, thatching materials, traditional shelters

Roof Repair (4) -

Patch a Leaking Tin Roof Using Available Materials
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Old bits of tin
• Rags
• White lead (paint)
• Carpet tacks or small wire nails
• Hammer
• Paint brush
• Red roof paint (optional for camouflage)

Steps to Patch a Leaking Tin Roof:
1. Inspect the Damage: Carefully...
Materials Needed:
• Old bits of tin
• Rags
• White lead (paint)
• Carpet tacks or small wire nails
• Hammer
• Paint brush
• Red roof paint (optional for camouflage)

Steps to Patch a Leaking Tin Roof:
1. Inspect the Damage: Carefully examine the roof for cracks, rust holes, and open seams.
2. Prepare the Area: If there are any obstacles or difficulty in driving tacks through the tin, use a small wire nail and hammer to punch holes first.
3. Secure Edges with Tacks: Use carpet tacks and a hammer to neatly tack down the edges of the opening (see Fig. 303). Place tacks close together for better coverage.
4. Apply White Lead: With a paint brush, thickly coat the mended parts with white lead (see Fig. 304).
5. Cover Holes with Rags: Cut a strip of rag to fit over the holes and tack it at its four corners (see Fig. 305). Ensure that the rag is securely fastened.
6. Apply Another Coat of White Lead: Cover the rag with a thick coat of white lead (see Fig. 306).
7. Cover with Tin Patch: Place a piece of tin over the damaged spots and secure it with tacks, placing them close together (see Fig. 306). Ensure that the patch is tightly secured.
8. Final Coating of White Lead: Apply another coat of white lead to cover the entire patched area.
9. Camouflage Patches (Optional): Once the white lead has dried, use red roof paint to dab over the white patches, making them blend with the surrounding tin.

Important Notes:
• Ensure that every joint, nail hole, and seam is properly covered, tacked, and coated with white lead. Water can seep through even small openings if they are not made absolutely tight.
• This method will last for at least twenty years if done correctly.
• Avoid using modern sheet steel for roofing as it disintegrates more quickly than older tin.
roof patching, tin roof repair, white lead, tacks, rag patch, rust holes, leak repair
Repair Roof Leaks with Sheet-Iron Shingles
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Identify cracks or holes in the roof that need repair.
2. Bend up bits of tin or sheet iron to create metal shingles (as shown in Fig. 300).
3. Drive the metal shingle into place:
- Underneath the shingle above the...
Procedure:

1. Identify cracks or holes in the roof that need repair.
2. Bend up bits of tin or sheet iron to create metal shingles (as shown in Fig. 300).
3. Drive the metal shingle into place:
- Underneath the shingle above the hole so that the 'weather' part of the tin covers the leak
- Underneath the leaking shingle itself
- Or drive a new shingle up under or over the damaged one.
4. For larger leaks, create a patch using multiple shingles as shown in the right-hand corner of Fig. 295½.
5. Do not remove old shingles unless the hole is very large.

Notes:
• These repairs are effective for keeping out rain and snow, preventing moisture damage to timbers.
• While not aesthetically pleasing, they provide a dry roof over your head.
• Over time, new shingles will tone down in color and become less noticeable.
sheet iron,repair,roof leaks,metal shingles,patching,timbers,damage prevention
Mark Roof Holes with Wooden Pegs
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Go up in the attic and inspect the roof for areas where daylight is visible through the roof.
2. Push a wooden peg through each hole to mark its location on the roof.
3. Once all holes are marked, **prepare for climbing...
Procedure:

1. Go up in the attic and inspect the roof for areas where daylight is visible through the roof.
2. Push a wooden peg through each hole to mark its location on the roof.
3. Once all holes are marked, prepare for climbing onto the roof:
- Remove your shoes
- Put on a pair of woollen socks to reduce the risk of slipping
- Alternatively, wear new india rubber shoes with corrugated soles, which also provide good traction when climbing on the roof.

Notes:
• This method ensures accurate identification of roof holes before repairs begin.
• Proper footwear is crucial for safety during roof work.
mark,holes,wooden pegs,roof repair,attic,slipping,woollen socks,corrugated soles
Patch a Shingle Roof with Available Materials
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Assess the Damage: Inspect the roof for leaks or openings around the chimney or other areas. Look for signs of water intrusion, such as wet spots inside the shelter or visible damage to shingles.

2. Gather Materials:...
Procedure:

1. Assess the Damage: Inspect the roof for leaks or openings around the chimney or other areas. Look for signs of water intrusion, such as wet spots inside the shelter or visible damage to shingles.

2. Gather Materials: Collect any available materials that can be used to patch the roof. These may include:
- Strips of tar roofing compounds
- Old tin sheets
- Oilcloth (ensure it is not too worn or perforated)
- Waterproof sealant or caulk if available

3. Prepare the Area: Clear any debris, loose shingles, or damaged materials from the area around the leak. Ensure that the surface is clean and dry to promote adhesion.

4. Apply Sealant: If using tar roofing compounds or waterproof sealant, apply a generous amount over the affected area. This will help prevent water from seeping through the patch.

5. Cover with Material: Place the chosen material (tar strips, tin sheet, or oilcloth) over the damaged section of the roof. Secure it in place using nails, adhesive, or additional layers of roofing compound if necessary.

6. Seal Edges and Gaps: Ensure that all edges and gaps around the patch are sealed tightly to prevent water from entering through any openings. Use additional sealant or tar as needed.

7. Inspect for Effectiveness: After completing the repair, inspect the patched area to ensure it is secure and watertight. Check periodically for signs of wear or new leaks.

Warnings/Notes:
• If the damage is extensive or if there are structural issues with the roof, consider seeking professional assistance.
• Ensure that all materials used are waterproof and suitable for outdoor use.
• Regular maintenance can help prevent future leaks and extend the life of your shelter's roof.
patch, shingle roof, tar, oilcloth, tin, waterproofing, leak repair

Roofing Installation (4) -

Install Corrugated Roofing Sheets
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
### Install Corrugated Roofing Sheets

Materials Required:
• Corrugated sheet iron
• Solid or slatted wood sheathing, or wood purlins
• Special nails (lead-headed nail or galvanized nail with neoprene washer)
• Common wire nails (optional,...
### Install Corrugated Roofing Sheets

Materials Required:
• Corrugated sheet iron
• Solid or slatted wood sheathing, or wood purlins
• Special nails (lead-headed nail or galvanized nail with neoprene washer)
• Common wire nails (optional, but may cause leaks)

Steps:
1. Prepare the Roof Structure: Ensure that the roof has a slope of at least 4 inches per foot. Corrugated roofing is not suitable for roofs with slopes less than this.
2. Install Sheathing or Purlins: Secure solid or slatted wood sheathing, or wood purlins to the roof frame.
3. Position Roofing Sheets: Place corrugated sheet iron over the sheathing or purlins. Overlap sheets by one or two corrugations on the sides and 6 or 8 inches on the ends, depending on the slope of the roof.
4. Secure with Nails:
- Use special nails (lead-headed or galvanized with neoprene washer) for secure fastening.
- If common wire nails are used, be aware that they may rust and cause slight leaks.
5. Nailing Pattern:
- Nail the side laps every 2 feet.
- Nail the end laps every foot to ensure proper sealing and stability.

Notes:
• Corrugated roofing should not be used on roofs with slopes less than 4 inches per foot.
• Ensure that all overlaps are properly secured to prevent water infiltration.
corrugated roofing, installation, nails, sheathing, purlins, roof slope, waterproofing
Install Flashing at Roof-Wall Junctions
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
### Install Flashing at Roof-Wall Junctions

Materials Required:
• Metal or roll roofing material
• Girt (if the vertical wall is not sheathed)
• Cutting tools

Steps:
1. Identify the Junction Location: Locate where a roof meets a...
### Install Flashing at Roof-Wall Junctions

Materials Required:
• Metal or roll roofing material
• Girt (if the vertical wall is not sheathed)
• Cutting tools

Steps:
1. Identify the Junction Location: Locate where a roof meets a vertical wall.
2. Prepare the Wall Structure: If the vertical wall is not sheathed, install a girt between the studs to support the flashing material.
3. Cut Flashing Material: Cut metal or roll roofing material to fit along the junction of the roof and the wall.
4. Position Flashing Material: Place the flashing material over the joint where the roof meets the vertical wall.
5. Secure Flashing Material: Secure the flashing with appropriate fasteners or adhesive, ensuring it is tightly sealed along the junction.

Notes:
• Metal or roll roofing may be used as the flashing material.
• Ensure that all edges are properly sealed to prevent water infiltration.
roof-wall junction, flashing installation, metal roofing, waterproofing
Install Prepared Roofing Sheets
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
### Install Prepared Roofing Sheets

Materials Required:
• Prepared roofing sheets
• Solidly sheathed roof structure
• Special nails (with large, flat heads)
• Asphaltic material (normally included with the roofing)

Steps:
1. **Prepare...
### Install Prepared Roofing Sheets

Materials Required:
• Prepared roofing sheets
• Solidly sheathed roof structure
• Special nails (with large, flat heads)
• Asphaltic material (normally included with the roofing)

Steps:
1. Prepare the Roof Structure: Ensure that the roof is solidly sheathed and suitable for prepared roofing. For slopes of more than 2 inches per foot, use an -inch end lap and a 4-inch edge or side lap.
2. Apply Asphaltic Material: Before installing the sheets, apply asphalt or tar to all laps (edges and ends) to ensure proper waterproofing.
3. Position Roofing Sheets: Lay the prepared roofing strips parallel to the roof slope for better water runoff and reduced likelihood of leaks.
4. Secure with Nails:
- Use special nails with large, flat heads.
- Nail edge and end laps 3 inches on centers.
5. Adjust for Slope:
- For flatter slopes (less than 2 inches per foot), use at least a 6-inch edge lap and ensure all joints are fully cemented.
- Roofs with slopes less than 1 inch per foot require multilayer, built-up roofing instead of prepared roofing.

Notes:
• A 40-pound roll (or 'square') of prepared roofing will cover only 80 square feet in TO construction.
• Rolls of 15- and 30-pound felt cover 320 and 160 square feet, respectively.
prepared roofing, installation, asphaltic material, roof slope, waterproofing
Install Roof Flashings in Valleys
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
### Install Roof Flashings in Valleys

Materials Required:
• Roll roofing (two layers: one 14 inches wide and one 22 inches wide)
• Sheet metal (22 inches wide, optional)
• Cutting tools

Steps:
1. Identify the Valley Location:...
### Install Roof Flashings in Valleys

Materials Required:
• Roll roofing (two layers: one 14 inches wide and one 22 inches wide)
• Sheet metal (22 inches wide, optional)
• Cutting tools

Steps:
1. Identify the Valley Location: Locate where two roofs meet at an angle to form a valley.
2. Cut Roll Roofing Material: Cut two layers of roll roofing from a 36-inch width of material:
- One layer should be 14 inches wide.
- The other layer should be 22 inches wide.
3. Position Flashing Material: Place the cut roll roofing or sheet metal along the valley, ensuring that it covers the joint between the two roofs.
4. Cut Back from Valley Center: Cut back the flashing material from the center of the valley by 3 to 4 inches to allow for proper overlap and sealing.
5. Secure Flashing Material: Secure the flashing with appropriate fasteners or adhesive, ensuring it is tightly sealed along the valley joint.

Notes:
• This method provides a hasty but satisfactory expedient for waterproofing valleys.
• Ensure that all edges are properly sealed to prevent water infiltration.
roof flashings, valley installation, roll roofing, sheet metal, waterproofing

Snow Shelters (3) -

Construct a Snow House in Open Country or Barrens
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Step-by-step procedure for building a snow house:

1. Begin with the same method as constructing a snow trench.

2. Close in the roof by using snow blocks to build up the ends of the trench and trimming them to form a sharp peak (see...
Step-by-step procedure for building a snow house:

1. Begin with the same method as constructing a snow trench.

2. Close in the roof by using snow blocks to build up the ends of the trench and trimming them to form a sharp peak (see Figure 3-22).

3. Lay snow blocks alternately along the sides, leaning inward toward each other. Ensure all snow blocks are cut to the same width so that joints are staggered.

4. Trim the top of the snow blocks for a close fit, ensuring the structure is stable and well-insulated (see Figure 3-23).
snow house construction, cold weather shelter, military snow house, open country shelter, insulated snow structure
Construct a Tent Roof Shelter in Open Country or Barrens
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Step-by-step procedure for building a tent roof shelter:

1. Lay out the tent on the ground and shape it as if you were pitching it. Mark the outline of this shape in the snow.

2. Remove the tent, then mark the snow again approximately...
Step-by-step procedure for building a tent roof shelter:

1. Lay out the tent on the ground and shape it as if you were pitching it. Mark the outline of this shape in the snow.

2. Remove the tent, then mark the snow again approximately eight inches inside the first mark (see Figure 3-16).

3. Dig a hole inside this second mark to a depth of four feet.

4. Create roof supports by laying logs across the hole. Approximately 10 logs are required (see Figure 3-17). If no logs are available, use the pole and guy ropes from the tent:
- Insert the pole upright in the center of the hole.
- Tie all the guy ropes to the top of the pole and extend them out around the hole in a wagon wheel fashion (see Figure 3-18).
- Ensure the top of the pole does not extend above the surface of the snow.

5. Lay the tent over the roof supports and pile snow on the outside edges and snow flaps.
- In an all-white area, ensure the tent liner is on the outside.
- If the liner is destroyed, use the outer tent as a cover and camouflage with ruck-sack covers.
- If no camouflage material is available, cover the roof with snow. Important: Keep the heat inside the shelter very low to prevent melting of the snow and water seepage through the roof.

6. Alternative method if snow depth is insufficient or concealment is not a priority:
- Dig the hole only three feet deep.
- Place a row of snow blocks 1.5 feet high between the first and second marks around the complete hole (see Figure 3-19).

7. Construct an exit tunnel that goes out level with the floor, then down so that the top of the tunnel entrance is eighteen inches below the floor level. This acts as an air trap to prevent cold air from entering the shelter (see Figure 3-19).
tent roof shelter, snow shelter construction, cold weather shelter, military shelter building, open country shelter
Construct Snow Walls for Wind Protection in Open Country or Barrens
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Step-by-step procedure for building snow walls as windbreaks:

1. Cut snow blocks from compact snow.

2. Build a semi-circle of snow blocks to a height of about three feet.

3. Bank up loose snow on the windward side of the wall to...
Step-by-step procedure for building snow walls as windbreaks:

1. Cut snow blocks from compact snow.

2. Build a semi-circle of snow blocks to a height of about three feet.

3. Bank up loose snow on the windward side of the wall to increase its effectiveness in blocking wind.

4. Use this structure as a protective barrier behind which you can sleep (see Figure 3-24).
snow wall construction, wind protection in cold weather, open country shelter, military snow barrier, survival windbreak

Bedding Construction (3) -

Pack Boughs Using a Stick for Transport
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Strong green hardwood stick (4–5 feet long), with a forked end and sharpened tip

Procedure:
1. Prepare the stick by leaving a fork about six inches long at the butt end to prevent boughs from sliding off.
2. Sharpen one...
Materials Needed: Strong green hardwood stick (4–5 feet long), with a forked end and sharpened tip

Procedure:
1. Prepare the stick by leaving a fork about six inches long at the butt end to prevent boughs from sliding off.
2. Sharpen one end of the stick so it can be easily poked through a handful of boughs.
3. Gather boughs in small handfuls and string them onto the stick, similar to how you would string fish.
4. Ensure that the butts of the boughs point in different directions for even distribution.
5. Once strung, throw the bundle over your shoulder as shown in Fig. 5 for easy transport.

Alternative Method (if a lash rope is available):
1. Place the boughs on a loop of the lash rope.
2. Bring both ends of the rope up through the loop and sling the bundle across your back, as illustrated in Fig. 6.
bough packing, transport stick, bedding materials
Collect Balsam Twigs for Bedding
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Balsam-tree twigs of varying lengths (18 inches for foundation, 10–12 inches for top layer)

Procedure:
1. Gather balsam twigs in different sizes: longer ones (18 inches) for the base and shorter ones (10–12 inches) for the...
Materials Needed: Balsam-tree twigs of varying lengths (18 inches for foundation, 10–12 inches for top layer)

Procedure:
1. Gather balsam twigs in different sizes: longer ones (18 inches) for the base and shorter ones (10–12 inches) for the top.
2. Avoid cutting the twigs with a knife, as this is slow and can blister your hands.
3. Use your fingers to snap the twigs off cleanly:
- Place the thumb on top of the twig, pointing toward its tip.
- Position the two forefingers underneath the twig.
- Press down with the thumb and twist the wrist to snap the twig like a pipe-stem.
4. Ensure you collect enough twigs for comfort; insufficient bedding can lead to discomfort or pain during sleep.

Warning: Be cautious when handling balsam twigs, as they may spring back and slap your face if cut improperly.
balsam beds, twigs collection, bedding construction, outdoor sleeping
Construct a Comfortable Balsam Bed
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Collected boughs (larger ones for the base, smaller ones for the top), blanket, and clothing bag

Procedure:
1. Lay out a layer of larger boughs on the ground to form the foundation of your bed.
2. Start at the head end of...
Materials Needed: Collected boughs (larger ones for the base, smaller ones for the top), blanket, and clothing bag

Procedure:
1. Lay out a layer of larger boughs on the ground to form the foundation of your bed.
2. Start at the head end of the bed and place the boughs in an overlapping pattern, with their tips pointing toward the head (as shown in Fig. 7).
3. Continue adding layers until the mattress is thick enough for comfort.
4. Cover the bed with a blanket to provide insulation and comfort.
5. Use a bag containing your coat, extra clothes, and sweater as a pillow.

Note: If you do not sleep well after this setup, it may be due to poor-quality food or inadequate preparation by the cook.
balsam bed construction, outdoor sleeping setup

Bedding (3) -

Assemble Bedding Kit
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Items to include in a bedding kit:

• Foam pad (closed cell)
• Sleeping bag
• Air pillow
sleeping, emergency shelter, bedding
Constructing a Comfortable Bed in a Lean-To
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Materials Needed:
• Thick layer of balsam or hemlock fans or branches
• Rubber blankets or ponchos
• Sleeping blanket

Procedure Steps:
1. On the floor of your lean-to, lay a thick layer of balsam or hemlock fans or branches with the...
Materials Needed:
• Thick layer of balsam or hemlock fans or branches
• Rubber blankets or ponchos
• Sleeping blanket

Procedure Steps:
1. On the floor of your lean-to, lay a thick layer of balsam or hemlock fans or branches with the convex side up and the butts of the stems toward the foot of the bed.
2. Thatch this over with more fans by thrusting the butt ends through the first layer at a slight angle toward the head of the bed, so that the soft tips will curve toward the foot of the bed.
3. Ensure the head of your bed is away from the opening of the lean-to and the foot is toward the opening.
4. Over this bed, spread your rubber blankets or ponchos with the rubber side down, followed by your sleeping blanket on top.

Benefits:
• Creates a soft, springy, and fragrant bed for rest.
• Helps protect against cold and discomfort in outdoor settings.
lean-to bed,balsam hemlock bedding,rubber blankets,sleeping arrangements,outdoor shelter
Create a Spring Mattress Using Green Branches
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Small green branches (springy and elastic)
• Hay, straw, dry leaves, or green material
• Rubber blanket or poncho

Procedure:
1. Collect small green branches from any tree that is springy and elastic.
2. Build the...
Materials Needed:
• Small green branches (springy and elastic)
• Hay, straw, dry leaves, or green material
• Rubber blanket or poncho

Procedure:
1. Collect small green branches from any tree that is springy and elastic.
2. Build the mattress as previously described (refer to earlier sections for detailed instructions on mattress construction).
3. Place a thick layer of hay, straw, dry leaves, or even green material on top of the branch base.
4. If using green material, cover it with a rubber blanket or poncho to prevent moisture from seeping through.
5. Add additional layers of blankets for comfort.

Notes:
• This method was successfully used in Kentucky by covering branches with purple blossoms of ironweed and then placing a rubber army blanket over them.
• The result is a comfortable bed that can be as cozy as any traditional mattress.
spring mattress, green branches, bedding, rubber blanket, ironweed, hay, straw

Building Materials (3) -

Cutting and Peeling Tree Bark
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Fell a tree to procure bark.
2. Make a circular cut at the bottom of the trunk and another circular cut at the top.
3. Create a slit connecting the two cuts as described.
4. Use a blunt instrument or stick, whittled to resemble a...
Procedure:

1. Fell a tree to procure bark.
2. Make a circular cut at the bottom of the trunk and another circular cut at the top.
3. Create a slit connecting the two cuts as described.
4. Use a blunt instrument or stick, whittled to resemble a paper-cutter or dull chisel, to insert under the edge of the bark.
5. Carefully peel the bark back from the tree.
6. If transporting the bark over a distance:
- Roll it up tightly.
- Bind the roll with cord or rope.
- Sling the bundle on your back as shown in Fig. 36 for ease of transport.
bark cutting, bark peeling, tree felling, transporting materials
Building Shelters from Wooden Pallets
Reference Source: Paleo Pocalypse
WOODEN PALLET SHELTERS

In lieu of wildcrafting wood for wickiups and cabin-style wooden shelters, an abundant building material is available in the form of a truly omnipresent contraption: the wooden pallet. These flat, wooden shipping...
WOODEN PALLET SHELTERS

In lieu of wildcrafting wood for wickiups and cabin-style wooden shelters, an abundant building material is available in the form of a truly omnipresent contraption: the wooden pallet. These flat, wooden shipping devices are found in every industrialized nation in the world.

• In a pre-apocalyptic state, they can be found behind most businesses that engage in shipping and/or receiving.
• In a post-collapse survival situation, pallets can be repurposed into a variety of shelters, ranging from simple shelters like wickiups, lean-tos, wigwams, and debris huts to more complex wood cabin-style structures.

See preceding page for pictures.
wooden pallets, shelter, survival, building materials, urban, apocalyptic
Optimal Time for Peeling Bark
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. During the winter months, it may be difficult to peel birch bark or other types of tree bark due to cold and dry conditions.
2. The best time to peel bark is during the spring when the sap is flowing, as this makes the process...
Procedure:

1. During the winter months, it may be difficult to peel birch bark or other types of tree bark due to cold and dry conditions.
2. The best time to peel bark is during the spring when the sap is flowing, as this makes the process easier.
3. Many varieties of trees will yield slabs of bark more easily at this time of year.
bark peeling season, sap flow timing, tree bark harvesting

Foundation Construction (3) -

Constructing a Foundation for Severe Winter Shelters
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Steps to Construct a Stable Foundation:

1. Dig Postholes: Dig holes at least three feet deep where you will place your foundation posts.
2. Fill with Broken Stone: Fill these holes with broken stone, which will provide stability and...
Steps to Construct a Stable Foundation:

1. Dig Postholes: Dig holes at least three feet deep where you will place your foundation posts.
2. Fill with Broken Stone: Fill these holes with broken stone, which will provide stability and help keep the structure plumb in severe winters.
3. Place Posts on Stone Base: On top of the broken stone, stack a pile of stones or use wooden posts made from durable materials such as locust, cedar, or chestnut.
4. Alternative Method (No Stone): If using no stone, pack the dirt tightly around the postholes and ensure it slopes away from the house to allow proper drainage.
5. Use Appropriate Wood for Sills: Use chestnut wood for sills as spruce and pine will rot quickly when in contact with damp earth.
6. Ensure Level Foundation: Drive two stakes a short distance beyond the foundation, align them with your foundation lines, and run a string between them to ensure the sill is perfectly level. Adjust until the string represents the correct level for the sill.
7. Set Posts and Sills: Set posts so that their tops are even with the level of the string and place sills on top of the posts. Test the level again, then fill in the dirt around the posts and pack it firmly.
8. Secure Sills to Posts: Spike your sill to the posts securely and repeat this process for opposite sets of posts and sills.
foundation, postholes, stone, wood, level, drainage, chestnut, spruce, pine
Construct Column or Pier Foundations for Efficient Use of Resources
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Construct Column or Pier Foundations:

1. Determine Spacing Requirements: Space piers between 6 to 10 feet apart based on the weight they need to support.
2. Select Material: Choose from masonry or wood, with wood being...
Steps to Construct Column or Pier Foundations:

1. Determine Spacing Requirements: Space piers between 6 to 10 feet apart based on the weight they need to support.
2. Select Material: Choose from masonry or wood, with wood being preferred for its ease of installation and lower resource requirements.
3. Install Piers with Footings: Use appropriate footings (as shown in figure 1-26) under each pier to ensure stability.
4. Add Braces for Elevated Wood Piers: If wood piers are installed 3 feet or more above the ground, install braces as illustrated in figure 1-7 to prevent swaying or collapse.

Notes:
• This method is preferred when time and labor efficiency are critical.
• Ensure that all components are properly secured and aligned for long-term stability.
column foundations, pier foundations, wood piers, foundation spacing, braces for piers
Construct Wall Foundations for Heavy Loads
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Construct Wall Foundations:

1. Assess Load Requirements and Soil Conditions: Determine the weight the foundation must support and evaluate the soil's load-bearing capacity.
2. Select Material: Choose from concrete, rock, brick,...
Steps to Construct Wall Foundations:

1. Assess Load Requirements and Soil Conditions: Determine the weight the foundation must support and evaluate the soil's load-bearing capacity.
2. Select Material: Choose from concrete, rock, brick, or cut stone based on availability and required strength.
3. Build Continuous Heavy Wall Structure: Construct a solid wall that spans the entire length of the building.
4. Add Footing at the Bottom: Ensure the foundation includes a footing to distribute weight evenly across the ground.
5. Use Reinforcements: Incorporate steel-rod reinforcements in all concrete walls and columns for added strength.

Notes:
• This type of wall is typically used only when other foundation types are not feasible due to its high resource requirements (time, labor, material).
• Ensure proper alignment and stability during construction.
wall foundations, heavy loads, foundation construction, concrete walls, steel reinforcement

Signal Tower Construction (3) -

Assembling the Signal Tower Structure
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Additional kite frames (duplicate of first frame)
• Long line or rope
• Guy ropes, stakes, trees, or stones for securing the tower
• Short braces and additional lashing material

Steps:
1. Duplicate Kite Frame:
-...
Materials Required:
• Additional kite frames (duplicate of first frame)
• Long line or rope
• Guy ropes, stakes, trees, or stones for securing the tower
• Short braces and additional lashing material

Steps:
1. Duplicate Kite Frame:
- Construct a second kite frame in the same manner as the first one.

2. Positioning the Frames:
- Lay both frames on the ground with their cross sticks (F, F) on the underside.
- Ensure that the butt ends of the similar poles on each frame are opposite and approximately 5 feet apart.

3. Securing the Frames During Erection:
- Fasten a long line to the point where the two F pieces cross each other.
- Assign scouts or assistants to hold the butt ends from slipping by placing their feet against them while others pull on the ropes to raise the frames into position (as shown in Figs. 81 and 88).

4. Erecting the Frames:
- Carefully raise the frames, ensuring that they are not pulled too far so as to risk falling on workers.
- Once erected, secure them with guy ropes fastened to stakes, trees, or stones, or have assistants hold them in place.

5. Adding Braces and Connecting Frames:
- Attach short braces at the top and bottom of the frames to hold them together (as shown in Fig. 90).
- Temporarily position two additional long sticks (G, G) as legs or braces between the kite frames.

6. Lashing the Braces:
- Mark the center point where the G sticks cross each other and lash them together using the same lashing technique described earlier (Figs. 83–86).
- Securely fasten the G poles at the top and where they intersect with other braces.

7. Final Securing:
- Add additional short braces to reinforce the framework as indicated in Fig. 90.
tower assembly, signal tower construction, lashing technique, scout shelter, woodcraft
Constructing a Kite Frame for a Signal Tower
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Eight straight poles, approximately 4.5 inches in diameter at their base and 16.5 feet long
• Four sticks, each 9 feet long and 2.5 to 3 inches in diameter at the base
• Additional stout sticks (approximately 20–26 pieces)...
Materials Required:
• Eight straight poles, approximately 4.5 inches in diameter at their base and 16.5 feet long
• Four sticks, each 9 feet long and 2.5 to 3 inches in diameter at the base
• Additional stout sticks (approximately 20–26 pieces) for braces and flooring, each about 4.5 feet long

Steps:
1. Prepare the Frame Components:
- Trim all branches from the poles.
- Cut four additional sticks to be 9 feet in length.
- Prepare twenty-six or more stout sticks of approximately 4.5 feet for braces and flooring.

2. Assemble the Kite Frame on the Ground:
- Lay out three of the 4.5-foot sticks (A, B, C) to form the top and bottom rails of a parallelogram.
- Use two of the 9-foot sticks (D, E) as sides of the parallelogram.
- Position A at the top rail, C at the bottom rail, and place D and E vertically on either side.
- Place B beneath D and E to form a base for the platform. Ensure that B is low enough to allow proper height for a railing around the platform.

3. Secure the Frame:
- Allow the ends of all sticks to project slightly from the frame.
- Use lash rope or strong twine to bind the joints together securely.

4. Add Diagonal Supports:
- Take two 16.5-foot poles and place them diagonally across the parallelogram, crossing at the center (forming an X).
- Ensure that the small ends of these poles lie over the ends of A, while their butt ends extend beyond C.
- Securely lash these diagonal supports in place using strong rope or wire.

5. Flatten and Lash Cross Sticks:
- At the center where the diagonals cross (the X), flatten them slightly by scoring with a hatchet, but avoid deep cuts that could weaken the poles.
- Use lash rope to bind the cross sticks securely in place.

6. Lashing Technique:
- Double the lash rope and run the loop under the cross sticks.
- Bring the loose ends up on the other side of the cross sticks, pull them taut, and bend each end in opposite directions (right and left) around the cross sticks.
- Pull both ends through the loop and tie them together with a square knot as shown in Figs. 85 and 86.
kite frame, signal tower, shelter construction, lashing technique, woodcraft, scout tower
Securing and Finalizing the Signal Tower
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Lash rope or wire
• Additional short braces for reinforcement
• Tools such as hatchet, axe, and lashing materials

Steps:
1. Reinforce Cross Sticks at the Center:
- At the center of the parallelogram where the...
Materials Required:
• Lash rope or wire
• Additional short braces for reinforcement
• Tools such as hatchet, axe, and lashing materials

Steps:
1. Reinforce Cross Sticks at the Center:
- At the center of the parallelogram where the diagonals cross (the X), ensure that the poles are flattened slightly by scoring with a hatchet.
- Avoid deep cuts to prevent weakening the structure.

2. Lashing Technique for Cross Sticks:
- Use double lash rope and run the loop under the cross sticks.
- Bring both loose ends up on the other side of the cross sticks, pull them taut, and bend each end in opposite directions (right and left) around the cross sticks.
- Pull both ends through the loop and tie them together with a square knot as shown in Figs. 85 and 86.

3. Secure All Joints:
- Ensure that all joints are bound securely using lash rope, wire, or other strong fastening materials.
- If using wire, it provides greater security than nails without the risk of splitting poles.

4. Add Final Braces and Flooring:
- Attach additional short braces to reinforce the structure as indicated in Fig. 90.
- Use the remaining stout sticks (approximately 4.5 feet long) for flooring or additional bracing where needed.

5. Final Inspection and Securing:
- Ensure that all parts of the tower are bound together securely, with no loose joints or weak points.
- If the tower is intended to be permanent, it may support a camp or cache as shown in Fig. 87.
- Simplify diagrams by using only a few lines for the frame to avoid confusion from too many poles.
tower reinforcement, signal tower construction, lashing technique, woodcraft, scout shelter

Tree House Construction (3) -

Considerations for Building a Tree House in High-Wind Areas
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Important considerations when building a tree house in high-wind areas:

• Select strong trees: Choose trees with heavy, sturdy trunks that can withstand the force of wind and storms.

• Secure all braces and supports: Ensure that every...
Important considerations when building a tree house in high-wind areas:

• Select strong trees: Choose trees with heavy, sturdy trunks that can withstand the force of wind and storms.

• Secure all braces and supports: Ensure that every brace, cross-sill, and diagonal support is nailed or bolted securely to prevent failure during high winds.

• Avoid extreme weather conditions: During severe storms, tornadoes, or hurricanes, it is not advisable to remain in a tree house. Even the most well-built structure may be vulnerable under such extreme conditions.

• Use additional reinforcement: In areas prone to strong winds, consider adding extra braces or using stronger materials for all structural components.
high wind construction, tree house safety, storm resistance
Construct a One-Tree Tree-Top House
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To construct a one-tree tree-top house:

1. Attach quartered logs to the trunk: Spike two quartered pieces of small log on each side of the tree trunk (refer to Figs. 91 and 92 for visual guidance).

2. Install cross-sills: Lay a couple of...
To construct a one-tree tree-top house:

1. Attach quartered logs to the trunk: Spike two quartered pieces of small log on each side of the tree trunk (refer to Figs. 91 and 92 for visual guidance).

2. Install cross-sills: Lay a couple of poles across these logs and nail them securely to the trunk (Fig. 91). These will serve as the initial support structure.

3. Add perpendicular cross-sills: Place another pair of poles at right angles to the first set, as shown in Fig. 91. Securely nail these in place.

4. Support with braces: Use braces nailed to the trunk below to support the ends of the four poles (Fig. 95). These will form a foundational base for further construction.

5. Add joists and flooring: Lay additional joists across the initial cross-sills, supported by diagonal braces running down to the tree trunk (as shown in Fig. 95). Once the floor is laid over these joists, you can begin constructing the actual shelter on top.

6. Construct the roof: Build any form of shack or structure from a simple open shed to a thatched-roof cottage. Ensure the middle rafters are supported by quartered pieces of logs (as shown in Fig. 92 and 95) for added stability.
tree house, one-tree construction, cross-sills, joists, roof support
Design and Build a Thatch-Roofed Tree House
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
To design and build a thatch-roofed tree house:

1. Use existing foundation: Begin with the cross-sills or joists already installed on one or two trees (as described in previous steps).

2. Construct the frame: Build a sturdy framework for...
To design and build a thatch-roofed tree house:

1. Use existing foundation: Begin with the cross-sills or joists already installed on one or two trees (as described in previous steps).

2. Construct the frame: Build a sturdy framework for your structure, ensuring it is securely attached to the supporting beams and braces.

3. Add thatching material: Use natural materials such as grasses, reeds, or palm fronds to create a thick layer over the roof frame. Secure this with ropes, twine, or wooden pegs.

4. Ensure stability: Reinforce all connections between the structure and the tree trunks using additional braces or supports (as shown in Fig. 97). This is especially important for thatch roofs, which can be heavy and require strong support.

5. Add finishing touches: Include windows, doors, or other features as desired to complete your thatch-roofed tree house.
thatch roof, tree house design, natural materials

Underground Shelters (3) -

Protecting Wood from Decay in Underground Structures
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
### Protecting Wood from Decay in Underground Structures

Materials Needed:
• Boiled linseed oil
• Pulverized charcoal
• Paintbrush or applicator for applying the mixture

Steps to Apply Protective Coating:
1. Mix the Preservative:...
### Protecting Wood from Decay in Underground Structures

Materials Needed:
• Boiled linseed oil
• Pulverized charcoal
• Paintbrush or applicator for applying the mixture

Steps to Apply Protective Coating:
1. Mix the Preservative: In a large container, mix boiled linseed oil with pulverized charcoal until the mixture reaches a thick, paint-like consistency.
2. Apply the Mixture to Wood Surfaces: Use a brush or applicator to evenly coat all exposed wood surfaces on the outside of the hogan frame and any wooden components in contact with damp earth.
3. Allow the Coating to Dry Completely: Ensure that the protective coating is fully dry before proceeding with further construction steps, such as boarding up the sides or adding roofing materials.

Additional Tips:
• This charcoal-based paint can be applied multiple times for added protection against decay and moisture damage.
• It may also be used on wooden fence posts or other outdoor structures exposed to damp conditions.
• Some sources claim that this treatment can extend the life of even easily rotting woods like basswood for many years, making it a valuable technique for long-term underground shelter construction.
wood preservation, decay prevention, boiled linseed oil, charcoal paint, underground wood protection
Boarding Up the Sides of an Underground Hogan
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
### Boarding Up the Sides of an Underground Hogan

Materials Needed:
• Planks or wooden boards for siding
• Nails and hammer
• Temporary supports (if needed)

Steps to Board Up the Sides:
1. **Position the Boards Between Frame and...
### Boarding Up the Sides of an Underground Hogan

Materials Needed:
• Planks or wooden boards for siding
• Nails and hammer
• Temporary supports (if needed)

Steps to Board Up the Sides:
1. Position the Boards Between Frame and Bank: Carefully slip the planks between the hogan's frame and the surrounding earth bank, ensuring that they are properly aligned with the structure.
2. Secure from the Inside if Necessary: If there is not enough space on the outside to swing a hammer, nail the boards from the inside of the hogan where possible.
3. Ensure Proper Alignment and Stability: Make sure each board is securely fastened and that the entire side is covered evenly without gaps or misalignment.

Additional Tips:
• Use temporary supports if needed to hold the planks in place while nailing them down.
• Ensure all boards are tightly secured to prevent any movement or damage from moisture or earth pressure over time.
boarding up hogan, underground shelter siding, planking techniques
Constructing a Safe Underground Hogan Frame
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
### Constructing a Safe Underground Hogan Frame

Materials Needed:
• Two-by-four lumber for posts and framing
• Temporary braces (wooden or metal)
• Nails, hammer, and tools for assembling the frame

Steps to Build the Frame:
1....
### Constructing a Safe Underground Hogan Frame

Materials Needed:
• Two-by-four lumber for posts and framing
• Temporary braces (wooden or metal)
• Nails, hammer, and tools for assembling the frame

Steps to Build the Frame:
1. Prepare the Foundation: Ensure the ground is level and stable before beginning construction.
2. Erect Rear Posts: Place two-by-four posts _A_, _B_ and _C_, _D_ (as shown in Fig. 156) at the rear of the structure. These should be of equal height and tall enough to allow for a sloping roof toward the front, as illustrated in Fig. 155.
3. Install Front Posts: Place shorter posts _E_, _F_ and _G_, _H_ at the front of the structure. Ensure these are tall enough to provide adequate headroom inside the hogan.
4. Add Intermediate Posts for Strength: Depending on the size of the hogan, add one or more additional posts between the rear (_A_, _B_) and front (_C_, _D_) posts if extra strength is required. Repeat this process on both sides of the structure as well.
5. Install Side Posts: Place one, two, or three posts along each side (labeled _M_, _N_ and _O_, _P_ in Fig. 156) to provide additional support and stability for the frame.
6. Secure the Frame with Temporary Braces: Use temporary diagonal braces running from post _B_ to _E_ and from _A_ to _F_ (as shown in Fig. 156). These will help stabilize the structure during construction until it is fully assembled.
7. Ensure a Compact and Strong Box Shape: The framework should be built tightly so that it forms a compact, strong box-like structure. This prevents the surrounding earth from pushing against the sides of the hogan in wet weather and damaging the structure.

Additional Tips:
• Always ensure that the frame is level and square to avoid structural instability.
• Use temporary braces until the frame is fully secured with permanent supports.
• Ensure all posts are properly aligned and spaced for even weight distribution.
cave house, hogan frame, underground shelter, framing, temporary braces, two-by-four posts

Underground Construction (3) -

Adding Ventilation and Light to an Underground Structure
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials: Barrel or keg (with heads knocked out), dirt, small sticks, fine brush, sod, weeds.

Steps:
1. If a window is desired, place it immediately over the entrance or hallway in the frame marked _I_, _K_, _Q_, _S_ (Fig. 156).
2. Remove...
Materials: Barrel or keg (with heads knocked out), dirt, small sticks, fine brush, sod, weeds.

Steps:
1. If a window is desired, place it immediately over the entrance or hallway in the frame marked _I_, _K_, _Q_, _S_ (Fig. 156).
2. Remove the top covering of dirt from the window to admit light, similar to how it is done in the dugout shown in Fig. 142 and the small sketch (Fig. 154).
3. Replace the ventilator shown in Fig. 155 with a chimney for an open fire if desired.
4. Use an open fire instead of a stove to improve ventilation within the underground house.
5. Position the ventilator over a square hole in the roof, using a barrel or keg (with heads knocked out) placed over the hole as needed.
6. After construction is complete, fill in dirt around the edges and pack it down firmly using a piece of scantling (two-by-four or four-by-four) as a rammer.
7. Cover the roof with small sticks, fine brush, and sod covered with growing weeds or grass.
underground ventilation, open fire, barrel ventilator, light window, sod covering
Constructing an Underground Passageway Frame
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials: Logs or lumber for framing, nails, two-by-fours, sticks for ground support.

Steps:
1. Nail _Q_, _S_ across the door-jambs to form the top of the doorway.
2. Install supports _Q_, _R_ and _S_, _T_.
3. Build a frame using _U_,...
Materials: Logs or lumber for framing, nails, two-by-fours, sticks for ground support.

Steps:
1. Nail _Q_, _S_ across the door-jambs to form the top of the doorway.
2. Install supports _Q_, _R_ and _S_, _T_.
3. Build a frame using _U_, _V_, _X_, _W_ and join it to _Q_, _S_ with pieces _Q_, _U_ and _S_, _V_.
4. Add the middle frame support marked _ZZZZ_.
5. Ensure that the passageway is about six feet long.
6. Make the front doorway (marked _U_, _V_, _X_, _W_) large enough to allow comfortable crawling through.
7. Nail the bottom piece _W_, _X_ to a couple of sticks driven into the ground for support.
underground passageway, frame construction, doorway support, two-by-fours
Constructing the Floor of an Underground Passageway
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials: Two-by-fours, shorter pieces of lumber.

Steps:
1. Lay a number of two-by-fours parallel to _B_, _D_ and _F_, _H_ to form the base structure for the floor.
2. Ensure that these boards are level.
3. Use shorter pieces of the same...
Materials: Two-by-fours, shorter pieces of lumber.

Steps:
1. Lay a number of two-by-fours parallel to _B_, _D_ and _F_, _H_ to form the base structure for the floor.
2. Ensure that these boards are level.
3. Use shorter pieces of the same material, running parallel to _F_, _H_ and _W_, _X_, for the hall floor as shown in Fig. 157.
4. Nail the floor securely together as illustrated in Fig. 155.
underground floor construction, two-by-fours, level flooring

Pole House Construction (3) -

Use a Cross-Cut Saw and Froe for Pole House Construction
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Objective: Learn to use a cross-cut saw and froe for constructing a pole house.

Materials Needed:
• A cross-cut saw
• A froe (a tool used for splitting wood)
• Poles or logs for the structure
• Ground space for...
Objective: Learn to use a cross-cut saw and froe for constructing a pole house.

Materials Needed:
• A cross-cut saw
• A froe (a tool used for splitting wood)
• Poles or logs for the structure
• Ground space for construction

Procedure:
1. Familiarize yourself with tools, including how to use a cross-cut saw and a froe.
2. Select poles or logs that are straight, strong, and suitable for building a pole house.
3. Use the cross-cut saw to cut the poles to the required lengths for framing the structure.
4. Utilize the froe to split larger pieces of wood into smaller sections if needed for construction details or joinery.
5. Assemble the framework, using the cut and split poles to build the pole house according to your plans.
6. Secure all joints with appropriate fastenings (e.g., wooden pegs, rope, or other available materials).

Notes:
• This method was used historically for constructing simple, sturdy shelters in remote areas.
• Ensure that tools are sharp and well-maintained to avoid accidents during use.
cross-cut saw, froe usage, pole house building, wooden construction tools, shelter framing
Constructing a Pole House Frame
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Straight poles, beams, nails, planks.

Steps to Construct a Pole House Frame:
1. Erect Corner Posts: Begin by setting up the four corner-posts of the structure. Ensure they are vertical and stable.
2. **Add Intermediate...
Materials Needed: Straight poles, beams, nails, planks.

Steps to Construct a Pole House Frame:
1. Erect Corner Posts: Begin by setting up the four corner-posts of the structure. Ensure they are vertical and stable.
2. Add Intermediate Posts (if needed): Depending on the size and design requirements, add any intermediate posts between the corner-posts for additional support.
3. Attach Plates to Posts: Nail plates on top of each post to help hold the frame together. This is shown in Fig. 172.
4. Fit Additional Posts: After securing the corner and intermediate posts, fit the remaining posts into place as illustrated in the sketches provided.
5. Secure with Nails: Use toe-nailing technique (driving nails slantingly down through the poles to the sill beneath) for added stability, as shown in Fig. 173.
6. Install Top Beam or Side-Plate: Nail the top beam or side-plate across the poles to complete the frame structure, as depicted in Fig. 172.

Notes: This method is suitable for constructing a small pole house without a veranda. Once you have learned how to build larger log houses, you can revisit this page and attempt a more extensive pole house like the one shown in Fig. 171.
pole house, construction, frame building, corner posts, intermediate posts, toe-nailing, beam installation
Sawing Poles on an Angle for Dormer Fit
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Straight poles, measuring tools (tape measure), planks, saw.

Steps to Saw Poles on an Angle:
1. Measure the Height and Base Distance: Measure off the height of the middle pole and the distance along the base from the...
Materials Needed: Straight poles, measuring tools (tape measure), planks, saw.

Steps to Saw Poles on an Angle:
1. Measure the Height and Base Distance: Measure off the height of the middle pole and the distance along the base from the middle pole to the corner at the eaves.
2. Fit Poles Together: Fit the poles you are going to use closely together to cover the measured distance.
3. Secure Temporarily with Planks: Hold the poles in place by nailing a plank temporarily across the bottom ends of the poles.
4. Mark Height Line: Place another plank at the point marked for the height of the middle pole and run it down to the bottom plank, then nail it temporarily along this line.
5. Saw Along Marked Line: Take hold of one end of the saw (as shown in Fig. 174) and have another person take the other end of the saw. Work the saw back and forth along the marked line to cut off the protruding ends of the poles.
6. Repeat for Base-Board: Proceed in the same manner along the base-board to ensure all poles are evenly cut and fitted.
7. Remove Temporary Planks: After securing the poles in place, remove the temporary planks used for marking.

Notes: This method is useful for cutting poles to fit dormers or other angled structures as shown in Fig. 171.
sawing on angle, dormer fit, pole cutting, temporary planks, measuring poles

Door Latch Construction (3) -

Construct a Better Spring-Latch for Doors and Gates
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Wooden catch (_A_)
• Guards (_C_, _D_) made from twig with branch
• Cotton spools (_E_, _F_)
• Stick or peg running through the spools and slot in the door
• Screws
• Thin wedges of wood or small pegs for fastening

**Steps...
Materials Required:
• Wooden catch (_A_)
• Guards (_C_, _D_) made from twig with branch
• Cotton spools (_E_, _F_)
• Stick or peg running through the spools and slot in the door
• Screws
• Thin wedges of wood or small pegs for fastening

Steps to Construct:
1. Create a wooden catch (_A_) and attach it to the door.
2. Make guards from a twig with a branch, splitting the twig in half and securing at the base with two screws; secure the upper end where the branch is bent down with one screw.
3. Attach cotton spools (_E_ on the outside of the door, _F_ on the inside) to each end of the stick that runs through a slot in the door.
4. Run the stick through the spools and the slot in the door, ensuring it also passes through a hole in the spring-latch (_B_).
5. Fasten the stick at the end using thin wedges of wood or by running a small peg through the protruding end (as shown at _F_, _G_ in Fig. 197).
6. Use a peg or nail at point _H_ to force the springy end of the latch downward and a peg or nail at point _K_ to lift the tail end upward, creating tension.
7. This tension will cause the latch to naturally push into the wooden catch (_A_), keeping the door closed unless the spool is lifted in the slot to release it.

Notes:
• The latch can be adjusted for spring strength by adjusting the position of pegs at _H_ and _K_.
• This design is more secure than the simple spring-latch and suitable for camp or cabin use.
better spring-latch, door latch construction, cotton spool, wooden catch, guard design
Construct a Simple Spring-Latch for Doors and Gates
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Peg (driven into the door-jamb)
• Piece of hickory (_B_)
• Screws

Steps to Construct:
1. Drive a peg (_A_) into the door-jamb.
2. Ensure the outer end of the peg has a notch cut into it.
3. Take a piece of hickory...
Materials Required:
• Peg (driven into the door-jamb)
• Piece of hickory (_B_)
• Screws

Steps to Construct:
1. Drive a peg (_A_) into the door-jamb.
2. Ensure the outer end of the peg has a notch cut into it.
3. Take a piece of hickory (_B_) and fasten it to the door using two screws.
4. The hickory should be positioned so that its inner end can slide into the notch on the peg.
5. When pushing the door, the latch will slide out of the rounded notch, allowing the door to open.
6. When pulling the door to close it, the spring-loaded hickory will strike the rounded end of the peg and drop naturally into the slot, holding the door closed.

Notes:
• This is a simple but effective latch for doors and gates.
• Ensure the notch on the peg is properly sized to fit the hickory piece.
spring-latch, door latch, gate latch, hickory, peg, shelter construction
Constructing the Tippecanoe Latch
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Well-seasoned wood (preferably hickory for the spring)
• Wooden bolt with offset shoulder
• Foundation block
• Two wooden clamps
• Nails or screws
• Peg

Steps to Construct the Tippecanoe Latch:
1. **Prepare the Spring...
Materials Required:
• Well-seasoned wood (preferably hickory for the spring)
• Wooden bolt with offset shoulder
• Foundation block
• Two wooden clamps
• Nails or screws
• Peg

Steps to Construct the Tippecanoe Latch:
1. Prepare the Spring Stick:
- Cut a piece of well-seasoned hickory wood, approximately 50% longer than shown in Fig. 201.
- Flatten the upper end and secure it with two nails.
- Flatten the lower end at right angles to the upper part and point it slightly for insertion into a notch in the bolt (Fig. 201).

2. Create the Foundation Block:
- Cut the foundation block as shown in Fig. 204.
- Securely nail or screw this block to the door.

3. Prepare and Attach Clamps:
- Cut two wooden clamps as shown in Figs. 205 and 206.
- Notch the clamps appropriately:
- The notch in Fig. 205 should be slightly larger than the butt end of the bolt.
- The notch in Fig. 206 should be slightly smaller than the opposite end of the bolt.
- Fasten both clamps to the door and also to the foundation block (Fig. 204).

4. Install the Bolt:
- Insert the wooden bolt into the clamps, ensuring that the offset shoulder on the bolt aligns with the notch in the clamps.
- The bolt should be able to slide freely within the slot but be stopped by the shoulder when the spring is loosened.

5. Attach the Peg:
- Insert the peg into the slot of the bolt so that it protrudes on the outside of the door (Fig. 202).
- Ensure that the peg can slide to the opposite end of the slot to allow the door to open.

6. Test the Latch:
- Slide the peg to the opposite end of the slot and verify that the bolt moves freely.
- Test the spring's tension by repeatedly opening and closing the latch.

Notes:
• The wooden spring will not rust or rot if kept dry, making it a durable alternative to metal latches.
• This latch is designed for simplicity and ease of use, requiring only basic tools like a jack-knife.
Tippecanoe latch, wooden spring, door latch construction, foundation block, clamps, bolt installation, peg positioning

Roofing Repairs (3) -

Constructing Roof Patches Around Chimneys
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Cement
• Galvanized caps and nails (preferred)
• Square caps (for better binding surface)
• Roofing compound, tin, oilcloth, tar paper, or paroid
• Tin cans (to create washers if needed)
• Wire nail
• Soft wood for punching...
Materials Required:
• Cement
• Galvanized caps and nails (preferred)
• Square caps (for better binding surface)
• Roofing compound, tin, oilcloth, tar paper, or paroid
• Tin cans (to create washers if needed)
• Wire nail
• Soft wood for punching holes in washers

Steps:
1. Cutting the Material:
- Take a piece of roofing material (e.g., tar paper or oilcloth) and cut it as shown in Fig. 292.
- Make slits at both ends of the material so that when the upper ends are bent back, they will fit snugly around the chimney.

2. Bending the Material:
- For the sides of the chimney, bend the upper ends of the material back as shown in Fig. 292 to create a snug fit around the chimney.
- For the ridge of the roof where the chimney meets it, cut pieces in the same manner but bend them differently:
- The upper lobe is bent on the bias to fit the chimney.
- The lower lobe is bent over the ridge of the roof (as shown in Figs. 293 and 294).

3. Installation:
- Use two pieces for each side of the chimney, totaling four pieces (two at each end) to cover the ridge.
- Tack the patches around the chimney in place as shown in Fig. 295.
- Trim off any protruding ends according to the dotted lines in the figure.

4. Creating Washers from Tin Cans:
- If tin washers are not available, use tin cans instead:
- Knock the cans apart at their seams and cut them into rectangular pieces (as shown in Fig. 301).
- Bend these pieces in the center to make squares.
- Place them on a piece of soft wood and punch holes through them by driving a wire nail through the tin.
- These homemade washers are more effective than store-bought ones, even if they are not as aesthetically pleasing.
roof patching,chimney flashing,tar paper,washer making,roof repair
Repairing a Leaking Roof with Splints and Pegs
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Jack-knife
• Wooden pegs or splints (approximately 6 inches long, slightly thicker than a pipe-stem)

Steps:
1. Preparing the Materials:
- Use a jack-knife to whittle a number of wooden pegs or splints from...
Materials Required:
• Jack-knife
• Wooden pegs or splints (approximately 6 inches long, slightly thicker than a pipe-stem)

Steps:
1. Preparing the Materials:
- Use a jack-knife to whittle a number of wooden pegs or splints from available wood.
- Each peg should be about six inches in length and slightly thicker than a pipe-stem.

2. Applying the Repair:
