- When the roof begins to leak, use these wooden pegs or splints to patch the damaged areas of the roof.
- Insert the pegs into the gaps or cracks where the roofing material has deteriorated.
- Secure them in place using nails or additional fasteners if available.

3. Additional Tips:
- If shingles are missing or damaged, replace them with new ones as needed.
- Ensure that all repairs are made to prevent further water damage and prolong the life of the roof.
roof repair,wooden pegs,leak repair,shingle replacement
Using Patent Roofing Materials for Flashings and Chimneys
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Applying Flashings Using Patent Roofing Materials:

1. Select appropriate patent roofing materials as a substitute for lead or copper if metal is unavailable.
2. Align the strips of roofing material with the angle formed by the...
Procedure for Applying Flashings Using Patent Roofing Materials:

1. Select appropriate patent roofing materials as a substitute for lead or copper if metal is unavailable.
2. Align the strips of roofing material with the angle formed by the object to be flashed (e.g., chimney).
3. Extend the roofing strip up the object by three to four inches from the base.
4. Secure the strips to the roof using standard methods, such as nailing cleats of wood over the top edges.

Important Notes:
• This method is effective for repairing leaks in chimneys and other structures.
• Diagrams (Figs. 295 and 297) provide visual guidance on how to apply these materials correctly.
flashings repair, patent roofing material, chimney sealing, roofing maintenance

Construction Materials (3) -

Identify Common Lumber Grades and Their Uses
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Identify common lumber, which is suitable for general construction and utility purposes.

2. Recognize the following grades:
- No. 1 common: Suitable for use without waste; sound and tightknotted; may be considered...
Procedure:

1. Identify common lumber, which is suitable for general construction and utility purposes.

2. Recognize the following grades:
- No. 1 common: Suitable for use without waste; sound and tightknotted; may be considered watertight lumber.
- No. 2 common: Less restricted in quality than No. 1 but of similar general quality; used for framing, sheathing, and other structural forms where stress or strain is not excessive.
- No. 3 common: Permits some waste with larger grade characteristics than No. 2; used for rough work such as footings, guardrails, and rough flooring.
- No. 4 common: Permits waste and is of low quality; admits coarse features like decay and holes; used for sheathing, subfloors, and roof boards in cheaper construction; also used for boxes and crates.
common lumber grades, construction materials, utility lumber
Identify Standard Sizes of Lumber (New Standard)
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Refer to Table 1-1 for nominal and minimum-dressed sizes of dry, softwood lumber.

2. Understand that dry lumber is defined as having a moisture content of 19 percent or less.

3. Recognize that green lumber (with moisture...
Procedure:

1. Refer to Table 1-1 for nominal and minimum-dressed sizes of dry, softwood lumber.

2. Understand that dry lumber is defined as having a moisture content of 19 percent or less.

3. Recognize that green lumber (with moisture content exceeding 19%) has slightly greater dressed dimensions.

4. Use this information for uniformity in planning structures and ordering materials.
lumber sizes, standard lumber dimensions, dry lumber, green lumber, construction materials
Select Lumber Grades and Their Uses
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Identify select lumber, which is of good appearance and finish.

2. Recognize the following grades:
- Grade A: Suitable for natural finishes; practically clear.
- Grade B: Suitable for natural finishes; high...
Procedure:

1. Identify select lumber, which is of good appearance and finish.

2. Recognize the following grades:
- Grade A: Suitable for natural finishes; practically clear.
- Grade B: Suitable for natural finishes; high quality, generally clear.
- Grade C: Adapted to high quality paint finishes.
- Grade D: Suitable for paint finishes between higher finishing grades and common grades; contains characteristics of both.
select lumber grades, construction materials, lumber quality

Construction Tools (3) -

Install Driftpins in Timber Structures
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Driftpins are long, heavy threadless pins used to hold heavy pieces of timber together. They have heads and vary in diameter from 1/2 to 1 inch, and in length from 18 to 26 inches.

Procedure:
1. Prepare the timber: Ensure that the...
Driftpins are long, heavy threadless pins used to hold heavy pieces of timber together. They have heads and vary in diameter from 1/2 to 1 inch, and in length from 18 to 26 inches.

Procedure:
1. Prepare the timber: Ensure that the timber is properly cut and ready for installation.
2. Drill a hole: Make a hole slightly smaller than the diameter of the driftpin in the timber.
3. Insert the driftpin: Drive the driftpin into the hole until it is fully seated.
4. Secure with compressive action: The pin is held in place by the natural compressive action of the wood fibers as they settle around the pin.

Notes:
• Driftpins are ideal for heavy timber joints where traditional fasteners may not provide sufficient strength.
• Ensure that the hole size matches the diameter of the pin to avoid slippage or improper installation.
driftpins, timber connectors, construction tools, shelter building
Install Timber Connectors for Structural Joints
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Timber connectors are metal devices used to increase the joint strength in timber structures. They simplify the design of heavy construction and provide greater efficiency.

Types of timber connectors:
• Split rings: Commonly used for...
Timber connectors are metal devices used to increase the joint strength in timber structures. They simplify the design of heavy construction and provide greater efficiency.

Types of timber connectors:
• Split rings: Commonly used for connecting timbers together.
• Toothed rings: Provide additional grip and holding power.

Procedure for installation:
1. Select the appropriate connector: Choose a connector based on the type of joint (timber-to-timber or timber-to-steel) and the load it will carry.
2. Prepare the joint surfaces: Ensure that both pieces of timber are clean, dry, and properly aligned for connection.
3. Position the connector: Place the connector over the joint where the two pieces of timber meet.
4. Secure the connector: Use appropriate tools or methods (e.g., nails, screws) to fasten the connector securely in place.

Advantages:
• Simplify the design and construction of heavy timber structures.
• Reduce the amount of timber and hardware needed for connections.
• Save time and labor during installation.

Notes:
• Timber connectors are ideal for situations where traditional framing may be complex or inefficient.
• Proper selection of connector type is critical to ensure structural integrity and load-bearing capacity.
timber connectors, construction tools, shelter building, joint reinforcement
Use Corrugated Fasteners for Timber Joints
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Corrugated fasteners are used to strengthen joints and splices in small timber and boards, particularly miter joints. They are made of sheet metal with alternate ridges and grooves.

Types of corrugated fasteners:
• Parallel ridge type:...
Corrugated fasteners are used to strengthen joints and splices in small timber and boards, particularly miter joints. They are made of sheet metal with alternate ridges and grooves.

Types of corrugated fasteners:
• Parallel ridge type: Ridges run parallel to one another (fig. 1-21, item 1).
• Angled ridge type: Ridges run at a slight angle to one another (fig. 1-21, item 2). This type may compress the material due to closer ridges and grooves at the top.

Size specifications:
• Width: 5/8 to 1-1/8 inches.
• Length: 1/4 to 3/4 inch.
• Number of ridges: Three to six per fastener.

Procedure for use:
1. Prepare the joint: Ensure that the boards or timbers are properly aligned and ready for connection.
2. Insert the fastener: Place the corrugated fastener into the joint, ensuring it is fully seated in the groove or ridge of the timber pieces.
3. Secure the joint: The ridges and grooves will grip the wood, providing a strong hold without splitting the material.

Applications:
• Fastening parallel boards (e.g., table tops).
• Strengthening any type of joint.
• Replacing nails in small timber to avoid splitting.

Notes:
• Corrugated fasteners provide greater holding power than nails in small timber.
• The angled ridge type may be more suitable for certain applications due to its compressive properties.
corrugated fasteners, timber joints, construction tools, shelter building

Permanent Shelter Construction (2) -

Construct a Semi-Permanent Hut for Long-Term Use
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Title: Construct a Semi-Permanent Hut for Long-Term Use

Overview: A semi-permanent hut is suitable for longer stays in cold or inclement weather and requires more time and resources to build.

Materials Required:
• Round poles (for the...
Title: Construct a Semi-Permanent Hut for Long-Term Use

Overview: A semi-permanent hut is suitable for longer stays in cold or inclement weather and requires more time and resources to build.

Materials Required:
• Round poles (for the framework)
• Battens (for thatching support)
• Vines, bark strips, or other fibrous material (for lashings)
• Thatching materials (long grass, reeds, palm leaves, etc.)
• A good knife, hatchet, or axe

Steps:
1. Plan the Structure: Determine the size and shape of your hut based on the number of people to be sheltered and local climatic conditions.
2. Gather Materials: Collect all required materials in advance and stack them in organized piles for easy access during construction.
3. Organize Labor: Divide tasks among group members (e.g., cutting poles, gathering thatching material, lashing framework).
4. Build the Framework: Construct a sturdy rectangular or pyramidal frame using round poles. Secure corners with lashings and ensure the structure is stable.
5. Add Battens and Thatching: Attach battens to the framework for support and layer thatching materials over them in overlapping rows to create a weatherproof roof.
6. Install Doors, Windows, and Fireplace: Add doors (with hinges), windows, and a fireplace for heating if needed.
7. Complete Interior Details: Install built-in bunks or sleeping areas as required.

Notes: This type of hut may take two to three days to construct with multiple people working together.
semi-permanent hut, cold weather shelter, long-term shelter, bushcraft, survival construction
Staking Out a Foundation for a Sawed-Lumber Shanty
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Two-by-four lumber (2 inches thick by 4 inches wide)
• Sills (four inches by four inches, made by nailing two two-by-fours together)
• Tape measure or ruler
• Stakes and string for marking
• Post-hole...
Materials Needed:
• Two-by-four lumber (2 inches thick by 4 inches wide)
• Sills (four inches by four inches, made by nailing two two-by-fours together)
• Tape measure or ruler
• Stakes and string for marking
• Post-hole digger

Procedure:
1. Stake Out the Foundation:
- Mark out the area where the shanty will be built.
2. Ensure Square Corners:
- Measure six feet from one corner along one side and eight feet from the same corner along the adjacent side.
- Use a tape measure or ruler to mark these points.
3. Check for Right Angles:
- Measure diagonally between the two marked points with a ten-foot rod.
- If the rod reaches exactly across, the corner is square and ready for post-hole digging.
4. Dig Post-Holes:
- Once corners are confirmed to be square, dig post-holes at each corner and along the foundation line as needed.

Notes:
• This method ensures a stable and properly aligned foundation for your shanty.
• Adjust dimensions based on convenience or available materials.
foundation,stake out,square corners,two-by-four,post-hole digging

Lean-To Huts (2) -

Constructing Permanent Lean-To Huts Using a Tree for Bracing
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
### Materials and Dimensions

• Ridge pole: 3" to 4" [7.5 to 10 cm] in diameter.
• Forked poles: Not less than 3" to 4" [7.5 to 10 cm] in diameter.
• Thatching battens: 1" to 2" [2.5 to 5 cm].

### Assembly Steps

1. Raise the ridge pole...
### Materials and Dimensions

• Ridge pole: 3" to 4" [7.5 to 10 cm] in diameter.
• Forked poles: Not less than 3" to 4" [7.5 to 10 cm] in diameter.
• Thatching battens: 1" to 2" [2.5 to 5 cm].

### Assembly Steps

1. Raise the ridge pole against a tree using two end forked poles at an angle of not less than 45°.
2. The height of the ridge pole should be between eight to ten feet, depending on the width of the hut.
3. If the length of the ridge is more than 10' to 12' [3 to 3.6 m], add one or two forked poles about halfway along for support.
4. Lash a crossbar ("A") onto the end forked poles and lash it again to the upright tree.
5. Attach pole ("B") to the crossbar, connecting it to the ridge, front eaves pole ("C"), and front thatching battens.
6. Thatching battens are lashed on to the two rear forks with a distance between them of 6" to 12" [15 to 30 cm], depending on the length of thatching material being used. A general guide is one-fourth of the average length of the thatching material.
7. Place an upright in the form of a light fork under the front corners to support the front eave pole.
8. Lash wall thatch battens horizontally from the rear forked poles to this upright to wall in the ends of the hut.
9. Drive wall pegs into the ground along the rear at whatever height is required and lash thatching battens to these pegs.

### Tips
• Use dry timber or dead timber wherever possible for easier handling and to avoid destruction of the bush.
• Bevel off the head of wall pegs so they drive into the ground without splitting.
permanent lean-to, tree bracing, ridge pole, forked poles, crossbar, thatching battens, wall pegs
Constructing Sectional Lean-To Huts
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
### Materials and Dimensions

• Use battens for frames.
• Each frame should be between 7' to 9' [2.1 to 2.7 m] in length and 3' 6" to 4' [1 to 1.2 m] deep.
• Frames are lashed onto two fork sticks with hooks at the upper end.

### **Assembly...
### Materials and Dimensions

• Use battens for frames.
• Each frame should be between 7' to 9' [2.1 to 2.7 m] in length and 3' 6" to 4' [1 to 1.2 m] deep.
• Frames are lashed onto two fork sticks with hooks at the upper end.

### Assembly Steps

1. Construct two or three frames depending on whether it is a one-person or two-person shelter.
2. Attach each frame to a crossbar and framework of sections, ensuring that the top of the top frame projects forward beyond the fork and ridge.
3. This projection protects the front frame and saves the work of ridge thatching.
4. If raised bunks are being installed:
- Raise the bottom of the thatch about 1' 6" to 2' [45 to 60 cm] above ground.
- This increases the ridge height by 1' [30 cm] to 1' 6" [45 cm].
- Side poles become 10' to 11' [3 to 3.3 m] instead of 8' to 9' [2.4 to 2.7 m].

### Tips
• Use dry or dead timber for easier handling and to avoid damaging the bush.
• Ensure that all lashings are secure to maintain structural integrity.
sectional lean-to, shelter construction, frames, fork sticks, crossbar, ridge thatching, raised bunks

Thatch Huts (2) -

Constructing Door Frames in Thatch Huts
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Materials Required:
• Fork sticks
• Upright poles
• Lashing material (e.g., vines, rope)

Steps to Construct a Door Frame:
1. Prepare the fork sticks and uprights:
- Use two uprights lashed across the fork for the door frame.
2....
Materials Required:
• Fork sticks
• Upright poles
• Lashing material (e.g., vines, rope)

Steps to Construct a Door Frame:
1. Prepare the fork sticks and uprights:
- Use two uprights lashed across the fork for the door frame.
2. Secure horizontal thatching battens:
- Attach horizontal thatching battens to these uprights using lashing material.
3. Hinge mechanism:
- The hinging of the door frame is obtained by a combination of hook and fork.
4. Refinements:
- Refinements such as doors are completely practical in thatch huts with minimal extra work involved.

Notes:
• Doors can be constructed similarly to gate frames but with additional uprights for stability.
door frames, thatch huts, fork sticks, lashing, upright poles
Constructing Window Frames in Thatch Huts
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Materials Required:
• Fork sticks
• Thatch battens
• Lashing material (e.g., vines, rope)

Steps to Construct a Window Frame:
1. Prepare the fork sticks:
- Cut two or three fork sticks off short below the fork, leaving one long end...
Materials Required:
• Fork sticks
• Thatch battens
• Lashing material (e.g., vines, rope)

Steps to Construct a Window Frame:
1. Prepare the fork sticks:
- Cut two or three fork sticks off short below the fork, leaving one long end projecting.
2. Attach thatch battens:
- Lash thatch battens to these fork sticks.
3. Position the window frame:
- Lift the framework and hang it on a thatch batten above the window opening in the hut.
4. Leave space for thatching:
- Leave the area beneath the window unthatched, allowing the window frame to be thatched as a complete unit.
5. Ensure proper overlap:
- Ensure at least six inches of overlap between the window and roof thatch on both sides.
6. Secure loose ends of thatching:
- Allow the loose ends of the thatching above the window frame to fall directly onto the window thatch, completely covering the sewing of the top thatching of the window frame.
7. Propping open:
- If necessary, prop the window open at the bottom while preserving a fair slope.

Notes:
• For very wide windows, use three fork sticks for added stability.
• The window frame should be slightly wider than the opening to allow for proper thatching and structural integrity.
window frames, thatch huts, fork sticks, lashing, thatching battens

Natural Shelter Construction (2) -

Use Ti Leaves for Shelter and Protection
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Locate large ti leaves.
2. Use the leaves to cover shelters or create a rain cloak by overlapping them and securing them with natural fasteners such as vines or sticks.
3. Cut the leaves into liners for shoes, especially useful if...
Procedure:
1. Locate large ti leaves.
2. Use the leaves to cover shelters or create a rain cloak by overlapping them and securing them with natural fasteners such as vines or sticks.
3. Cut the leaves into liners for shoes, especially useful if you have a blister.
4. Fashion temporary sandals from the ti leaves by weaving or tying them together.
5. If the terminal leaf is not completely unfurled, use it as a sterile bandage.
6. Cut the leaves into strips and braid the strips into rope for various uses.

Notes:
• Ti leaves are versatile in survival situations due to their size and strength.
• They can be used for both practical and medical purposes.
ti leaves, shelter construction, rain cloak, shoe liner, sterile bandage
Use Water Lettuce for Survival Purposes
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Locate the floating leaves of a water lettuce plant.
2. Use the large, coarsely toothed leaves as a food source by consuming them raw or cooking them.
3. If necessary, use the plant's structure to create temporary shelters or for...
Procedure:
1. Locate the floating leaves of a water lettuce plant.
2. Use the large, coarsely toothed leaves as a food source by consuming them raw or cooking them.
3. If necessary, use the plant's structure to create temporary shelters or for other survival-related purposes.

Notes:
• Water lettuce is an aquatic plant that can be used both as a food source and for shelter in survival situations.
• It grows in freshwater environments and is found in many parts of the world.
water lettuce leaves, survival food, shelter construction

Lean To Shelter (2) -

Construct a Lean-To Shelter Using Natural Materials
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed: Uprooted tree, poles for roof, natural wall (clay, cliff, or bank).

Steps:
1. Identify an uprooted tree with exposed clay roots or use the side of a cliff as a natural wall.
2. Use the exposed clay surface as a foundation...
Materials Needed: Uprooted tree, poles for roof, natural wall (clay, cliff, or bank).

Steps:
1. Identify an uprooted tree with exposed clay roots or use the side of a cliff as a natural wall.
2. Use the exposed clay surface as a foundation for your shelter's wall.
3. Place poles horizontally against the natural wall to form the roof structure of a lean-to.
4. Ensure the doorway is positioned on one side of the shelter (as shown in Fig. 23).
5. If upright poles are used on stony ground, brace them with slanting poles for stability (Fig. 25).

Notes:
• This method utilizes available materials and adapts to the terrain.
• Each campsite presents unique challenges that can enhance the experience of building a shelter.
lean-to, natural materials, shelter construction, clay wall, stony ground, uprooted tree
Construct a Single Lean-to Shelter in Cold Weather
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Objective: Build a single lean-to shelter for cold weather survival.

Materials Needed:
• Live trees (8 inches thick)
• Crosspieces (4–5 inches thick at the butt end, 4 feet longer than distance between uprights)
• Bottom crosspieces (same...
Objective: Build a single lean-to shelter for cold weather survival.

Materials Needed:
• Live trees (8 inches thick)
• Crosspieces (4–5 inches thick at the butt end, 4 feet longer than distance between uprights)
• Bottom crosspieces (same size as front crosspiece)
• Back pieces (2–3 inches thick at the butt end, 10–12 feet long)
• Boughs for insulation
• Canvas or ponchos (optional)
• Logs for fire and reflector wall
• Green logs for fire base

Steps:

1. Select Uprights:
- Choose two live trees about 8 inches thick as uprights.
- The distance between them should be at least 2 feet per person.
- If possible, clear snow to near ground level.

2. Place Top Crosspiece:
- Use a crosspiece that is 4–5 inches thick at the butt end and about 4 feet longer than the distance between uprights.
- Place it on the roof side of the lean-to, approximately 5 feet or shoulder height off the ground.
- If the distance between trees exceeds 15 feet, brace the crosspiece in the center with a pole (3–4 inches in diameter, 6 feet long) resting on solid footing.

3. Place Front Bottom Crosspiece:
- Use a crosspiece of the same size as the top crosspiece to span between uprights.
- Place it on the roof side of the lean-to.
- Its purpose is to prevent sleeping bags from sliding into fire and hold boughs in place.

4. Place Bottom Rear Crosspiece:
- Use a crosspiece of the same size as the front bottom crosspiece, placed about 7 feet from the front bottom crosspiece.
- It helps raise the back of the lean-to roof and holds boughs in place.

5. Install Back Pieces:
- Place two to three pieces (2–3 inches thick at the butt end, 10–12 feet long) with their butt ends on the ground resting against the rear bottom crosspiece.
- The top ends should rest on the top crosspiece and be spaced no more than 18 inches apart.

6. Cover the Roof:
- Shingle boughs from the bottom upward to build up a roof at least six inches thick, or thick enough for a person to walk on.

7. Close in the Sides:
- Use short trees or large tree tips leaning against the sides of the lean-to with the cut or butt end up.

8. Insulate the Ground:
- Place boughs at least six inches thick on the bottom of the lean-to for insulation.
- Use only the tips of branches, placing them sharp or broken ends down and tips pointing up.
- If time is limited or concealment is needed, lay boughs flat but ensure no sharp points or small twigs are sticking up to avoid puncturing sleeping pads.

9. Trim and Bank:
- Trim any branches or bough tips hanging between the backpieces and bank the back and sides of the lean-to with snow for insulation.

10. Secure Roof Covering (Optional):
- If using canvas, ponchos, or other materials to cover the roof, place logs against the backpieces with the covering between them to prevent it from being lifted by wind.

11. Build a Log Fire:
- Place short green logs on the ground as a fire base (same length as the fire).
- Lay two dry logs at least the length of the lean-to and 8–15 inches thick over the green logs.
- Place a green log in the center of the dry logs to hold them together.
- Start the fire in several places to help it spread along the entire length of the logs.
- Chip the surfaces facing each other for better burning.

12. Estimate Firewood Needs:
- If using smaller dry logs, gather three times the amount of firewood you estimate will be needed to keep a fire burning all night.

13. Construct Reflector Wall (Optional):
- Build a reflector wall with green logs the length of the lean-to, approximately 3 feet from the outside edge of the fire.
- This reflects heat into the lean-to and acts as a windbreak.
cold weather shelter, lean to construction, survival shelter, military shelter, insulation, fire building

Radiation Shielding (2) -

Radiation Shielding and Shelter Selection
Critical Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Objective: Select appropriate shelter to maximize radiation shielding.

Shelter Priority (from best to worst):
1. Cave or tunnel covered with 3 or more feet of earth
2. Storm/storage cellars
3. Culverts
4. Basements
5....
Objective: Select appropriate shelter to maximize radiation shielding.

Shelter Priority (from best to worst):
1. Cave or tunnel covered with 3 or more feet of earth
2. Storm/storage cellars
3. Culverts
4. Basements
5. Abandoned stone/mud buildings
6. Foxhole 4 feet deep (remove topsoil within 2 feet radius of foxhole lip)

Radiation Shielding Efficiencies:
• Iron/Steel: 0.7 inches
• Brick: 2.0 inches
• Concrete: 2.2 inches
• Earth: 3.3 inches
• Cinder Block: 5.3 inches
• Ice: 6.8 inches
• Wood (Soft): 8.8 inches
• Snow: 20.3 inches

Each additional thickness of shielding reduces the radiation dose by half.

Steps for Shelter Selection:
1. Find protective shelter immediately.
2. If no shelter is available, dig a trench or foxhole deep enough for protection and enlarge it for comfort (see Figure IX-2).
3. Cover with available materials to increase shielding.
4. Leave contaminated equipment and clothing near the shelter for retrieval after radioactive decay.
5. Lie down, keep warm, sleep, and rest to conserve energy.

Important Notes:
• Avoid exposure to radiation by minimizing time outside of shelter.
• Use natural materials (earth, wood) to increase shielding where possible.
nuclear shelter, radiation protection, emergency shelter
Shielding Windows and Doors in Fallout Shelters
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Purpose: Provide additional radiation shielding for windows and doors in fallout shelters.

Materials Required:
• Loose stacks of concrete blocks

Steps:
1. Assess Window/Door Placement: Determine which windows or doors require...
Purpose: Provide additional radiation shielding for windows and doors in fallout shelters.

Materials Required:
• Loose stacks of concrete blocks

Steps:
1. Assess Window/Door Placement: Determine which windows or doors require shielding based on the shelter's location and potential threats (e.g., nuclear blast, high winds).
2. Place Concrete Blocks: Use loose stacks of concrete blocks to cover windows and doors for radiation protection.
3. Evaluate Stability: In areas with high wind speeds or blast risks, note that these blocks may not remain in place during extreme conditions and should be avoided if possible.

Additional Notes:
• This method is effective against gamma radiation but may not withstand the force of a tornado, hurricane, or nuclear blast.
• For maximum protection, consider eliminating windows entirely in high-risk areas.
window shielding, door shielding, radiation protection, fallout shelter design, concrete block barriers

Natural Materials (2) -

Harvest Balsam Tree Materials for Shelter Construction
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Gather Leaves and Needles: Collect the flat, dark bluish-green leaves (needles) of the balsam tree. These are ideal for making beds or insulation due to their softness and insulating properties.

2. Collect Cones: Gather...
Procedure:

1. Gather Leaves and Needles: Collect the flat, dark bluish-green leaves (needles) of the balsam tree. These are ideal for making beds or insulation due to their softness and insulating properties.

2. Collect Cones: Gather upright cones from branches. These can be used as natural fasteners or decorative elements in shelters if needed.

3. Extract Blisters (Resin): Carefully pop the blisters on the bark of the tree to extract the sticky sap-like substance. This resin is useful for:
- Sealing seams in shelters to make them waterproof.
- Acting as an adhesive for attaching materials together.

4. Harvest Small Branches: Use small branches (up to 3 feet long) for constructing frames or supports within the shelter structure.

5. Use Trunk Sections (if available): If a fallen tree is found, use sections of the trunk as structural support in larger shelters.

Notes:
• Always take only what you need and avoid over-harvesting from any single tree or area.
• Be cautious when handling resin; it can be sticky and may cause skin irritation if not handled properly.
• This procedure is best performed during the spring and summer months when the tree is in full growth and the bark blisters are most active.
harvest,balsam,shelter materials,natural resources,Abies,resin extraction,tree harvesting
Identify and Locate Balsam Trees for Shelter Materials
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Understand the Habitat: Look for balsam trees in mountainous regions, from Labrador down to New England, New York, Virginia, North Carolina, Tennessee, Alaska, along the Pacific and Rocky Mountains, and as far south as...
Procedure:

1. Understand the Habitat: Look for balsam trees in mountainous regions, from Labrador down to New England, New York, Virginia, North Carolina, Tennessee, Alaska, along the Pacific and Rocky Mountains, and as far south as Guatemala.

2. Recognize the Tree: The balsam tree (also known as *Abies*) is a conifer with:
- Leaves that are blunt or rounded at the ends, some dented or notched.
- Leaves about one inch in length and very flat.
- Upper surface of leaves dark bluish-green, under-side light or silvery white.

3. Identify Buds and Cones: The balsam tree produces cones that stand upright on the branches, varying from two to four inches in length. These are found on trees that are generally not very large (rarely over sixty feet tall), with trunks one to less than three feet in diameter.

4. Look for Blisters: The bark of balsam trees is gray and marked with horizontal rows of blisters containing a sticky sap similar to glycerine.

5. Use Local Names: In different regions, the tree may be known as:
- *Sapin* (French-Canadians)
- *Cho-kho-tung* (New York Indians)
- Balsam (tenderfoot)
- Christmas-tree (common name)

6. Seasonal Availability: The balsam tree is most useful during the spring and summer months when its cones are present and the tree is in full growth.

Notes:
• This procedure assumes access to natural environments where balsam trees grow.
• Use caution when identifying trees, as some conifers may look similar but have different properties.
• Always verify identification before using any plant material for shelter or other purposes.
balsam tree,identify,balsam,shelter materials,natural resources,Abies,conifer identification

Over Water Camps (2) -

Constructing Over-Water Camp Foundations Using Barrels
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Constructing Over-Water Camp Foundations Using Barrels:

1. Gather materials: Collect a number of old barrels.
2. Modify the barrels: Knock out the tops and bottoms of the barrels to create open containers.
3. **Attach...
Procedure for Constructing Over-Water Camp Foundations Using Barrels:

1. Gather materials: Collect a number of old barrels.
2. Modify the barrels: Knock out the tops and bottoms of the barrels to create open containers.
3. Attach cross planks: Nail some cross planks on the ends of your spiles (supporting posts).
4. Slide barrels over spiles: Place the modified barrels over the spiles.
5. Set in place: Position the barrels in the water where you want to build your structure.
6. Secure with weight: Fill the barrels with rocks, stones, or coarse gravel to hold them firmly in place and provide stability for the uprights.

Notes:
• This method is particularly useful when building piers or over-water structures on hard bottoms like rock or sand.
• Ensure that the barrels are filled sufficiently to prevent floating or shifting due to water currents.
barrels, over-water camps, foundations, support posts, rocks, stones, gravel
Using Piles for Over-Water Camp Foundations
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Using Piles as Foundations for Over-Water Camps:

1. Drive piles into the bottom: Use wooden mallets or a crowd of people jumping on poles attached to the posts to drive the supporting piles into the water's bottom.
2. **Use a...
Procedure for Using Piles as Foundations for Over-Water Camps:

1. Drive piles into the bottom: Use wooden mallets or a crowd of people jumping on poles attached to the posts to drive the supporting piles into the water's bottom.
2. Use a piledriver if available: If constructing a more permanent structure, use an ordinary piledriver for driving the piles.
3. Secure against ice movement: Ensure that the piles are driven deep enough so that they can withstand the pressure of freezing ice during winter.
4. Avoid water level changes: Be cautious about water levels rising after freezing; this may cause the ice to lift and potentially pull the piles out of the ground.

Notes:
• Piles are more stable than stone piers in icy conditions, as ice tends to slide around them rather than push them over.
• This method is recommended for long-term or seasonal use in areas with freezing temperatures.
piles, over-water camps, foundations, ice movement, water level changes

Building Construction (2) -

Constructing a Barabara Shelter Frame
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Eight short posts for the outside wall
• Six or eight longer posts for inside supports

Steps to Build the Frame:
1. Dig Holes and Plant Posts:
- Dig holes for the posts.
- Plant eight short posts for the...
Materials Needed:
• Eight short posts for the outside wall
• Six or eight longer posts for inside supports

Steps to Build the Frame:
1. Dig Holes and Plant Posts:
- Dig holes for the posts.
- Plant eight short posts for the outside wall, ensuring they stand about three feet above the ground after being planted.
- Cut the top of these posts wedge-shaped to fit into notch _B_ (as shown in Fig. 144).

2. Arrange Front Posts:
- Plant your first four posts for the front of the barabara in a line:
- Two posts at the corners (_B_ and _E_) as shown in Fig. 145 _A_.
- Two posts at the middle of the line (_C_ and _D_) for door-jambs (as per plan, Fig. 145 _A_).
- Ensure the tops of these front posts are level with each other so that a straight log placed over them will lie flat.

3. Plant Side Posts:
- Plant two side-posts (_F_ and _G_) at equal distances from the two front posts, making sure they are a few feet farther apart than the front posts.

4. Set Back Posts:
- Set the two back posts (_H_ and _K_) much closer together so that the bottom frame will be near the shape of a boy's hexagonal kite when rails are placed on the posts.

5. Erect Inside Posts:
- Inside, erect another set of posts opposite the outside ones, about 1.5 to 2 feet farther in (or less if using different materials).

6. Set Door-Jamb Posts:
- Set some additional posts for the hallway or entrance, which will serve as door-jambs.

7. Build Log Roof Frame:
- Secure a rail on the two side-posts at the right and left of the building.
- Secure a back plate on the two back posts at the rear of the building.
- Rest a long log over the side rails at the front of the building, ensuring that the door-posts are tall enough to allow for the thickness of the log so that the front log will rest upon their top.
- Place your two corner logs on and complete the outside rail.

8. Build Inside Rail:
- Build the inside rail in the same manner as the outside rail.

9. Continue Building Up with Logs:
- Continue to build up with logs as shown in the diagram until you have a frame like that in Fig. 145.

Additional Notes:
• The framework is suitable for use in all parts of the country for more or less permanent camps.
• In cold climates, consider using a long entrance and low doorway to enhance insulation.
• For children's playhouses, the long entrance and low doorway are unnecessary but can add to the mystery of the cave house effect.
barabara, shelter frame, log construction, building materials, post placement, roof structure
Install Studs in Frame Structures
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
Steps to Install Studs:

1. Prepare the Structure: Ensure that the sills, posts, plates, and braces are already in place.
2. Layout Window and Door Openings: Before setting the studs, layout the window and door openings.
3. **Measure and...
Steps to Install Studs:

1. Prepare the Structure: Ensure that the sills, posts, plates, and braces are already in place.
2. Layout Window and Door Openings: Before setting the studs, layout the window and door openings.
3. Measure and Mark Stud Positions: Measure from one corner of the sill or sole to determine where the studs will be placed. The distance between studs can range from 1 to 12 feet depending on the type of building, whether girts are used, and the type of finish (inside and outside).
4. Place and Secure Studs: Place the studs and secure them with two 16- or 20-penny nails through the top plate.
5. Considerations for Siding: If vertical or large-panel siding is used, studs can be spaced wider apart, and horizontal girts should be installed between them to provide nailing surfaces.
6. Residential vs. TO Construction: In residential work in the United States, studs are commonly spaced on 16-inch centers. In TO (Tactical Operations) construction, if girts are used, studs are typically spaced 4 feet apart.

Doubling Posts for Door Openings:
• Place the outside studs into position and nail them securely.
• Cut short studs or filler studs to match the size of the opening.
• Nail these filler studs to the inside face of the outside studs as shown in figure 2-4.

Framing Headers for Windows and Doors:
• For window openings, a bottom header must be framed. This can be single or double.
• When doubled, the bottom piece is nailed to the opening studs at the proper height, and the top piece of the bottom header is nailed into place flush with the bottom section.
• The door header is framed as shown in figure 2-4.
• The filler stud rests on the sole at the bottom.
studs, framing, window opening, door opening, header, girts, spacing, construction

Dugout Construction (2) -

Build a Sod-Walled Dugout Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Sod
• Tools for cutting and shaping sod
• Rafters
• Logs or wooden supports

Steps to Build the Sod-Walled Dugout:
1. Dig Out the Site: Choose a level area in a bank and dig out space for the walls of the shelter.

2....
Materials Needed:
• Sod
• Tools for cutting and shaping sod
• Rafters
• Logs or wooden supports

Steps to Build the Sod-Walled Dugout:
1. Dig Out the Site: Choose a level area in a bank and dig out space for the walls of the shelter.

2. Construct Walls with Sod: Pile sod up like stones in a stone wall (as shown in Fig. 154) to form the walls of the dugout. Ensure that the sod is tightly packed and interlocked for stability.

3. Add Rafters and Roofing: Install rafters above the structure for the roof. The roof should be flat, as described in Figs. 54, 55, and 56, and covered with logs or additional layers of sod. In some cases, a log may be pegged to the rafters above the eaves to help hold the sod in place.

4. Complete the Structure: Ensure that all openings (such as doorways) are properly sealed or protected with a shed entranceway if needed.
sod-walled dugout, shelter construction, flat roof, sod covering
Construct a Log Dugout Shelter
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Needed:
• Logs
• Tools for notching logs
• Rafters
• Sod or brush for covering

Steps to Build the Log Dugout:
1. Choose a Location: Select a level area in a bank where you will dig out space for the walls of the shelter.

2....
Materials Needed:
• Logs
• Tools for notching logs
• Rafters
• Sod or brush for covering

Steps to Build the Log Dugout:
1. Choose a Location: Select a level area in a bank where you will dig out space for the walls of the shelter.

2. Build the Walls: Construct the walls using logs, notching them as described in Figs. 162 and 166 to ensure proper interlocking and stability.

3. Add Rafters and Roofing: Install rafters above the structure for the roof. The roof should be flat (as shown in Figs. 54, 55, and 56) and covered with logs or sod. In some cases, a log may be pegged to the rafters above the eaves to help hold the sod in place.

4. Complete the Structure: Ensure that the roof is securely fastened and that all openings (such as doorways) are properly sealed or protected with a shed entranceway if needed.
log dugout, shelter construction, flat roof, sod covering, notching logs

Door Locks And Fastenings (2) -

Construct a Trigger Latch Door Mechanism
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Trigger Latch Construction

1. Materials Required: Wooden shaft, trigger mechanism, hardwood pegs or wire nails, latch bar, screw or nail, door.
2. Assembly Steps:
- Attach the trigger protruding from the lower part of the door to a...
Trigger Latch Construction

1. Materials Required: Wooden shaft, trigger mechanism, hardwood pegs or wire nails, latch bar, screw or nail, door.
2. Assembly Steps:
- Attach the trigger protruding from the lower part of the door to a wooden shaft using hardwood pegs or wire nails that move freely in their sockets.
- Connect the wooden shaft to the latch with similar fastenings (hardwood pegs or wire nails).
3. Latch Installation:
- The latch is a simple wooden bar, fastened at one end with a screw or nail so it can move up and down freely.
- The other end of the latch should drop into the catch when not in use.
4. Trigger Attachment:
- Fasten the trigger to a block on the outside of the door using a nail or peg that allows free movement.
5. Functionality:
- When weight is applied to the trigger from the outside, it pushes the attached end of the shaft upward, which in turn lifts the latch, unfastening the door.
6. Diagram Reference: See Fig. 200 for visual details (left diagram shows the edge of the door with the trigger on the outside and the shaft inside; right diagram shows the inside of the door with the trigger end, shaft, latch, and catch).
trigger latch, door mechanism, wooden shaft, latch installation, fastening method
Install a Latch-String Door Mechanism
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Latch-String Installation

1. Materials Required: Wooden hinge rod, door, sill log, nail or screw.
2. Hinge Construction:
- Nail a round wooden rod to the edge of the door; allow both ends of the rod to project above and below the...
Latch-String Installation

1. Materials Required: Wooden hinge rod, door, sill log, nail or screw.
2. Hinge Construction:
- Nail a round wooden rod to the edge of the door; allow both ends of the rod to project above and below the door.
3. Sill Log Preparation:
- Bore a hole about two inches deep in the sill log below the door to receive the short end of the hinge rod.
- Bore a deeper hole in the sill log above the door to receive the long end of the hinge rod.
4. Door Hanging Process:
- Insert the long end of the hinge rod into the top hole until the door is lifted enough to drop the lower end of the hinge rod into the lower hole.
5. Latch-String Setup:
- The latch-string hangs outside the door, allowing anyone to pull it and enter.
- When the owner is inside and does not want to be interrupted, they can pull the string in, signaling others to knock before entering.
6. Functionality: This simple mechanism allows for easy access while also providing a way to signal when entry should be avoided.
7. Diagram Reference: See Figs. 193 and 194 for visual details.
latch string, door mechanism, wooden hinge, sill log installation, simple latch

Roofing Methods (2) -

How to Make Shingles from Logs
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Saw up a hemlock, pine, or spruce log into billets of one foot four inches long.
2. Use a froe and mall (Fig. 179) to split the shingles from the billets of wood.
3. Alternatively, use a broadaxe for splitting the...
Procedure:

1. Saw up a hemlock, pine, or spruce log into billets of one foot four inches long.
2. Use a froe and mall (Fig. 179) to split the shingles from the billets of wood.
3. Alternatively, use a broadaxe for splitting the shingles.

Details:
• Broadaxes are dangerous weapons in the hands of an amateur.
• The author successfully split shingles with a broadaxe at age seven and still has all ten toes and fingers.
shingle making, log splitting, roofing materials
How to Prepare a Thatched Roof
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure:

1. Collect poles about four inches in diameter.
2. Flatten both sides of the poles.
3. Nail the flattened poles to the rafters, ensuring they are no more than two inches apart.
4. Allow the ends of the sticks to extend beyond the...
Procedure:

1. Collect poles about four inches in diameter.
2. Flatten both sides of the poles.
3. Nail the flattened poles to the rafters, ensuring they are no more than two inches apart.
4. Allow the ends of the sticks to extend beyond the walls of the house at least six inches.

Details:
• If you intend to thatch the roof, there is no need to flatten the poles fastened across the rafters because the thatch will hide all unevenness of the underpinning.
• Lay the poles at right angles to the rafters between six and eight inches apart.
• Thatch the roof as described and illustrated by Fig. 66.
thatched roof, roofing materials, log cabin thatching

Plumbing Repairs (2) -

Repairing Leaks in Farm Equipment and Roof Leaders
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Repairing Leaks in Tubs, Barrels, Tanks, and Roof Leaders:

1. Identify the location of the leak in tubs, barrels, tanks, or roof leaders.
2. Use rags to cover the leak and secure them with tin if necessary.
3. Apply white lead...
Procedure for Repairing Leaks in Tubs, Barrels, Tanks, and Roof Leaders:

1. Identify the location of the leak in tubs, barrels, tanks, or roof leaders.
2. Use rags to cover the leak and secure them with tin if necessary.
3. Apply white lead paint over the entire patched area to seal it effectively.
4. For roof leaders, avoid replacing old pipes with galvanized-steel tubes as they are not durable for outdoor use.
5. If replacement is unavoidable, prefer copper or galvanized iron over galvanized steel, as copper lasts longer and provides better value in the long run.
6. Galvanized iron can be sourced from specialized manufacturers if needed, though it may require assistance from an architect to locate suppliers.

Important Notes:
• Galvanized steel is not recommended for outdoor use due to its short lifespan (2–3 years) and susceptibility to damage in one season.
• Patching old tin or galvanized pipes is more economical than replacing them with galvanized steel.
• Copper, while more expensive initially, proves more cost-effective over time.
farm equipment repair, roof leader replacement, galvanized steel vs copper, plumbing materials
Repairing a Leaky Drain Pipe Using White Lead and Linen
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Procedure for Repairing a Leaky Drain Pipe:

1. Identify the location of the leak in the drain pipe.
2. Use a hammer to carefully tap the damaged area of the lead pipe, attempting to close the hole as much as possible.
3. Apply white lead paint...
Procedure for Repairing a Leaky Drain Pipe:

1. Identify the location of the leak in the drain pipe.
2. Use a hammer to carefully tap the damaged area of the lead pipe, attempting to close the hole as much as possible.
3. Apply white lead paint generously over the damaged section of the pipe.
4. Cut a strip of linen and place it directly over the leak.
5. Wrap the linen tightly around the pipe, ensuring full coverage of the leak.
6. Paint the entire wrapped area with white lead to seal it completely.

Important Notes:
• White lead is crucial for sealing leaks effectively; other materials like tin or rags are only used to protect and hold the white lead in place.
• This method has been successfully used for over six years without further issues.
• Avoid using tar, as it can run when heated by sunlight and is less effective than white lead.
leak repair, drain pipe fix, white lead application, linen patching, plumbing maintenance

Tent Setup (2) -

Establish Shelter and Set Up Tent Away from Landing Area
High Priority Source: US Antarctic Field Manual
Procedure:

1. Establish a shelter.
2. Set up a tent away from the landing area.

Details:
• This ensures safety by keeping the camp site clear of potential hazards associated with aircraft operations.
tent setup, shelter
Constructing an Open Forester Tent
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
1. Cutting the poles: Cut three small saplings, one twelve feet long and two ten feet long.

2. Creating shear poles: Tie the two ten-foot poles together at their ends to form a "shears" structure.

3. Setting up the ridge pole: Take the...
1. Cutting the poles: Cut three small saplings, one twelve feet long and two ten feet long.

2. Creating shear poles: Tie the two ten-foot poles together at their ends to form a "shears" structure.

3. Setting up the ridge pole: Take the twelve-foot pole and run it down the ridge inside the tent, and out through the hole in the back.

4. Assembling the frame: Raise the ridge pole with one end stuck in the ground and the front end resting on the two shear poles. Tie all three together at the intersection point.

5. Attaching eyelet holes: At the end of each seam along the hem, work in a little eyelet hole for a short piece of twine to tie to the tent pegs.

6. Stretching and securing the tent: Stretch out the back triangle and peg it down at the two corners on the ground. Peg out each hole along the foot until the entire tent stretches out taut.

7. Creating the gambrel roof: Three feet from the peak along the front edge, create another eyelet hole with a short piece of twine. Tie this to the shear pole on each side to form the gambrel roof shape, which provides more interior space.

8. Final check: Ensure that the tent is fully stretched and secured as shown in illustrations.
tent setup, forester tent, shelter construction, camping, outdoor survival

Emergency Shelter (2) -

Assemble Essential Shelter & Warmth Items
High Priority Source: Home Survival Kit Checklist v3 2 Page
Procedure:

1. Include waterproof tarps for creating temporary shelters or covering items from the rain.
2. Acquire a survival blanket to retain body heat in cold environments.
3. Store a sleeping bag or bivvy for rest and warmth...
Procedure:

1. Include waterproof tarps for creating temporary shelters or covering items from the rain.
2. Acquire a survival blanket to retain body heat in cold environments.
3. Store a sleeping bag or bivvy for rest and warmth during outdoor emergencies.
4. Keep firewood on hand for starting fires in cold conditions.
5. Include matches, lighters, and ferro rods for reliable fire-starting methods.
6. Acquire a magnifying glass to start fires using sunlight.
waterproof tarps,survival blanket,sleeping bag,firewood,matches,lighter,ferro rod,magnifying glass
Assemble Shelter Kit
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Items to include in a shelter kit:

• Tent
• Tent fly
• Tent poles
• Tent pegs
• Ground cloth
• Ultra lightweight tarp
• Visk clamps
• Nylon line, 50 ft. (2 ea)
tent, shelter, emergency tent

Fallout Shelter Construction (2) -

Construct Permanent Exterior Below-ground Shelter for Fallout Protection
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Build a permanent exterior below-ground shelter that provides high protection from blast, radiation, and fallout.

Materials Required:
• Concrete or reinforced masonry materials
• Dirt (at least 3 feet depth for roof slab)
-...
Objective: Build a permanent exterior below-ground shelter that provides high protection from blast, radiation, and fallout.

Materials Required:
• Concrete or reinforced masonry materials
• Dirt (at least 3 feet depth for roof slab)
• Waterproofing materials
• Floor drain system
• Air vents (disguised as patio features)
• Hatch door (preferably disguised as a bench/table)

Steps to Construct:
1. Location Selection:
- Choose a site far from nearby buildings to prevent collapse during blast or wind events.
- If building near the house, ensure a direct access path into the basement without exposing occupants to fallout contamination.
2. Design and Planning:
- Use a design similar to Figure 21 for initial construction (provides PF of 40).
- For higher protection factor (PF) of 1,000, cover the roof slab with three feet of dirt as shown in the upper right portion of Figure 21.
3. Excavation and Foundation:
- Dig a hole large enough to accommodate the shelter dimensions.
- Construct a reinforced concrete or masonry foundation for the shelter structure.
4. Waterproofing and Drainage:
- Apply waterproofing materials to all surfaces of the shelter to prevent water seepage.
- Install a floor drain system to allow water runoff, preventing standing water inside the shelter (as seen in some existing shelters).
5. Air Vents and Hatch Design:
- Install air vents for proper ventilation.
- Disguise air vents as part of permanent patio features such as light stanchions, tetherball poles, or beach umbrella poles to avoid drawing attention.
- Redesign the hatch to form a wooden top of a masonry bench or table, possibly integrating it into an outdoor area like a barbeque patio for better concealment.
6. Final Construction and Testing:
- Ensure all components are properly sealed and tested for water resistance and structural integrity.
- Verify that the shelter is fully concealed and blends with the surrounding environment to avoid detection.

Notes:
• This shelter provides a protection factor (PF) of 1,000 when fully buried under three feet of dirt, offering sufficient radiation shielding to survive cumulative exposures up to 150,000–200,000 R.
• Avoid using the design without proper drainage and waterproofing as it may lead to water accumulation inside the shelter.
permanent shelter,below-ground,fallout protection,blast radiation,construction,dirt roof,drainage,watertight,hatch design,air vents
Constructing a Permanent Above-Ground Fallout Shelter
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Purpose: Build a durable above-ground fallout shelter for protection against nuclear blast, tornadoes, and hurricanes.

Materials Required:
• Twenty-inch thick concrete blocks
• Eight-inch reinforced concrete slab
• Wooden shingles for the...
Purpose: Build a durable above-ground fallout shelter for protection against nuclear blast, tornadoes, and hurricanes.

Materials Required:
• Twenty-inch thick concrete blocks
• Eight-inch reinforced concrete slab
• Wooden shingles for the roof
• Optional: Windows (not recommended in high-wind or blast-prone areas)

Steps:
1. Design Layout: Plan the shelter's location in your backyard, ensuring it is away from potential hazards and accessible.
2. Foundation Construction: Lay a solid base using twenty-inch thick concrete blocks for the walls.
3. Roof Installation: Cap the walls with an eight-inch reinforced concrete slab. Cover this with a wooden shingled roof to give it a traditional appearance.
4. Ventilation Ducts: Install ventilation openings near the top of the walls for air circulation and potential modification into loopholes for defense purposes.
5. Window Consideration: In areas prone to high winds or nuclear blast, avoid including windows. If windows are added, they should be shielded with loose stacks of concrete blocks (note: these may not withstand extreme conditions like tornadoes or atomic blasts).
6. Door Installation: Ensure the door is reinforced and can provide adequate radiation shielding.

Additional Notes:
• This shelter design can also serve as a workshop, garden tool shed, potting shed, or changing area next to a swimming pool.
• The structure is designed to withstand blast effects up to five pounds per square inch overpressure at four miles from a 1-megaton nuclear burst.
• It should resist winds exceeding 150 miles per hour, although the decorative roof may be blown off in such conditions.
above-ground shelter, fallout shelter, tornado shelter, hurricane shelter, concrete block construction, radiation shielding, ventilation ducts

Group Shelter Design (1) -

Design a Long Hut for Group Use
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Title: Design a Long Hut for Group Use

Overview: A long hut can accommodate multiple people and is suitable for both mild and cold climates depending on its construction.

Dimensions:
• Length: 12' (3.6 m)
• Width: 10' (3...
Title: Design a Long Hut for Group Use

Overview: A long hut can accommodate multiple people and is suitable for both mild and cold climates depending on its construction.

Dimensions:
• Length: 12' (3.6 m)
• Width: 10' (3 m)

Capacity:
• Can house two to twelve men, depending on bunking arrangements.

Construction Time:
• Can be built in about 40 man hours.

Design Features:
• Open or enclosed based on climate needs.
• Suitable for summer or winter use with appropriate modifications (e.g., adding walls and a fireplace).

Steps to Build:
1. Plan the Structure: Determine whether the hut will be open or enclosed based on local weather conditions.
2. Gather Materials: Collect poles, battens, thatching materials, and lashings as needed.
3. Build the Framework: Construct a long rectangular frame using round poles for the main structure.
4. Add Thatching and Lashings: Secure battens to the framework and layer thatching material over them in overlapping rows.
5. Install Doors and Windows (if enclosed): Add doors and windows as needed, ensuring they are properly lashed and secured.
6. Complete Interior Features: Install bunks or sleeping areas if required for long-term use.

Notes: This design is flexible and can be adapted to different needs depending on the number of occupants and climate conditions.
long hut, group shelter, bushcraft design, survival construction

Cold Weather Shelter Design (1) -

Construct a Pyramidal Hut for Cold Weather Use
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Title: Construct a Pyramidal Hut for Cold Weather Use

Overview: A pyramidal hut is ideal for cold weather and can accommodate multiple people with proper bunking arrangements.

Dimensions:
• Inside: 14' (4.2 m) square
• Wall Height: 5'...
Title: Construct a Pyramidal Hut for Cold Weather Use

Overview: A pyramidal hut is ideal for cold weather and can accommodate multiple people with proper bunking arrangements.

Dimensions:
• Inside: 14' (4.2 m) square
• Wall Height: 5' (1.5 m)

Capacity:
• Can house eight to sixteen men, depending on bunking arrangements.

Construction Time:
• Can be built in about 20 man hours.

Design Features:
• Pyramidal shape provides excellent insulation and protection from wind and snow.
• Suitable for long-term use in cold climates with proper modifications (e.g., adding a fireplace).

Steps to Build:
1. Plan the Structure: Determine the size of the hut based on the number of people to be sheltered.
2. Gather Materials: Collect poles, battens, thatching materials, and lashings as needed.
3. Build the Framework: Construct a pyramidal frame using round poles for the main structure. Ensure all corners are properly lashed and secured.
4. Add Thatching and Lashings: Secure battens to the framework and layer thatching material over them in overlapping rows for weatherproofing.
5. Install Doors, Windows, and Fireplace (if needed): Add doors (with hinges), windows, and a fireplace for heating if required.
6. Complete Interior Features: Install bunks or sleeping areas as needed for long-term use.

Notes: This design is ideal for cold weather and can be constructed relatively quickly with proper organization of labor.
pyramidal hut, cold weather shelter, survival construction, bushcraft design

Team Organization (1) -

Organize Labor for Efficient Hut Construction
Reference Source: the ten bushcraft books
Title: Organize Labor for Efficient Hut Construction

Overview: Proper organization of labor is essential to ensure efficient and effective construction of huts or shelters.

Steps:
1. Assign Roles: Divide tasks among group members...
Title: Organize Labor for Efficient Hut Construction

Overview: Proper organization of labor is essential to ensure efficient and effective construction of huts or shelters.

Steps:
1. Assign Roles: Divide tasks among group members based on their skills (e.g., cutting poles, gathering thatching material, lashing framework).
2. Coordinate Work: Ensure all workers are aware of their assigned tasks and the overall plan for construction.
3. Maintain Order: Keep materials organized in piles near the work site to avoid delays or confusion during construction.
4. Monitor Progress: Regularly check on progress and adjust labor distribution as needed to ensure timely completion.
5. Encourage Teamwork: Foster a sense of cooperation among workers to maintain morale and efficiency throughout the project.

Notes: Efficient organization can significantly reduce construction time and improve the quality of the final structure.
labor organization, team building, survival construction, hut building

Pyramidal Huts (1) -

Constructing Pyramidal Huts
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
### Design and Purpose

• The pyramidal hut has a square base and is useful for maximizing wall and floor space.
• Construction is similar to circular huts, except for the use of intermediate poles.
• Erection time is significantly less compared...
### Design and Purpose

• The pyramidal hut has a square base and is useful for maximizing wall and floor space.
• Construction is similar to circular huts, except for the use of intermediate poles.
• Erection time is significantly less compared to other hut types.

### Assembly Steps

1. Use forked corner poles (F) and intermediate poles (I) as part of the framework.
2. Lash thatching battens to secure them in place.
3. When lashing on thatching battens:
- Make one lashing at each corner to secure two thatching battens if the span between fork holes is 6' [1.8 m] or less.
- If the span between corner poles is greater than 6' [1.8 m], lash battens to intermediate poles as well.

### Structural Elements
• Wall rail (W): Used for attaching wall thatch and providing support.
• Intermediate poles (I): Provide additional structural support in larger huts.
• Forked corner poles (F): Form the corners of the hut and are lashed to other components.

### Tips
• Use dry or dead timber for easier handling and to avoid damaging the bush.
• Ensure that all lashings are secure to maintain structural integrity.
pyramidal hut, square base, intermediate poles, wall rail, forked corner poles, lashing techniques

Tree Shelters (1) -

Constructing a Tree Swinging Shelter (Swinging Bunk)
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Materials Required:
• Strong forked frame stick
• Vine or cane loops/hooks
• Rope (vine or cane)
• Frame poles for thatch battens
• Cross sticks for rigidity
• Thatching material

Steps to Construct a Tree Swinging Shelter:
1. **Select and...
Materials Required:
• Strong forked frame stick
• Vine or cane loops/hooks
• Rope (vine or cane)
• Frame poles for thatch battens
• Cross sticks for rigidity
• Thatching material

Steps to Construct a Tree Swinging Shelter:
1. Select and prepare the forked frame stick:
- Choose a very strong forked frame stick, ensuring it is sturdy at both the fork on the tree and the main juncture.
2. Secure the forked frame to the tree:
- Use either a cane or vine loop or a hook at the top section of the forked frame stick to attach it securely to the tree.
3. Add additional suspension:
- Attach a vine or cane rope from the extreme end of the main frame to as high up in the tree as is practical for added suspension and stability.
4. Construct the framework:
- Lash frame poles for thatch battens separately with square lashing to the bottom of the forked frame stick.
5. Add cross sticks for rigidity:
- Add a short cross stick lashed horizontally to each of the opposite sides of the frame poles to provide additional support and stability.
6. Thatching process:
- Thatch one row on one side, then thatch the row on the opposite side to strengthen the framework and ensure proper balance.
7. Construct the bunk separately:
- The main frame of the bunk is made from four poles lashed together to form a rectangle about 3' by 7' [0.9 by 2.1 m].
8. Create the sleeping area:
- The space between the poles forming the bunk proper can be woven or constructed with crossed sticks, similar to a camp bed.

Notes:
• This shelter is ideal for swampy areas or regions with high snake infestation.
• It can be constructed by one person in a day and provides a safe and stable sleeping area.
tree swinging shelter, tree bunk, thatching, lashing, cross sticks, vine rope

Building Shelters (1) -

Building a Lean-To Shelter
High Priority Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Materials needed: Evergreen branches, bullrushes, reeds, sod, bark, rock slabs, snow blocks, canvas, or plastic sheeting.

Steps:
1. Choose a location that is protected from wind, unless in summer when windswept areas may be preferable to...
Materials needed: Evergreen branches, bullrushes, reeds, sod, bark, rock slabs, snow blocks, canvas, or plastic sheeting.

Steps:
1. Choose a location that is protected from wind, unless in summer when windswept areas may be preferable to avoid insects.
2. Construct the lean-to with at least a 45-degree slope on the roof to allow rain to shed properly.
3. Use roots, strips of bark, or animal hide as lashings to secure the structure.
4. Ensure the shelter is not larger than necessary to hold you and your gear; smaller shelters are easier to keep warm.
5. In cold weather, insulate the floor with evergreen boughs, dry grass, or rushes for added warmth.
6. Use loose snow as insulation for sides and roofs in cold conditions.
7. Avoid using natural shelters like caves if they are damp or occupied by bears.
8. Consider spruce trees with branches to the ground as snug winter shelters, but reinforce them with additional branches.
9. Do not build fires inside natural shelters made of snow or ice, as this may cause melting and collapse.

Notes:
• Snow houses require a snow saw-knife to construct, which is rarely available outside of downed aircraft.
• Southern exposures are warmer and should be preferred for shelter placement.
lean-to, shelter building, insulation, materials, cold weather, fire safety

Stove Repair (1) -

Mend Cracks in Stoves and Pipes with Wood Ashes and Salt Paste
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ready Mode of Mending Cracks in Stoves, Pipes, and Iron Ovens, as Practised in Germany:

1. When a crack is discovered in a stove, through which fire or smoke penetrates, immediately apply the following mixture:
2. Mix wood-ashes and common salt...
Ready Mode of Mending Cracks in Stoves, Pipes, and Iron Ovens, as Practised in Germany:

1. When a crack is discovered in a stove, through which fire or smoke penetrates, immediately apply the following mixture:
2. Mix wood-ashes and common salt into a paste with a little water.
3. Plaster the paste over the crack.
4. This method effectively closes cracks whether the stove, pipe, or oven is cold or hot.
mend,stove,pipes,cracks,wood-ashes,salt,paste

Debris Shelter (1) -

Constructing a Debris Shelter for Insulation and Rain Protection
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Materials Needed:
• Light, soft debris (leaves, grasses, brush, leaf litter)
• Bark slabs or moss

Steps to Construct the Shelter:
1. Build the Skeletal Structure: Start by constructing a basic framework for your shelter using available...
Materials Needed:
• Light, soft debris (leaves, grasses, brush, leaf litter)
• Bark slabs or moss

Steps to Construct the Shelter:
1. Build the Skeletal Structure: Start by constructing a basic framework for your shelter using available materials such as branches and poles.
2. Heap Debris Over the Framework: Cover the skeletal structure with a thick pile of light, soft debris. Use leaves, grasses, brush, or any type of leaf litter.
3. Form a Dome Shape: The debris should form a large dome-shaped mound approximately 60cm thick over the structure. A thicker pile provides better insulation and rain protection.
4. Add Waterproof Layer: On top of the debris, add bark slabs or moss to create a protective waterproof layer that also helps keep the structure together.

Notes:
• This method is particularly effective in cold or wet weather.
• Shelters built using this technique are sometimes called “oven shelters.”
debris shelter, insulation, rain protection, survival shelter, cold weather shelter

Manmade Shelters (1) -

Identify One Man-Made Expedient Shelter
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Objective: Without the aid of references and from a given list, choose one of man-made expedient shelters.

Several types of man-made shelters can be used in any situation. Examples include:

• Poncho Shelter: A simple shelter constructed...
Objective: Without the aid of references and from a given list, choose one of man-made expedient shelters.

Several types of man-made shelters can be used in any situation. Examples include:

• Poncho Shelter: A simple shelter constructed using ponchos or tarps.
• Sapling Shelter: Constructed by bending saplings and securing them with rope or cordage.
• Lean-to: A single-slope shelter made from logs, branches, or other available materials.
• Double Lean-to: Similar to a lean-to but with two sides for additional protection.
• A-frame Shelter: Constructed using two poles tied together at the top and supported by stakes at the base.
• Fallen Tree Bivouac: A shelter made from a fallen tree, often used in forested areas.

Note: For this objective, select one type of man-made expedient shelter from the list above.
man-made shelters, poncho shelter, sapling shelter, lean-to, a-frame shelter, fallen tree bivouac

Site Selection And Planning (1) -

Selecting a Shelter Site and Considerations
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Considerations for Shelter Construction:

Group Size: Take into account the number of people requiring shelter.

Thermal Efficiency:
• Use low silhouette and reduced living area dimensions to improve heat conservation.
• Avoid...
Considerations for Shelter Construction:

Group Size: Take into account the number of people requiring shelter.

Thermal Efficiency:
• Use low silhouette and reduced living area dimensions to improve heat conservation.
• Avoid exposed hill tops, valley floors, moist ground, and avalanche paths.
• Apply snow (if available) to the roof and sides of the shelter to create a thermal barrier.

Location Factors:
• Choose a site that is close to firewood, water, and signaling materials, if necessary.
• Consider how much time and effort are needed to build the shelter.
• Ensure the shelter can adequately protect from elements (sun, wind, rain, snow). Plan for worst-case scenarios.

Tools and Materials:
• Determine whether tools are available or if you must make improvised tools.
• Assess the type and amount of materials available to construct the shelter.

Tactical Considerations (if applicable):
• Provide concealment from enemy observation.
• Maintain camouflaged escape routes.
• Use the acronym BLISS as a guide:
- B – Blend in with the surroundings.
- L – Low silhouette.
- I – Irregular shape.
- S – Small.
- S – Secluded location.
shelter site selection, thermal efficiency, tactical considerations, BLISS acronym

Camping (1) -

Establishing a Campsite in Cold Weather Conditions
Reference Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Objective: To establish a campsite that is functional and safe in cold weather conditions.

Procedure:
1. Select an appropriate location: Choose a site that provides adequate shelter from wind, snow, and other environmental factors while...
Objective: To establish a campsite that is functional and safe in cold weather conditions.

Procedure:
1. Select an appropriate location: Choose a site that provides adequate shelter from wind, snow, and other environmental factors while ensuring it is accessible to all personnel.
2. Clear the area: Remove any debris or obstacles that may interfere with the establishment of the campsite or pose a safety hazard.
3. Set up tents and shelters: Assemble and position tents, sleeping bags, and other sheltering equipment in accordance with established procedures and guidelines.
4. Prepare cooking areas: Establish designated cooking areas that are protected from wind and moisture to ensure safe food preparation and consumption.
5. Construct latrines and garbage pits: Set up latrines and garbage pits in accordance with established procedures to maintain hygiene standards and prevent contamination.
6. Ensure security measures: Implement appropriate security measures to protect the campsite from potential threats or intrusions.
7. Monitor conditions and usage: Regularly check the campsite for any issues that may affect its functionality, such as damage to tents or shelters, or problems with latrines and garbage pits.
8. Maintain hygiene standards: Ensure that all personnel follow established hygiene protocols to maintain cleanliness and prevent the spread of disease.

Details:
• The campsite should be established in a location that is accessible to all personnel but not near water sources or other sensitive areas.
• Use of appropriate materials and techniques will help ensure the campsite remains functional even in extreme cold weather conditions.
• Regular maintenance and inspection are essential to prevent the spread of disease and maintain hygiene standards.
establishing campsite,cold weather camping,military operations,camp setup,shelter

Tent Assembly (1) -

Assemble the Tent Properly
Reference Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure for Assembling a Tent:

1. Lay out the outer tent flat, with the apex in the centre and panels outward, ensuring the inside is facing upwards and the door zipper is fastened.

2. Place the inner tent liner on top of the outer...
Procedure for Assembling a Tent:

1. Lay out the outer tent flat, with the apex in the centre and panels outward, ensuring the inside is facing upwards and the door zipper is fastened.

2. Place the inner tent liner on top of the outer tent, also with the inside facing upwards.

3. Attach the stove pipe toggles:
- Align the stove pipe openings of the outer and inner liner.
- Attach both the top and bottom toggles to position the inner and outer portions correctly.

4. Attach remaining toggles:
- Work either way around the tent, attaching all remaining toggles.
- Use the corners as reference points to ensure no toggle is missed.
- Continue until all toggles are through the seam grommets of the inner liner.

5. Thread the long or lower drying line:
- Pass the drying line through the drying line keepers.
- Feel through the liner at the peak or centre of the doorway, follow up the seam on the panel of the outer tent until reaching the drying line keeper.
- Insert it through the split seam of the liner and thread the drying line on.
- There are 10 keepers for the lower drying lines.

6. Thread the short or upper drying line:
- Start at the door seam again, repeating the same process as with the lower drying line.
- There will be a keeper on each side of this one and then one on every second seam.
- This results in six drying line keepers for the top.

7. Insert the tent pole spike:
- Pass the spike through the apex of both the inner and outer tents.
- Lash these three components (spike, inner tent, outer tent) securely together.

8. Attach bottom tie-down pegs:
- Run a 15-inch rope through the bottom wall eyelets of the outer and inner tents.
- Tie the pegs to the outside of the tent.

9. Attach wall guy lines (11):
- Thread each guy line through one hole of the runner, then through the guy line loop on the outer tent, and back through the other hole of the runner.
- Tie a figure 8 knot at this end to prevent slipping out of the runner hole.
- On the other end, thread it through the eye of the peg and secure with a slip knot to prevent being pulled out.
- This method is essential as ropes can freeze in peg holes, and reversing the procedure may cause guy lines to tighten improperly.
- In hard ground or soft snow, wrap several turns of the guy line around the centre of the peg. Freeze it in place or use a large stone or log on top of the peg for added security.

10. Attach five top guy lines (for 10-man tent):
- Follow the same method as described above for attaching wall guy lines.

11. Secure the zipper tracks:
- After assembling, close the zippers and sew the track of the zipper together near the top of the door to prevent disengagement.
- Do this for both the outer and inner tent zippers.

12. Handle the fly screen:
- The fly screen is not useful in cold weather and should be rolled up and secured using the ties running from the door to the outside corners.
- Ensure this is done only after the tent has been pitched, as doing it earlier may cause misshaping when pitching the tent.

13. Secure guy ropes:
- To prevent guy lines from hanging loose or tangling, roll the guy rope around the tent peg and in the guy rope loop.
- If the loops are too small for the pegs to fit securely, thread short pieces of rope through the guy line loops and tie them with a square knot.
- Adjust the knot so that the peg fits securely.
assembling tent, cold weather shelter, military tent setup, tent toggles, drying line installation, guy lines, zipper security, fly screen storage

Cold Weather Base Installation (1) -

Base Installation Requirements for Airhead or Forward Bases in Cold Weather
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Ensure air portable, dismantable shelters or complete units with aluminum insulated walls are available during fall and winter periods.
2. These units should be used for:
- Operations and communications centres
- Air...
Procedure:

1. Ensure air portable, dismantable shelters or complete units with aluminum insulated walls are available during fall and winter periods.
2. These units should be used for:
