• Clothes-line for drying

Steps to waterproof:

1. Mixing the solution: Stir ten...
Materials needed:
• Ten ounces of quick lime
• Four ounces of alum
• Ten quarts of water
• A pail for mixing solution
• Another pail to hold the tent
• Clothes-line for drying

Steps to waterproof:

1. Mixing the solution: Stir ten ounces of quick lime with four ounces of alum in ten quarts of water until the lime has slackened.

2. Applying the solution: Put the tent in another pail and pour the solution over it, letting it stand for twelve hours.

3. Drying the tent: Hang the tent on a clothes-line to dry after the solution has been applied.

Result: The tent will be entirely waterproof after this process.
waterproofing tents, camping gear maintenance, tent care

Tent Organization (1) -

Storing Gear in the Forester Tent
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Storage tips:

• Duffle storage: Place all duffle bags way back in the peak against the little back triangle to keep them dry and form a sort of back for your pillows.

• Insulation: Pile up a mountain of dry leaves over which you can...
Storage tips:

• Duffle storage: Place all duffle bags way back in the peak against the little back triangle to keep them dry and form a sort of back for your pillows.

• Insulation: Pile up a mountain of dry leaves over which you can stretch your blankets for additional insulation.

Benefits:
• Keeps gear dry and organized.
• Enhances comfort by providing a supportive pillow area.
gear storage, tent organization, camping tips, shelter efficiency

Arctic Shelter (1) -

Build Snow Huts for Arctic Expedition
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:
1. Construct narrow snow huts based on the length of available sticks.
2. Use brushwood to form the roof structure of the hut.
3. Cover the roof with snow to provide insulation and protection from the elements.
Notes:
• This...
Procedure:
1. Construct narrow snow huts based on the length of available sticks.
2. Use brushwood to form the roof structure of the hut.
3. Cover the roof with snow to provide insulation and protection from the elements.
Notes:
• This procedure is essential for Arctic expeditions where shelter must be constructed quickly in extreme conditions.
• Ensure that all scouts are familiar with the technique of building snow huts.
snow hut, arctic shelter, survival, cold weather, construction

Tent Building (1) -

Using the Scout Staff to Make Patrol Tents and Tepees
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Use the scout staff as a central support structure for constructing patrol tents or tepees.
2. Insert the staff into the ground at an angle to serve as a pole for the tent framework.
3. Secure additional poles or branches around...
Procedure:

1. Use the scout staff as a central support structure for constructing patrol tents or tepees.
2. Insert the staff into the ground at an angle to serve as a pole for the tent framework.
3. Secure additional poles or branches around the staff to create a stable and sturdy shelter.
4. Ensure that the tent is properly anchored to withstand wind and weather conditions.
scout staff, tent building, shelter construction, survival tool

Types Of Shelters (1) -

Identify and Locate Different Types of Shelters
High Priority Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
Procedure:

1. Understand the different types of shelters available in your area:
- General Population Shelters: For individuals without special needs.
- Functional and Medical Needs Shelters (formerly Special Needs): Provide medical...
Procedure:

1. Understand the different types of shelters available in your area:
- General Population Shelters: For individuals without special needs.
- Functional and Medical Needs Shelters (formerly Special Needs): Provide medical care for those with chronic conditions or disabilities.
- Pet/Animal Shelters: Accommodate pets and animals during emergencies.
- Temporary Warming/Cooling Shelters: Offer short-term relief from extreme weather but do not provide meals or sleep facilities.
- Shelter-in-Place: Designed for situations where evacuation is not possible, such as chemical spills or nuclear incidents.
2. Research shelter locations in advance and store this information securely.
3. In Michigan, mass shelters are typically required during floods, snowstorms, or extended power outages.
4. For localized incidents like fires, the American Red Cross may place displaced residents in hotels rather than opening small shelters.
shelter types, emergency shelter locations, Michigan shelters, temporary shelters, functional medical needs

General Population Shelters (1) -

Utilize General Population Shelters Effectively
High Priority Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
Procedure:

1. Identify general population shelters, which are typically operated by the American Red Cross and may be located in schools, community centers, or other public buildings.
2. Verify shelter availability through local radio...
Procedure:

1. Identify general population shelters, which are typically operated by the American Red Cross and may be located in schools, community centers, or other public buildings.
2. Verify shelter availability through local radio or TV stations during an emergency, as they will announce open shelters.
3. Note that general population shelters generally do not accept pets (except service animals) or provide medical care unless co-located with a Functional and Medical Needs Shelter.
4. Caregivers should accompany patients to ensure their needs are met in the shelter environment, as they may require assistance similar to what is provided at home.
5. Use the American Red Cross website to search for open shelters by zip code; information is updated every 30 minutes and should be kept accessible at all times.
general population shelter, caregiver accompaniment, American Red Cross, shelter location search

Fallout Shelters (1) -

Constructing a Permanent Interior Ground-level Shelter
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Permanent Interior Ground-level Shelters

To construct a built-in ground floor shelter during the construction of an average house without a basement, follow these steps:

1. Design Integration: Incorporate the shelter directly into the...
Permanent Interior Ground-level Shelters

To construct a built-in ground floor shelter during the construction of an average house without a basement, follow these steps:

1. Design Integration: Incorporate the shelter directly into the house design so that no one but you and the contractor is aware of its existence.
2. Mass Shielding: Build mass shielding into the walls and ceiling in such a way that nothing out of the ordinary is detectable.
3. Concrete Slab Construction: Use a one-story house built on a concrete slab for this design.
4. Reinforced Concrete Walls: Construct several interior walls surrounding the bath-utility area using foot-thick reinforced concrete. These walls can be finished in brick, paneling, plaster, or any other material of your choice.
5. Disguising Thickness: The extra thickness is noticeable only in the doorways and can be disguised even there to avoid the 'monolith' look.
6. Reinforced Concrete Slab: Cap the shelter area with a slab of reinforced concrete that is one foot thick, poured in place to interlock with the walls and provide additional structural strength.
7. Radiation Protection Factor (PF): This interior module provides a radiation PF of about 40, which would be sufficient to ensure survival for a family in an area receiving as much as 6,000 R during the first two weeks after an atomic attack.
8. Bathroom Shelter Design: Selecting a bathroom shelter design is recommended because it allows for access to running water and a toilet, which can add comfort during extended stays.
9. Space Consideration: Ensure that there is enough room in the selected area to allow everyone in the family to lie down to sleep.
permanent shelter, ground-level shelter, fallout shelter, radiation protection factor, reinforced concrete, bathroom shelter

Basement Shelters (1) -

Constructing a Permanent Interior Basement Shelter with Hollow Plywood Walls and Sand/Gravel Filling
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure for Constructing a Permanent Interior Basement Shelter:

1. Design Selection: Choose a shelter design that incorporates hollow plywood walls filled with dry sand or gravel, as opposed to traditional concrete block bunkers.
2....
Procedure for Constructing a Permanent Interior Basement Shelter:

1. Design Selection: Choose a shelter design that incorporates hollow plywood walls filled with dry sand or gravel, as opposed to traditional concrete block bunkers.
2. Cost Estimation: Estimate construction costs at approximately $500 for integration into a new house, depending on local labor and material costs, and the exact dimensions of the room being shielded.
3. Shielding Implementation:
- Construct hollow plywood walls and fill them with dry sand or gravel to provide mass shielding.
- Use normal floor joists to create bins for overhead shielding, also filled with sand or gravel.
4. Alternative Overhead Shielding: Consider replacing the overhead sand/gravel bins with galvanized water tanks if additional water storage is needed, though this will reduce radiation shielding effectiveness.
5. Safety Consideration: Opt for sand or gravel over bricks or concrete blocks in overhead bins to minimize risk of injury from falling debris during a shock wave or earthquake.
6. Shielding Thickness: Achieve adequate protection (PF) with walls as thin as 4 to 6 inches, relying on the basement's location for additional shielding.
7. Discreet Construction: Design and build the shelter in such a way that it remains undetectable from the outside, avoiding unnecessary attention during construction.
basement shelter, radiation shielding, sand filled walls, water tanks, emergency shelter design

Ventilation System Design (1) -

Ventilation Duct Design for Fallout Shelters
Standard Source: Life After Doomsday
Purpose: Ensure proper ventilation and potential defensive modifications in fallout shelters.

Materials Required:
• Concrete blocks (for duct construction)
• Reinforced concrete slab (for integration with shelter structure)

Steps:
1....
Purpose: Ensure proper ventilation and potential defensive modifications in fallout shelters.

Materials Required:
• Concrete blocks (for duct construction)
• Reinforced concrete slab (for integration with shelter structure)

Steps:
1. Plan Ventilation Placement: Install ventilation openings near the top of the walls during initial construction.
2. Construct Ducts: Use reinforced materials to create durable ventilation ducts that can be modified for defensive purposes such as loopholes.
3. Test Airflow: Ensure that the ventilation system allows for adequate air circulation while maintaining structural integrity.

Additional Notes:
• These ducts are designed to be easily modified into loopholes, which may be useful in a defense scenario.
• Proper ventilation is critical for long-term occupancy and safety within the shelter.
ventilation ducts, fallout shelter ventilation, loophole design, air circulation, structural integrity

Expedient Shelter (1) -

Create an Expedient Shelter Using a Manhole
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
### Create an Expedient Shelter Using a Manhole

Materials Needed:
• Two bolts
• A short length of steel cable or rope

Steps to Open a Manhole Cover:
1. Prepare the Key: Tie the steel cable between the centers of the two bolts, forming...
### Create an Expedient Shelter Using a Manhole

Materials Needed:
• Two bolts
• A short length of steel cable or rope

Steps to Open a Manhole Cover:
1. Prepare the Key: Tie the steel cable between the centers of the two bolts, forming an "H" shape.
2. Insert Bolt Through Hole: Insert one bolt through the hole in the edge of the manhole cover.
3. Position Bolt Under Cover: Jiggle the bolt around inside to get it crosswise under the hole.
4. Use the Other Bolt as a Handle: Use the other bolt as a handle and pull the cover off.

Considerations:
• Not all manholes lead to dry, clean areas; some may lead to sewers.
• It is advisable to explore multiple manholes in your area to find one that is suitable for use as a shelter.
• Manhole covers are made of thick steel and can be heavy. A motivated adult should be able to open them with the described method.
manhole, shelter, nuclear fallout, expedient shelter, survival, doomsday

Fallout Shelter (1) -

Build an Expedient Fallout Shelter in an Emergency
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Construct a temporary fallout shelter during an emergency.

Steps:
1. Identify a Suitable Location: Choose a location that is underground or inside a basement, ideally with thick walls and minimal exposure to the outside...
Objective: Construct a temporary fallout shelter during an emergency.

Steps:
1. Identify a Suitable Location: Choose a location that is underground or inside a basement, ideally with thick walls and minimal exposure to the outside environment.
2. Seal Entry Points: Use plastic sheeting, duct tape, and other available materials to seal all entry points and windows to prevent radioactive particles from entering.
3. Create a Filter System: Construct a simple air filtration system using layers of cloth, charcoal, and paper towels to filter out fallout particles.
4. Stock Supplies: Ensure the shelter is stocked with enough food, water, and medical supplies for at least 72 hours.
5. Plan for Emergencies: Have a plan in place for communication and evacuation if necessary.

Warnings/Contraindications: Do not rely solely on an expedient fallout shelter without additional protective measures. Always follow official guidelines from emergency management authorities.
expedient fallout shelter, nuclear war, emergency shelter, radiation protection

Group Management (1) -

Establishing Shelter Rules for Survival
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Create clear and enforceable rules to ensure the survival and well-being of the group within a shelter environment.

### Key Considerations:
• Entry and Exit Rules:
- Clearly define who is allowed to enter the shelter (e.g.,...
Objective: Create clear and enforceable rules to ensure the survival and well-being of the group within a shelter environment.

### Key Considerations:
• Entry and Exit Rules:
- Clearly define who is allowed to enter the shelter (e.g., family units, pets, or others) and what they can bring with them. This should be decided in advance of any crisis.
- Establish strict rules about when and under what circumstances individuals may leave the shelter. Emphasize that leaving is dangerous and only permitted for essential reasons.

• Daily Living Rules:
- Set clear limits on food and water consumption per person per day to ensure equitable distribution and prevent waste.
- Prohibit misuse of supplies, especially during times of stress (e.g., excessive snacking).
- Designate who will dispense medicine to avoid unnecessary use or hoarding.
- Define quiet hours for rest and sleep. Ensure that these hours are respected by all group members.

• Special Rules:
- Decide on policies regarding smoking, alcohol, drugs, sex, gambling, and fighting based on the group's circumstances and survival goals.
- Prohibit any activity that reduces the group’s chances of survival or extends dependency on outside aid.
- Avoid making rules solely based on moral judgments; focus instead on practicality and survival needs.

### Group Involvement:
• Allow some flexibility by letting the group decide certain aspects, such as the timing of quiet hours, to reduce resistance and make the process feel less arbitrary.
• Ensure that all rules are clearly communicated and consistently enforced for fairness and effectiveness.
shelter rules, group management, survival protocols, food rationing, quiet hours, entry exit policies

Civil Defence Shelter (1) -

Locate and Use Civil Defence Shelters
High Priority Source: Sweden In Case of Crisis or War
Procedure for locating and using civil defence shelters:

1. Find the nearest shelter by visiting msb.se, which provides a map of all shelters in Sweden.
2. Shelters should have water and basic toilet facilities, so bring food, warm...
Procedure for locating and using civil defence shelters:

1. Find the nearest shelter by visiting msb.se, which provides a map of all shelters in Sweden.
2. Shelters should have water and basic toilet facilities, so bring food, warm clothing, and toiletries if possible.
3. Be prepared to stay for several days as needed during an emergency.
4. During peace time, shelters may be used for other purposes but must be reset and ready within 48 hours of a crisis.
5. During heightened alert, property owners are required to ensure shelters are properly prepared.
6. Shelters are marked with a sign: A blue triangle inside an orange rectangle, indicating they are protected under the laws of war.
civil defence shelter,map location,emergency shelter,orange rectangle,blue triangle,warm clothing,toiletries

Rural Shelter (1) -

Selecting Appropriate Shelter for Rural Evacuation
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Choose durable and weather-resistant shelter solutions suitable for rural environments.

• Include an ALPS Mountaineering Meramac 2 Person Tent (7.5lb) if traveling with a partner, or opt for a 1-person tent if traveling alone.
-...
Objective: Choose durable and weather-resistant shelter solutions suitable for rural environments.

• Include an ALPS Mountaineering Meramac 2 Person Tent (7.5lb) if traveling with a partner, or opt for a 1-person tent if traveling alone.
• Add a Klymit Static V Insulated Sleeping Pad for cold climates to prevent heat loss through conduction.
• Include an Esbit Alcohol Stove & Trekking Cook Set, along with Esbit 14g fuel tablets and denatured alcohol, for cooking in adverse weather conditions.
• Use a Snugpak Jungle Bag as a lightweight sleeping bag option. Pair it with the emergency blanket, bivy, warm clothing, and Klymit pad for added warmth.

Notes:
• Consider splitting the load with a bug out partner to manage weight effectively.
• For larger groups, use a ALPS Mountaineering Zephyr 3 Person Tent (6.1lb), and paint it with Rust Oleum Camo Spray Paint for better camouflage.
rural shelter, insulated sleeping pad, alcohol stove, multi-person tent

Hammock Shelter (1) -

Setting Up a Hammock Shelter System for Solo Travelers
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Set up a hammock shelter system quickly and safely for solo travelers.

• Use the Hennessy Hammock Explorer Deluxe (zip version preferred) to avoid accidental slipping out.
• Include the following accessories:
- (2) Omega...
Objective: Set up a hammock shelter system quickly and safely for solo travelers.

• Use the Hennessy Hammock Explorer Deluxe (zip version preferred) to avoid accidental slipping out.
• Include the following accessories:
- (2) Omega Pacific Rap Rings
- (2) Omega Pacific Carabiners
- (1) Hennessy Snake Skin
- (1) Hennessy Double Wide Hex Fly
• Place a UST Tube Tarp underneath the hammock to keep gear off the ground and provide a stepping surface.

Notes:
• Ensure that all components are packed efficiently in your bug out bag.
• A tarp is essential for both protection from the elements and as a utility item.
hammock setup, solo shelter, tarp under hammock

Lightweight Tent (1) -

Selecting a Lightweight Tent for Solo Travelers
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Choose a lightweight and compact tent suitable for solo travelers.

• Use the Snugpak Ionosphere Tent (2.5lbs) for its lightweight design and extra protection from the elements.
• This tent is ideal for trapping body heat in a...
Objective: Choose a lightweight and compact tent suitable for solo travelers.

• Use the Snugpak Ionosphere Tent (2.5lbs) for its lightweight design and extra protection from the elements.
• This tent is ideal for trapping body heat in a smaller area, making it easier to stay warm in colder conditions.

Notes:
• Keep weight down by selecting this ultra-lightweight option.
• Ensure that the tent is packed efficiently in your bug out bag.
lightweight tent, solo traveler, heat retention

Group Shelter (1) -

Optimizing Shelter Load Distribution for Group Travel
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Distribute the weight of shelter items effectively among group members.

• For groups of 3+ people, use the ALPS Mountaineering Zephyr 3 Person Tent (6.1lb) and ensure that each member carries a portion of the load.
• Use...
Objective: Distribute the weight of shelter items effectively among group members.

• For groups of 3+ people, use the ALPS Mountaineering Zephyr 3 Person Tent (6.1lb) and ensure that each member carries a portion of the load.
• Use compression sacks to pack all gear efficiently, reducing bulk and weight in your bug out bag.

Notes:
• Consider painting the tent with Rust Oleum Camo Spray Paint for better camouflage during evacuation.
• Always keep extra supplies like fuel tablets and denatured alcohol on hand for cooking.
group shelter, load distribution, compression sack

Equipment Maintenance (1) -

Guy Line and Anchor Point Inspection Procedure
Standard Source: US Antarctic Continental Field Manual
Purpose: Ensure the structural integrity of camp equipment.

Procedure:
1. Check and tighten all guy lines and anchor points regularly.
2. This is especially important in high-wind conditions or after a storm event.
3. Document any...
Purpose: Ensure the structural integrity of camp equipment.

Procedure:
1. Check and tighten all guy lines and anchor points regularly.
2. This is especially important in high-wind conditions or after a storm event.
3. Document any issues found during inspections for future reference.
guy line inspection, anchor point maintenance

Expedient Shelter Characteristics (1) -

Identify Four Characteristics of a Safe Expedient Shelter
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Objective: Without the aid of references and from a given list, choose the four characteristics of a safe expedient shelter.

The six basic characteristics that make any type of shelter (permanent building, tentage, or an expedient shelter) safe...
Objective: Without the aid of references and from a given list, choose the four characteristics of a safe expedient shelter.

The six basic characteristics that make any type of shelter (permanent building, tentage, or an expedient shelter) safe and effective are:

1. Protection from the Elements: The shelter must provide protection from rain, snow, wind, sun, etc.
2. Heat Retention: It must have some type of insulation to retain heat; thus preventing the waste of fuel.
3. Ventilation: Ventilation must be constructed, especially if burning fuel for heat. This prevents the accumulation of carbon monoxide. Ventilation is also needed for carbon dioxide given off when breathing.
4. Drying Facility: A drying facility must be constructed to dry wet clothes.
5. Free from Natural Hazards: Shelters should not be built in areas of avalanche hazards, under rock fall or “standing dead” trees have the potential to fall on your shelter.
6. Stable: Shelters must be constructed to withstand the pressures exerted by severe weather.

Note: For this objective, select any four from the six listed above.
shelter characteristics, expedient shelter, protection from elements, heat retention, ventilation, drying facility, natural hazards, stability

Natural Shelter Hazards (1) -

Identify Four Hazards to Avoid When Using Natural Shelters
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Objective: Without the aid of references and from a given list, choose the four hazards to avoid when using natural shelters.

Natural shelters may present several hazards that must be avoided:

1. Animals: Natural shelters may already be...
Objective: Without the aid of references and from a given list, choose the four hazards to avoid when using natural shelters.

Natural shelters may present several hazards that must be avoided:

1. Animals: Natural shelters may already be inhabited (i.e., bears, coyotes, lions, rats, snakes, etc.). Other concerns from animals may be disease from scat or decaying carcasses.
2. Lack of Ventilation: Natural shelters may not have adequate ventilation. Fires may be built inside for heating or cooking but may be uncomfortable or even dangerous because of the smoke build up.
3. Gas Pockets: Many caves in a mountainous region may have natural gas pockets in them.
4. Instability: Natural shelters may appear stable, but in reality may be a trap waiting to collapse.

Note: For this objective, select any four from the listed hazards above.
natural shelters, shelter hazards, animal dangers, lack of ventilation, gas pockets, instability

Tree Pit Shelter (1) -

Constructing a Tree-Pit Shelter
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Materials Required:
• Large tree with thick lower branches
• Snow (to create the pit)
• Branches for lining walls and floor
• Canvas or other material for roof

Steps:
1. Select a large tree in a wooded area with thick lower branches.
2....
Materials Required:
• Large tree with thick lower branches
• Snow (to create the pit)
• Branches for lining walls and floor
• Canvas or other material for roof

Steps:
1. Select a large tree in a wooded area with thick lower branches.
2. Shake snow from the lower branches to create a natural pit around the trunk of the tree.
3. Enlarge the pit manually if necessary.
4. Line the walls and floor of the pit with branches for insulation and comfort.
5. Thicken the roof using additional branches or other available materials.
6. Cover the roof with canvas or any other material on hand to provide additional protection from the elements.
tree-pit shelter, cold weather shelter, survival shelter, military shelter

Modern Shelters (1) -

Construct a Tar Paper Shelter for Modern Campers
Reference Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
Materials Required:
• Poles or wooden frames
• Tar paper (as an alternative to bark)
• Stakes or fasteners

Procedure Steps:
1. **Build the Frame:
- Construct a skeleton of poles to form the basic structure of the shelter.
2. **Cover with...
Materials Required:
• Poles or wooden frames
• Tar paper (as an alternative to bark)
• Stakes or fasteners

Procedure Steps:
1. **Build the Frame:
- Construct a skeleton of poles to form the basic structure of the shelter.
2. **Cover with Tar Paper:
- Tack tar paper over the frame, ensuring complete coverage on all sides and the roof.
3. **Construct Additional Spaces:
- Create an open shed for the kitchen or other areas using additional poles and tar paper for the roof.
4. **Build Furniture:
- Construct a table by covering cross sticks with sheets of tar paper instead of birch bark, as shown in Fig. 48.

Notes:
• Tar paper is used when natural materials like birch bark are scarce or unavailable.
• This method is popular in modern lumber camps and among campers who travel by automobile due to the ease of transporting and applying tar paper.
• While effective, it lacks the natural aesthetic and scent associated with traditional bark-covered shelters.
tar paper shelter, modern camping, alternative materials, shelter construction
